<!-- page 312 -->

302
chapter 3
Furthermore, he often supplements al-ʿAyn’s text with lexical items ignored by 
its author.130
Another book with the title Muḫtaṣar al-ʿAyn is authored by Abū l-Ḥasan 
ʿAlī b. al-Qāsim al-Ḫawāfī, a scholar of the third/ninth century.131 The book fol-
lows al-ʿAyn not only in its phonetic ordering of letters but also in the internal 
arrangement of each chapter. Yet it differs significantly from al-ʿAyn in con-
tent. The author explains in the introduction132 that he is interested in what he 
calls al-ḍarb al-awsaṭ (lit. middle type), that is, lexical material which is neither 
strange and obscure (al-ġarīb al-ġāmiḍ) nor commonplace and easily under-
stood by the generality of people (al-qarīb al-sahl allaḏī tafhamuhu l-ʿāmma). 
Accordingly, he deletes from the origin a substantial number of roots none 
of whose derivatives he seems to have found consistent with his definition of 
al-ḍarb al-awsaṭ. More importantly, Ḫawāfī’s purpose is more than to simply 
abridge al-ʿAyn, and he makes it clear in the introduction that he wants his book 
to include the ġarīb material of the Quʾrān and Ḥadīṯ as well as nawādir (i.e. 
rare usage – hardly compatible with his al-ḍarb al-awsaṭ!) and proverbs. The 
book abounds with lexical items that are related to jurisprudence ( fiqh) and 
ġarīb al-Qurʾān/al-Ḥadīṯ. Unlike Zubaydī, Ḫawāfī includes in his text a large 
number of šawāhid which are not found in al-ʿAyn. These are not restricted 
to his main focus (i.e. Qurʾān and Ḥadīṯ) but also include a number of poetry 
šawāhid. In short, the work greatly resembles al-ʿAyn in form and arrangement, 
and its author’s abridgement of some material is compensated for by sizable 
additions, in line with his assertion in the introduction that he wants his book 
to be exhaustive of most of what a scholar might need (ḥattā yakūn kitāban 
ǧāmiʿan li-ǧull mā yaḥtāǧ ilayhi man yumāris al-ʿilm).133
We are also in possession of al-Ḫaṭīb al-Iskāfī’s (d. 420/1029) Muḫtaṣar K. 
al-ʿAyn. It begins with an introduction that summarizes some of the essential 
points in Ḫalīl’s introduction (e.g. biliterals, triliterals, etc., sound and unsound 
roots, and phonetic ordering of letters),134 but which does not indicate the pur-
pose or method of the abridged version. Like Ḫawāfī, Iskāfī follows al-ʿAyn’s 
phonetic arrangement as well as the internal division of its chapters. Other 
than the omission of morphological and syntactical comments, his abridge-
ment rests mainly on the reduction of the number of šawāhid, particularly 
130

detailed list in II, 570–575.

303
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
from poetry, and the abbreviation of the definitions of lexical items. The 
importance of the work, however, truly lies in its inclusion of material which 
does not occur in the known version of al-ʿAyn. Given that Iskāfī’s own addi-
tions (which he marked in the text) are very few, the editor convincingly argues 
that the considerable amount of material which al-Muḫtaṣar contains and 
which occur neither in the extant manuscripts of al-ʿAyn nor in its published 
text goes back to an early copy of al-ʿAyn.135 Whether or not that copy is the 
one which Ḫalīl wrote (or dictated to Layṯ), as the author suggests, is obviously 
impossible to substantiate.
2.2	
al-Bāriʿ fī l-luġa by Abū ʿAlī al-Qālī (d. 356/967)
Authored almost two centuries after al-ʿAyn, the first muǧannas lexicon in 
Arab Spain, al-Bāriʿ, was heavily influenced by Ḫalīl’s system.136 According 
to Zubaydī (d. 379/989), al-ʿAyn was introduced to Andalus by Qāsim b. Ṯābit 
al-Saraqusṭī (d. 302/915), author of al-Dalāʾil fī ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ,137 and his father 
Ṯābit b. ʿAbdalʿazīz (also known as Ṯābit b. Ḥazm; d. 313/925), who outlived his 
son.138 By the reign in Andalus of al-Mustanṣir al-Umawī (350/961 – 366/976), 
there were many manuscripts of al-ʿAyn, and it is reported that al-Mustanṣir 
asked a number of scholars, including Qālī, to compare between those manu-
scripts, some of which were full of mistakes.139 But even before al-Mustanṣir’s 
request, Qālī had already spent several years in authoring al-Bāriʿ. Qifṭī 
(d. 646/1248) mentions a statement written by Qālī’s son in which he specified 
the month of Raǧab of the year 339 A.H. as the date when his father had started 
to write his lexicon.140 Following an unspecified period of difficulties which 
distracted Qālī from continuing the work he had started, he resumed writing 
al-Bāriʿ in 349 A.H., and when he was through with the draft in the month of 
Šawwāl in 355 A.H., he started copying it, but died in the following year. The 
rest of the draft according to Ibn al-Abbār (d. 658/1260) was then copied by two 
scribes, Muḥammad b. al-Ḥusayn al-Fihrī (d. ?) and Muḥammad b. Maʿmar 
al-Ǧayyānī (d. 377/987).141 The two scribes presented the finished manuscript 
to al-Mustanṣir who wanted to find out by how many words it exceeded the

304
chapter 3
manuscript of al-ʿAyn which Qālī and his colleagues had decided was the best 
one available. Given Qālī’s profound knowledge of al-ʿAyn and its various man-
uscripts, al-Mustanṣir’s query implies that his lexicon was based on al-ʿAyn and 
that it would be easy to compare the two works to determine the number of 
new words introduced by Qālī. In Ibn al-Abbār’s riwāya, the exact answer given 
to al-Mustanṣir was 5,683 words. Further evidence related to the comparison 
between the two lexica comes from Ibn Ḫayr (d. 575/1179), who tells us that 
Qālī’s lexicon was larger than al-ʿAyn by more than 400 pages and that Qālī’s 
additions were of two types: supplying the words (or roots) which Ḫalīl had left 
out, and broadly expanding the material which, in al-ʿAyn, was either too brief 
or not supported by šawāhid.142
Qālī’s knowledge of al-ʿAyn certainly goes back to the period which he spent 
in Baghdad between 305/918 and 328/940 before he left to Andalus.143 His fore-
most teacher during that period was none other than Ibn Durayd (d. 321/933), 
author of the famous Ǧamharat al-luġa. Ibn Ḫayr mentions tens of books 
which Qālī studied with his teacher, including al-Ǧamhara,144 itself influenced 
by al-ʿAyn. But despite the similarity between al-ʿAyn and al-Ǧamhara, there 
were enough differences between the two to offer Qālī two distinct models 
of arrangement, at least in two major aspects: Ḫalīl’s phonetic arrangement 
and his division of each chapter (i.e. letter) into biliterals, triliterals, etc., and 
Ibn Durayd’s alphabetical arrangement and his division of the whole lexicon 
and not individual letters according to root length. In both aspects, Qālī fol-
lowed Ḫalīl, and although the choice of adopting either Ḫalīl’s or Ibn Durayd’s 
method of structuring individual chapters or the whole book based on root 
length would have had little effect on the user of al-Bāriʿ, his choice of adher-
ing to Ḫalīl’s phonetic arrangement is rather surprising, given his teacher’s 
conviction145 – as well as the conviction of other contemporary scholars, such 
as Ibn Wallād (d. 332/944)146 – that the alphabetical system is easier and more 
accessible to users. Yet it seems that the authoritative status of al-ʿAyn made it 
a more appealing model to emulate than the relatively recent al-Ǧamhara. In 
this respect, it should be noted that Qālī was not deterred by the doubts raised 
concerning Ḫalīl’s authorship of al-ʿAyn – doubts which Qālī himself, accord-
ing to his student Zubaydī, attributed to Abū Ḥātim al-Siǧistānī (d. 255/869)

305
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
and his contemporaries when al-ʿAyn was imported from Ḫurāsān.147 Ḫalīl is 
regularly mentioned by name in al-Bāriʿ, unlike Qālī’s other book, al-Maqṣūr 
wa-l-mamdūd,148 where the expression ṣāḥib al-ʿAyn (al-ʿAyn’s author) is reg-
ularly mentioned.149 It is probable that Qālī, having drawn so heavily from 
al-ʿAyn’s material in al-Bāriʿ, found it inappropriate to leave any doubt sur-
rounding the identity of its author. In contrast, the use of the expression ṣāḥib 
al-ʿAyn – if it is indeed meant to indicate uncertainty about who its real author 
was – would not seem out of place in al-Maqṣūr wa-l-mamdūd since the book 
deals merely with a restricted subject and is not a comprehensive lexicon of 
Arabic usage. Qālī’s position on the identity of al-ʿAyn’s author, however, has 
little bearing on the fact that al-Bāriʿ emulates al-ʿAyn in its essential features 
although it has some characteristics of its own, as we shall demonstrate.
Qālī’s phonetic arrangement of letters differs from Ḫalīl’s in some respects. 
The problem with determining with precision the order of letters in al-Bāriʿ is 
that its extant parts represent a fraction of the original, more specifically, only 
parts of the letters h, ḫ, ġ, q, ǧ, ṭ, d and t (listed in the order of their occurrence 
in the published text). The introduction, in which Qālī might have specified 
the order of letters, as he did in al-Maqṣūr wa-l-mamdūd, is also missing. In 
fact, compared with the 4,446 pages of the original manuscript,150 the extant 
parts are about 140 pages only.151 According to C. S. Fulton, who reproduced in 
facsimile the British Museum manuscript of al-Bāriʿ (London, 1933), the pho-
netic order of the lexicon is as follows:
h, ḥ, ʿ, ḫ, ġ, q, k, ḍ, ǧ, š, l, r, n, ṭ, d, t, ṣ, z, s, ẓ, ḏ, ṯ, f, b, m, w, alif, y
Yet it would be strange indeed for Qālī to choose in al-Bāriʿ an order which 
differs from the one he adopted in al-Maqṣūr wa-l-mamdūd and which he 
described in its introduction as proceeding from the farthest point of articula-
tion to the nearest. This order is as follows:
ʾ, h, ʿ, ḥ, ġ, ḫ, q, k, ḍ, ǧ, š, y, l, r, n, ṭ, d, t, ṣ, z, s, ẓ, ḏ, ṯ, f, b, m, w152

148	
Cf. above, 245.

150

ber of pages is said to be 5,000, and Ibn al-Ḥanbalī, Šaḏarāt, where the number is 3,000.
151

<!-- page 316 -->

306
chapter 3
The most noteworthy difference between Fulton’s order and the one described 
in al-Maqṣūr wa-l-mamdūd is that the former does not include hamza and 
places alif towards the end, whereas Qālī places the hamza first and explains 
that he dropped the alif because no word can begin with it. Furthermore, in 
the previously mentioned statement by Qālī’s son concerning his father’s lexi-
con, it is clearly indicated that the first three chapters which his father had 
copied before he died were kitāb al-hamz, kitāb al-hāʾ and kitāb al-ʿayn.153 This 
agrees with the order of the first three letters as specified by Qālī in al-Maqṣūr 
wa-l-mamdūd and strongly suggests that he adopted the same arrangement in 
both books. As noted earlier, Qālī’s arrangement is closer to Sībawayhi’s than 
to Ḫalīl’s154 and demonstrates that he did not slavishly follow al-ʿAyn in all 
aspects.
The arrangement of the material within each chapter also shows certain 
modifications introduced by Qālī to Ḫalīl’s system. The chapters are succes-
sively divided into biliterals, sound triliterals, unsound triliterals, ḥawāšī, 
awšāb, quadriliterals and quinqueliterals. In the section of biliterals, Qālī only 
includes roots whose second radical is geminated, that is, roots that are bilit-
eral in their written form but triliteral in reality (al-ṯunāʾī fī l-ḫaṭṭ wa-l-ṯulāṯī 
fī l-ḥaqīqa li-tašaddud aḥad ḥarfayhi);155 e.g. ǦLL and LǦǦ, ǦDD and DǦǦ, 
ǦZZ and ZǦǦ,156 etc. Other biliterals which are not geminated but contain 
an unsound letter, such as the interjections īh and wāh,157 are mentioned 
with what he calls ḥawāšī (lit. marginal forms). Ḥawāšī also include triliter-
als that contain two unsound letters and thus correspond to Ḫalīl’s lafīf; e.g. 
hayy bin bayy (a person who is unknown and whose father is also unknown).158 
Onomatopoeic biliterals and reduplicated biliterals – such as ṭaq (sound of 
stones), qaq-qaq (ha-ha), and tiġin tiġin (ha-ha)159 – are listed under awšāb (lit. 
rabble, social misfits),160 which he defines as a collection of words which are 
153	
Qifṭī, Inbāh I, 244.
154	
Cf. above, 246.
155	
Qālī, Bāriʿ 568, 579–580, 584, 586, etc. Note that the extant parts of the lexicon do not 
contain biliterals that are not geminated (except those that contain an unsound letter). 
Such biliterals (e.g. the particles qad and min) are placed in al-ʿAyn in the same section as 
other biliterals (cf. Ḫalīl, ʿAyn V, 16; VIII, 375). Qālī, on the other hand, either ignores them 
altogether or, more likely, places them in sections of which we have no extant examples.
156	
See these and other roots in Qālī, Bāriʿ 564–602.

perhaps a result of equating the perfect language with the perfect society.

<!-- page 317 -->

307
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
either onomatopoeic (ḥikāyāt/aṣwāt), or cries by which one chides or urges 
an animal (zaǧr), or are defective in form (manqūṣāt).161 Under awšāb are also 
listed onomatopoeic words which are either triliteral, such as waġā (noise of 
war), or quadriliteral, such as ġaṭġaṭa (cry of a sand grouse or sound of a boil-
ing cooking-pot).162 Unfortunately, the extant parts of the lexicon contain but 
few examples of awšāb, and we cannot form a full picture of this type, which 
is unique to al-Bāriʿ among muǧannas lexica. Yet we know from the awšāb 
sections of the letters ġ and q163 that, in spite of the elaborate division of their 
material into biliterals, triliterals and quadriliterals, they contain several incon-
sistencies in arrangement. For example, the root WĠY is listed with unsound 
triliterals (where awāġī “place through which water flows” is mentioned), as 
well as with awšāb (where waġā is cited because of its onomatopoeic nature).164 
Furthermore, the quadriliteral qaṭqaṭa is listed under awšāb when it refers to 
the cry of a sand grouse, but is mentioned as a non-onomatopoeic word with 
the meaning of “weak rain” in the section of quadriliterals.165 It is also note-
worthy that the sections on ḥawāšī and awšāb, unlike other sections of the 
lexicon, violate the principle of taqālīb, which is strictly adhered to in al-ʿAyn 
and the other lexica that follow the phonetic-permutative system.166 As for 
quinqueliterals, they do not occur in the extant parts of the lexicon, but since, 
in these parts, none is listed with quadriliterals, they were most probably listed 
in independent sections.
The changes introduced by Qālī to the system of al-ʿAyn in the order of 
letters and division of sections are minor in comparison with the additions he 
introduced to its material. This is largely due to Qālī’s method of collecting data 
from a large number of sources and ascribing lexical items as well as specific 
meanings of certain words to the philologists who reported them. In describ-
ing al-Bāriʿ, Zubaydī highlights the fact that Qālī assembled in it philological 
works (kutub al-luġa) and ascribed each word to the philologist who reported 
161	
Qālī, Bāriʿ 446.

Ibid., 447–448.
163	
Ibid., 447–448, 523–527.
164	
Ibid., 446, 447.
165	
Ibid., 525, 560. Quadriliterals other than onomatopoeic ones occur at times in two places 
as well; e.g. qarqama/qurqum (552, 557), qafandar (554, 556), and funduq/fundāq (557, 
560).
166	
Qālī seems to ignore taqālīb intentionally in the sections on ḥawāšī and awšāb perhaps 
because the occurrence of taqālīb in these types of words is rare. Even when two words 
such as ṭaqṭaqa and qaṭqaṭa offer the chance of being considered as maqlūb, he lists them 
under separate divisions of awšāb (Bāriʿ 523, 525).

<!-- page 318 -->

308
chapter 3
it.167 It should be borne in mind that, by Qālī’s time, philologists had but lim-
ited access to trustworthy Aʿrāb from whom to derive their data.168 Given that 
the process of data collection known as ǧamʿ al-luġa had already been accom-
plished, Qālī and most other lexicographers made up for lack of direct hear-
ing (samāʿ) from the Aʿrāb by citing earlier philologists who collected their 
data from the trustworthy Bedouins of their time. In al-Bāriʿ, Ḫalīl is cited in 
almost every page, often more than once, followed (in order of frequency) by 
Abū Zayd al-Anṣārī (d. 215/830), Ibn al-Sikkīt (d. 244/858), Aṣmaʿī (d. 216/831), 
Abū Ḥātim al-Siǧistānī (d. 255/869), Abū ʿUbayda (d. 209/824), Ibn Durayd 
(d. 321/933), Abū ʿAmr al-Šaybānī (d. 206/821), Abū ʿUbayd (d. 224/838), Farrāʾ 
(d. 207/822), Kisāʾī (d. 189/805), and Ibn Kaysān (d. 320/932).169 These scholars 
include Basrans as well as Kufans – a reminder that the lexicographical tradi-
tion is largely free from the partisan divide which by the fourth/tenth century 
was evident in the grammatical tradition. Also noteworthy are the relatively 
few times in which Qālī’s principal teacher, Ibn Durayd, is mentioned.170 This 
stands in sharp contrast with Qālī’s K. al-Amālī, a miscellany of literary mate-
rial and anecdotes of which Ibn Durayd is one of the main sources of riwāya. It 
may well be that in spite of Qālī’s extensive citing of his teacher in poetry and 
anecdotal material in al-Amālī, he opted to cite him sparingly in lexicographi-
cal material in al-Bāriʿ due to the doubts expressed by some contemporary 
scholars as to the authenticity of some of al-Ǧamhara’s lexical items and the 
accusation that it is a plagiarized version of al-ʿAyn.171
To demonstrate Qālī’s method of deriving data from various sources, we can 
examine the lemma WǦH. Ḫalīl’s text is cited at the end of the lemma, as is 
often the case, and is in seven lines in each of al-ʿAyn and al-Bāriʿ.172 The rest 
of the material is in about forty lines and is mainly derived from Abū ʿUbayda 
(mentioned three times) and Abū Zayd (mentioned twice), in addition to one 
unspecified source (wa-qāla ġayruhu). Other than the lexical items which 
occur in the cited text of al-ʿAyn, the lemma contains material ignored by 
Ḫalīl. This material consists of lexical items (e.g. tawǧīh “proximity of a horse’s 
hooves”), new meanings of words cited in al-ʿAyn (e.g. tawaǧǧaha, in the sense 
of “to face the wind as one voids his excrement”), idiomatic expressions (e.g.

<!-- page 319 -->

309
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
ʿabd al-waǧh, ḥurr al-waǧh and sahl al-waǧh), and, strangely enough, a techni-
cal term related to metrics (tawǧīh). The lemma also contains seven poetry 
šawāhid as well as dialectal material attributed by Abū Zayd to the Kilābiyyūn, 
a Numayrī, and an Aʿrābī. Based on such differences in many other lemmata, 
Ṭaʿʿān’s claim that al-Bāriʿ is identical with al-ʿAyn and that it is thus the old-
est copy of al-ʿAyn in our possession173 is certainly erroneous. Although Qālī 
emulates Ḫalīl’s system (with some modifications in the order of letters and 
division of chapters), the content of his lexicon, which incorporates al-ʿAyn, 
is also derived from a large number of sources, including mubawwab lexica, 
some of which are mentioned in the text (e.g. Abū Zayd’s al-Luġāt, Aṣmaʿī’s 
al-Ḫayl, and Ibn al-Sikkīt’s al-Alfāẓ).174 By diversifying his sources, Qālī was 
able to check the correctness of some of the lexical items which he cited. It is 
quite significant in this respect that, on one occasion, he expresses doubt as to 
the correctness of a lexical item (namely, aḍmaġa “to slaver excessively”) 
because he found it in no other source than al-ʿAyn.175 Qālī’s efforts at scruti-
nizing the data and diversifying his sources made al-Bāriʿ, according to Suyūṭī, 
one of the most trustworthy lexica (min aṣaḥḥ mā ullifa fī l-luġa ʿalā ḥurūf 
al-muʿǧam).176 Yet Suyūṭī further notes that, in spite of this, it did not enjoy 
much popularity (wa-lam yuʿarriǧū . . . ʿalā Bāriʿ Abī ʿAlī).177
The lemma WǦH also demonstrates two other features of al-Bāriʿ, the first 
of which is its author’s interest in dialectal usage.178 Qālī derives his dialectal 
material either from al-ʿAyn or, as in the case of WǦH, from his other sources. 
He also shows special interest in Arabized words, and, according to Zubaydī in 
al-Mustadrak (for which see below), words of Persian origin, including those 
which Qālī mentioned in his lemmata, form a separate chapter at the end of 
al-Bāriʿ.179 In this, Qālī apparently followed in the footsteps of his teacher, Ibn 
Durayd, who appended to his al-Ǧamhara several chapters on a miscellany of 
topics, including Arabized words.180 What we are unable to determine, how-
ever, is whether al-Bāriʿ contained other chapters which were annexed to its 
main body. The second feature – also present in subsequent lexica, such as

310
chapter 3
Ǧawharī’s (d. c. 400/1010) al-Ṣaḥāḥ and Fīrūzābādī’s (d. 817/1415) al-Qāmūs 
al-muḥīṭ – is the specification of the relevant vowels of certain lexical items or 
of their patterns in order to ensure that they be read correctly. In our illustra-
tive lemma WǦH, for example, the expressions bi-ḍamm al-tāʾ, bi-kasr al-tāʾ 
and al-wāw maksūra specify the correct vowels of tuǧāhaka/tiǧāhaka and 
wiǧha respectively, as do the patterns faʿl, fiʿla and fiʿal with respect to waǧh, 
wiǧha and ǧiha.181 By comparing Qālī’s quotations from al-ʿAyn with the text of 
the latter, it becomes obvious that the distinguishing vowels and patterns are 
his own addition to the original text.182
Unlike al-ʿAyn, there was little interest in authoring commentaries on 
al-Bāriʿ. We know of only one such commentary, but it is not exclusive to 
al-Bāriʿ since it also deals with al-ʿAyn. This is Zubaydī’s al-Mustadrak min 
al-ziyāda fī K. al-Bāriʿ ʿalā K. al-ʿAyn,183 in which he collected the material that 
Qālī had added to al-ʿAyn. Zubaydī followed the arrangement of al-ʿAyn and 
pointed out alleged errors by Ḫalīl and Qālī, such as ignoring certain lexical 
items, erroneously arranging material or repeating words, and alteration of 
diacritical points (i.e. taṣḥīf ). As for the book titled Ǧawāmiʿ K. al-Bāriʿ, we 
know that its author was Qālī’s student, Muḥammad b. al-Ḥusayn al-Fihrī 
(d. ?),184 but have no clue as to its content.
As fragmentary as the picture one gets from the extant parts of al-Bāriʿ 
may be, it is certainly one of the major lexica and represents a relatively early 
attempt at introducing improvements to previous works. From the perspective 
of exhaustiveness, it was the first lexicon which aimed at bringing together 
material scattered in several sources. The main muǧannas lexicon among 
these sources (if not the only one if the relatively scarce material ascribed to 
Ibn Durayd was orally transmitted to Qālī by his teacher and not taken from 
al-Ǧamhara) is al-ʿAyn, whereas the other sources are mubawwab lexica which 
deal either with one specific topic (e.g. Aṣmaʿī’s al-Ḫayl) or with a variety of 
topics (e.g. Ibn al-Sikkīt’s al-Alfāẓ). In addition there exists riwāya material that 
reached Qālī orally through earlier philologists, including his main teacher, Ibn 
181	
Note that, according to the traditional morphological measure of forms (mīzān ṣarfī ), the 
pattern of ǧiha is ʿilah, and not fiʿal, since the initial w of the root WǦH (which represents 
fāʾ al-fiʿl) is elided.

For example, al-hayḍ bi-fatḥ al-hāʾ wa-sukūn al-yāʾ and al-wahaǧ bi-fatḥ al-wāw wa-l-hāʾ 
(Qālī, Bāriʿ 89, 96) are both derived from al-ʿAyn (IV, 69, 66), from which the specification 
of vowels is missing.

the manuscript, and the description given above is Wadġīrī’s.

311
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
Durayd. Qālī’s attempt at exhaustiveness was paralleled later in the tradition, 
in particular by Ibn Manẓūr (d. 711/1311), who assembled in Lisān al-ʿArab vast 
material taken directly from five earlier extensive lexica.
2.3	
Tahḏīb al-luġa by Azharī (d. 370/981)
Tahḏīb al-luġa resembles al-Bāriʿ in being modelled on al-ʿAyn, although 
Azharī follows the latter’s arrangement more closely than Qālī. The two lexica 
are also alike in greatly expanding the content of al-ʿAyn by quoting various 
other sources in almost every lemma. Fortunately, our knowledge of al-Tahḏīb 
is not fragmentary as in the case of al-Bāriʿ. Its published version is complete in 
fifteen large volumes,185 and its lengthy introduction is intact. We are not sure 
which of the two lexica is larger, but by comparing the extant parts of al-Bāriʿ 
with their counterparts in al-Tahḏīb, it seems that they are roughly comparable 
in size. Accordingly, Carter’s view that al-Tahḏīb “represents the first mature 
and fully developed dictionary”186 does not take into account the fact that the 
equally mature al-Bāriʿ, which greatly resembles al-Tahḏīb in its expansive 
approach, was authored simultaneously with it or, more probably, earlier.187
The title of al-Tahḏīb (lit. correction) is strongly suggestive of its author’s 
critical approach to earlier works. Other than religious duty, Azharī mentions 
two reasons for authoring his book, namely, to include in it material that he 
had directly heard from the Arabs among whom he lived in captivity for some 
time (see below), and to amend the mistakes committed by his contempo-
raries (fī ʿaṣrinā hāḏā).188 Concerning the latter reason, he makes a link, at the 
end of the introduction,189 between the title of the lexicon and his intention to 
discard the lexical items which the foolish (aġbiyāʾ) have erroneously changed 
either through alteration of diacritical points (i.e. taṣḥīf) or other types of mis-
takes – an allusion to what he describes earlier in the introduction as wrongly 
interpreted material (al-tafsīr al-muzāl ʿan waǧhihi).190 He not only makes 
185	
Note that parts of the seventh, eighth and ninth volumes were inadvertently dropped 
from the published text, but were independently published by ʿUbaydī (1975).
186	
Carter (1990: 111).
187	
Azharī must have authored al-Tahḏīb in the latter part of his life, since he mentions in 
the introduction that he had reached the age of seventy (I, 7). Born in 282/895, Azharī 
either started authoring his book in 352 A.H. or finished it in that year if he wrote the 
introduction last, whereas Qālī started his work on al-Bāriʿ in 339 A.H. and finished it in 
355; cf. above, 303. Neither Qālī in Cordova nor Azharī in Herat was likely to have seen the 
finished product of the other.
188	
Azharī, Tahḏīb I, 6–7.
189	
Ibid., I, 54.
190	
Ibid., I, 7.

312
chapter 3
general reference to his predecessors or contemporaries but provides a critical 
synopsis of philological study up to his time, and the list of works which he 
either specifies in the introduction or quotes in the lemmata amounts to more 
than 120 titles.191 He generally praises the earliest authors who collected their 
data directly from the Bedouin fuṣaḥāʾ. These include Yazīdī (d. 202/818), Abū 
Zayd al-Anṣārī (d. 215/830), Aṣmaʿī (d. 216/831), Abū ʿUbayd (d. 224/838), Ibn 
al-Sikkīt (d. 244/858), and Abū Ḥātim al-Siǧistānī (d. 255/869). On the other 
hand, he criticizes some of his predecessors, including Quṭrub (d. 206/821), 
Ǧāḥiẓ (d. 255/869), and Ibn Qutayba (d. 276/889), all of whom he accuses of 
being unreliable.192 But his harshest criticism is directed against contemporary 
authors of lexica. We have already alluded to his attack on al-Buštī al-Ḫārzanǧī 
(d. 348/959), author of Takmilat K. al-ʿAyn, and Abū l-Azhar al-Buḫārī (fl. In 
the second half of the third/ninth century), author of K. al-Ḥaṣāʾil.193 Some of 
his harshest words are also aimed at Ibn Durayd (d. 321/933), whom he describes 
as a drunkard and accuses of inventing words and committing grave mistakes 
in Ǧamharat al-luġa.194 In his attack on Ibn Durayd,195 Azharī is obviously 
influenced by his teacher Nifṭawayhi (d. 323/935), who was a bitter adversary of 
Ibn Durayd’s. His criticism of al-ʿAyn (for which see below) and his attack on 
Ibn Durayd are clearly intended at discrediting earlier muǧannas lexica in 
order to highlight his own contribution to lexicography and justify the title 
of his lexicon. In the book’s epilogue, Azharī renews his severe attack on ear-
lier lexicographers by accusing them of committing a serious crime (ǧināya) 
against the Arabic language, and unfavorably contrasting their work with his 
own book, which he claims to be free from taṣḥīf and misinterpretation.196
As for the other reason cited by Azharī for authoring his lexicon, i.e. to 
include in it material properly attested through samāʿ, it should be stressed 
that he is one of the last lexicographers to have transmitted data he had heard 
directly from the Aʿrāb197 – data which he seems to have mostly gathered dur-
ing the period in which he was held captive by the Qarāmiṭa. His captivity, 
which began in 312/924, lasted, in his words, for sunayyāt (a short number of

<!-- page 323 -->

313
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
years) or dahran ṭawīlan (a long period of time).198 His captors were mainly 
from the tribe of Hawāzin, in addition to a few Tamīmīs and Asadīs. Azharī 
describes the speech of these Aʿrāb as almost free of error (wa-lā yakād yaqaʿ 
fī manṭiqihim laḥn aw ḫaṭa⁠ʾ fāḥiš),199 and asserts that he collected from their 
speech a large number of words and anecdotes, most of which are included in 
his lexicon. Indeed, there are countless references in al-Tahḏīb to the dialects 
of specific tribes,200 as well as to Azharī’s own experience with Bedouin culture 
and habits.201 Besides his own samāʿ, Azharī explains that he only admits to his 
lexicon material which he has heard from a reliable source (ṯiqa).202 Certainly 
a vast body of linguistic data was reported to him by his teachers, foremost 
among whom was Muḥammad b. Ǧaʿfar al-Munḏirī (d. 329/940)203 in his 
native Herat, and several other scholars he had met in Baghdad, most notably, 
Nifṭawayhi (d. 323/935) and Abū Bakr b. al-Anbārī (d. 328/940).
Azharī’s focus on samāʿ as the most reliable source of linguistic data is 
linked to his keenness on including only correct usage (mā ṣaḥḥa lī samāʿan) 
in his lexicon.204 In this respect, he predates Ǧawharī (d. c. 400/1010), the title 
of whose dictionary, al-Ṣaḥāḥ, reflects the same concern. Based on the cru-
cial role he allots to samāʿ, Azharī criticizes some earlier authors for deriving 
their material from manuscripts, rather than through samāʿ. In particular, he 
sharply attacks al-Buštī al-Ḫārzanǧī (d. 348/959) for relying in his Takmilat K. 
al-ʿAyn on written documents (ṣuḥuf; cf. its derivative taṣḥīf) without any sup-
port from samāʿ.205 For his part, he stipulates that, outside the sphere of samāʿ, 
the material which he derives from written sources should not only be in the 
handwriting of a knowledgeable scholar (ḫaṭṭ ḏī maʿrifa ṯāqiba),206 but should 
also be consistent with his own (i.e. Azharī’s) knowledge. This notwithstand-
ing, he uses the method of indicating a word’s pattern or specifying its vowels 
or diacritics much less than Qālī or Ǧawharī do.

Ḫillikān, Wafayāt IV, 334.

<!-- page 324 -->

314
chapter 3
Another aspect of Azharī’s adherence to correct usage is his critical approach 
to earlier works, the most important of which for our purpose are K. al-ʿAyn 
and Ǧamharat al-luġa. Although he modeled his lexicon after al-ʿAyn and 
incorporated the bulk of its material into his own lexicon, Azharī meticulously 
checked the correctness of data cited from it. The standard expression that 
he uses in introducing quotations from al-ʿAyn in the lemmata is qāla l-Layṯ, 
and it is a recurring reminder of his belief that Ḫalīl’s role in it is restricted 
to what he calls the concise foundation (al-ta⁠ʾsīs al-muǧmal) in its begin-
ning, whereas the rest is the work of Layṯ (d. 190/805).207 In contrast, he uses 
the expression qāla l-Ḫalīl in connection with the quotations from al-ʿAyn’s 
introduction which he cites towards the end of his own introduction.208 It is 
interesting to note that the frequent occurrence of qāla l-Layṯ in Ibn Manẓūr’s 
(d. 711/1311) Lisān al-ʿArab is most probably a direct quotation from al-Tahḏīb, 
one of the five sources from which al-Lisān is derived, and is not indicative 
of Ibn Manẓūr’s personal view as to the book’s author since he often refers to 
Ḫalīl in other quotations, also based on his sources.209
Azharī’s attribution of al-ʿAyn, with the exception of its introduction, to Layṯ 
and not to Ḫalīl, who was unanimously held in high esteem by subsequent phi-
lologists, opened the way for him to highlight what he believed to be grave errors 
in its text. In the introduction, he explains that he has repeatedly examined 
al-ʿAyn and recorded its errors and the dubious lexical items that it contained, 
although he admits that these are few in number compared to the outstand-
ingly correct material.210 For him, any lexical item which occurs in al-ʿAyn is 
dubious unless he is able to confirm its correctness through a reliable source 
(imām min al-ṯiqāt). In the text of al-Tahḏīb, Azharī frequently dismisses Layṯ’s 
definitions as wrong or questionable,211 corrects his interpretation of certain 
šawāhid,212 and amends the diacritical points of words which exhibit taṣḥīf.213 
Furthermore, if the ascription to Azharī of a refutation of Layṯ in a book titled 
al-Radd ʿalā l-Layṯ is correct,214 it would most probably have contained some 
of the points raised against Layṯ in al-Tahḏīb. But despite Azharī’s numerous

<!-- page 325 -->

315
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
amendments to the text of al-ʿAyn, it was one of his main sources for lexical 
items, definitions and šawāhid. This seems to have prompted some of his con-
temporaries to accuse him of plagiarizing al-ʿAyn. The three lines of poetry 
which contain this accusation and are reported by Ṯaʿālibī (d. 429/1039)215 are 
curiously similar to the three lines in which Ibn Durayd was accused of exactly 
the same plagiarism. As in the case of Ibn Durayd,216 this accusation is grossly 
unjustified, given not only Azharī’s critical approach to al-ʿAyn’s content but 
also the large number of scholars, other than Ḫalīl, whom he quotes in almost 
every lemma of his lexicon.
Azharī adopts a similar strategy in dealing with Ibn Durayd’s Ǧamharat 
al-luġa. He reports in the introduction that he visited Ibn Durayd in his residence 
in Baghdad and heard his riwāya on the authority of Abū Ḥātim al-Siǧistānī 
(d. 255/869), Riyāšī (d. 257/871), and Aṣmaʿī’s nephew (i.e. ʿAbdalraḥmān b. 
ʿAbdallāh; d. ?).217 Azharī quotes his teacher Nifṭawayhi who did not think 
much of Ibn Durayd (istaḫaffa bihi) and described his riwāya as unreliable. 
He then confirms that his own reading of al-Ǧamhara did not reveal a deep 
knowledge on the part of its author, whom he accuses of falsifying Arabic and 
introducing neologisms (iftiʿāl al-ʿArabiyya wa-tawlīd al-alfāẓ), and concludes 
that the lexicon contains several lexical items which are either incorrect or 
dubious. Concerning items which are dubious because they are uniquely men-
tioned by Ibn Durayd, Azharī says that he has included them in his lexicon 
( fa-aṯbattuhā . . . fī mawāqiʿihā), but would only approve of them if they are 
supported by a reliable source. Indeed, the lemmata include references to a 
number of lexical items taken from al-Ǧamhara and judged by Azharī to be 
questionable because he could not find confirmation in other sources that 
they were actually used. Several such items are quadriliterals, such as ḥardama 
(wrangling), ḥurqūf (small reptile),218 or quinqueliterals such as ḥubaqbīq (ill-
tempered) and qalaḥdam (light and swift).219 On several occasions, Azharī sim-
ply cites words of Yamānī origin with which al-Ǧamhara abounds.220 Unlike 
his quotations from al-ʿAyn, those from al-Ǧamhara do not normally include

<!-- page 326 -->

316
chapter 3
faulty definitions or šawāhid. Interestingly enough, Azharī comments neither 
on the alphabetical order of letters which Ibn Durayd adopts nor on his refer-
ence to its ease compared with al-ʿAyn’s phonetic arrangement.
As for the arrangement of al-Tahḏīb, Azharī takes up Ḫalīl’s system almost 
fully, with very minor changes that do not match those introduced by Qālī 
in al-Bāriʿ. Accordingly, the phonetic order of letters is identical with Ḫalīl’s, 
as is the division of each letter into six sections, i.e. biliterals, sound triliter-
als, unsound triliterals, lafīf, quadriliterals and quinqueliterals.221 Azharī also 
follows al-ʿAyn’s system of taqālīb and of separating used roots from unused 
ones. Perhaps the only change he introduces to the arrangement of the mate-
rial has to do with unsound roots. He explains towards the end of the lexicon 
that he has amended Layṯ’s confusing system of listing together hamzated 
and unhamzated roots by separating the two types.222 But although Azharī 
often indicates that a certain root is hamzated and lists it separately from its 
unhamzated counterparts,223 he does not allocate a special section for roots 
of this type. Thus a typical section of muʿtall roots includes those that con-
tain wāw, yāʾ, alif and hamza, as in the section of hāʾ and nūn, which embraces 
hamzated roots, such as HNʾ and NHʾ, as well as initial, median or final weak 
roots, such as WHN, HWN and NHY.224 Appended to the lemmata at the end 
of the lexicon are a number of short chapters which deal with morphological 
and syntactical characteristics related to alif, yāʾ and wāw in a manner similar 
to what we find in books that deal with specific letters;225 the letters at the 
beginning of some Qurʾānic sūras; and several issues related to hamz.226 These 
chapters are reminiscent of those which occur at the end of Ibn Durayd’s 
al-Ǧamhara and perhaps Qālī’s al-Bāriʿ.227
Little effort is made at internally organizing the lemmata. The derivatives of 
the roots are arranged randomly in most cases – even more so than in al-ʿAyn 
itself. Lemmata that are not listed in al-ʿAyn are often begun by the expres-
sion ahmalahu l-Layṯ, whereas material taken from al-ʿAyn is indicated by qāla 
l-Layṯ, either at the beginning of the lemma, within it, or in both locations. 
221	
Note that Naṣṣār (1968: I, 358) and ʿUbaydī (2001a: 502) wrongly state that al-Tahḏīb differs 
from al-ʿAyn in separating quadriliterals from quinqueliterals, whereas the two types are 
in fact separate in both lexica.
222	
Azharī, Tahḏīb XV, 692.

224	
Ibid., VI, 431–446.
225	
Cf. above, 211 ff.
226	
Azharī, Tahḏīb XV, 662–693.
227	
Cf. above, 309 and below, 343.

---

<!-- page 327 -->

317
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
Philologists other than Layṯ are consistently quoted and acknowledged, and 
material derived from them normally accounts for the best part of most lem-
mata. To take but one example, in the root ḪZR,228 the sequence of quoted 
authorities is as follows: Layṯ, ʿAmr (i.e. b. Abī ʿAmr al-Šaybānī), Ibn al-Anbārī, 
Abū Zayd, Ṯaʿlab, Ibn Ḥabīb, Ibn al-Sikkīt, Abū ʿUbayd, Layṯ, Abū ʿUbayd, Ibn 
al-Sikkīt, Ṯaʿlab, Abū ʿUbayd, Mubarrad, a certain šayḫ, Abū l-Hayṯam, and ʿAmr 
b. Baḥr (i.e. al-Ǧāḥiẓ).229 His quotations from these authorities, which follow 
no specific order and often result in repetition, include lexical items and their 
definitions, as well as poetry šawāhid and ġarīb material. Azharī’s own addi-
tions, which are introduced by qultu, are quite numerous, and often lengthy.
Azharī was obviously interested in producing a lexicon that is as exhaus-
tive of roots and lexical items as can be. He thus supplied the roots that are 
not included in al-ʿAyn and considerably increased the number of lexical items 
(often with new meanings introduced to previously mentioned items) in com-
parison with the two muǧannas lexica on which he relied most, i.e. al-ʿAyn 
and al-Ǧamhara. In this regard, he quoted prophetic Ḥadīṯ much more fre-
quently than any of the previous authors of muǧannas lexica. In fact, subse-
quent authors of the genre ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ – such as Zamaḫšarī (d. 538/1144) 
in al-Fāʾiq fī ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ and Ibn al-Aṯīr (d. 606/1210) in al-Nihāya fī ġarīb 
al-Ḥadīṯ230 – often quote Azharī’s definitions of words which occur in Ḥadīṯ. 
Zamaḫšarī, in a note in his own handwriting on the twentieth volume of 
al-Tahḏīb’s manuscript,231 points out that he found in Azharī’s lexicon ḥadīṯs 
which do not occur in the books of Abū ʿUbayd (d. 224/838), Ibn Qutayba 
(d. 276/889), and Ḫaṭṭābī (d. 388/998), that is, in books that specialize in Ḥadīṯ 
and are not general lexica like al-Tahḏīb. Qifṭī (d. 646/1248), who reports the 
content of Zamahšarī’s note, asserts that Abū ʿUbayd al-Harawī (d. 401/1011), a 
student of Azharī’s, derived his K. al-Ġarībayn from al-Tahḏīb232 – an assertion 
by which the Ḥadīṯ part of Harawī’s book is most probably meant, although 
one cannot underestimate Azharī’s numerous references to Qurʾānic ġarīb in 
his lexicon. Even in the šawāhid containing proverbs, Zamaḫšarī admits that 
al-Tahḏīb contains proverbs which are not found in his own book, al-Mustaqṣā. 
Furthermore, and in line with his concern for exhaustiveness, Azharī includes

<!-- page 328 -->

318
chapter 3
a host of toponyms in his lexicon and often describes localities which he saw 
in person.233 Many of these toponyms are mentioned, on Azharī’s authority, 
in Yāqūt’s Muʿǧam al-Buldān.234 From a wider perspective, the influence of 
al-Tahḏīb on subsequent muǧannas lexica can be best illustrated by the fact 
that Ibn Manẓūr (d. 711/1311) incorporated it into his Lisān al-ʿArab along with 
four other lexica, and that Zabīdī (d. 1205/1790), author of one of the most 
exhaustive lexica, Tāǧ al-ʿarūs, listed it among his main sources235 and exten-
sively quoted it. There are also a few abridgements of al-Tahḏīb,236 as well as 
a lexicon titled Tahḏīb al-Tahḏīb, in which its author, Abū l-Ṯanāʾ Maḥmūd b. 
Muḥammad (Abī Bakr) b. Ḥāmid al-Urmawī (d. 723/1323), included material 
from Azharī as well as from Ǧawharī’s (d. c. 400/1010) al-Ṣaḥāḥ and Ibn Sīda’s 
(d. 458/1066) al-Muḥkam.237
Azharī was not an innovator but faithfully followed Ḫalīl’s system in almost 
every detail. His real contribution lies (other than his introduction which 
provides an overview of the whole discipline, and the fact that he preserved, 
from his encounters with the Aʿrāb and his desert experience, linguistic data 
that might have otherwise never been recorded) in the exhaustiveness of his 
material and his meticulousness in checking data derived from earlier sources, 
albeit in a manner which betrays arrogance and at times involves exagger-
ated criticism. The two features of exhaustiveness and meticulousness are 
closely linked to the linguistic situation in Azharī’s time. As he explains in the 
introduction, the time of the Prophet’s Companions (Ṣaḥāba) – who learned 
directly from the Prophet what they needed to know about the Qurʾān and its 
difficult or obscure meanings – is long gone.238 According to him, the Ṣaḥāba, 
unlike people of his own days, needed neither to know the various dialects 
of the Arabs nor make any effort at learning correct Arabic. He obviously 
believed that earlier lexicographers did not do enough to achieve the arduous 
aim of exhausting as many lexical items as possible, and that his own lexicon 
made up for this deficiency by encompassing the largest possible amount of 
correct material that could benefit users who no longer mastered Arabic nor 
had direct access to “pure” Bedouin usage, although he admits that it did not

139, 143, 146, etc.

<!-- page 329 -->

319
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
encompass all words and all meanings.239 Azharī must have felt that he was 
uniquely qualified to perform the task he assigned for himself. First of all, he 
had access to direct samāʿ from the fuṣāḥāʾ, in particular during his captivity, 
at a time when it was becoming increasingly difficult to encounter trustwor-
thy fuṣaḥāʾ whose speech – just like that of his captors –  was not tarnished 
with incorrect usage (laḥn) or blatant error (ḫaṭa⁠ʾ fāḥiš).240 In addition to this, 
he relied on extensive riwāya and close knowledge of the sources listed in his 
introduction to detect what he referred to as errors of taṣḥīf and definition, 
which contemporary users of lexica (ṭullāb hāḏā l-ša⁠ʾn min abnāʾ zamāninā) 
were no longer able to spot,241 and which even escaped the notice of con-
temporary philologists, save the discerning few (al-mumayyizūn min ʿulamāʾ 
al-luġa).242 Regardless of how justified were some of the corrections made by 
Azharī to alleged mistakes committed by earlier philologists, the abundance 
of these corrections reflected his keenness on critically examining the quoted 
material in order to ensure its soundness – a riding concern of other fourth/
tenth century lexicographers, such as Qālī in al-Bāriʿ and Ǧawharī in al-Ṣaḥāḥ.
2.4	
al-Muḥīṭ fī l-luġa by al-Ṣāḥib b. ʿAbbād (d. 385/995)
Abū l-Qāsim Ismāʿīl b. ʿAbbād al-Ṭālaqānī, better known as al-Ṣāḥib b. ʿAbbād, 
is one of the most versatile figures of the fourth/tenth century. He was a gifted 
poet and a highly cultured man whose strong relationship with Buwayhī rul-
ers and eventual appointment as wazīr by Muʾayyid al-Dawla (d. 373/984) and 
Faḫr al-Dawla (d. 387/997) did not distract him from authoring a number of 
literary and linguistic works.243 We have already encountered in the previ-
ous chapter two of his works, namely, al-Amṯāl al-sāʾira min šiʿr al-Mutanabbī 
and al-Farq bayna l-ḍād wa-l-ẓāʾ.244 His most important contribution however 
is undoubtedly al-Muḥīṭ fī l-luġa, published in ten volumes (plus indices in 
volume 11).
Unlike Azharī, whose lengthy introduction to al-Tahḏīb contains an over-
view of philological sources and a critical assessment of earlier lexica, 
al-Ṣāḥib’s introduction briefly points out some of the morphological and pho-
netic principles which Ḫalīl adopted in al-ʿAyn. These include the division of 
lexical items into biliterals, triliterals, quadriliterals and quinqueliterals; the

<!-- page 330 -->

320
chapter 3
difference between sound (saḥīḥ) and unsound (muʿtall) forms; the reason for 
Ḫalīl not beginning his lexicon with uniliterals despite Sībawayhi’s assertion 
that the minimum number of radicals a word can have is one; Ḫalīl’s phonetic 
arrangement of letters; and the reason for his beginning with the letter ʿayn.245 
On the last issue, he quotes Mubarrad’s (d. 285/898) view that Ḫalīl’s division of 
gutturals was tripartite, namely, hamza and hāʾ; ʿayn and ḥāʾ; and ġayn and ḫāʾ. 
According to Mubarrad, Ḫalīl chose to begin with ʿayn because it falls in the 
middle of the group and is thus close to both its ends. Apparently unsatisfied 
with the explanation (and rightly so), al-Ṣāḥib proposes that the correct justi-
fication of Ḫalīl’s beginning with ʿayn is that it is the letter which has the clear-
est ring and is the most pleasant to hear (anṣaʿ al-ḥurūf ǧarsan wa alaḏḏuhā 
samāʿan) and hence the most frequent in use, contrary to hamza, which is con-
stricted (mahtūta, maḍġūṭa), and hāʾ, which is constricted and unclear (fīhā 
hatta wa-ḫafāʾ).246 This marginal and speculative issue aside, al-Muḥīṭ’s intro-
duction truly reflects the fact that it is based primarily on al-ʿAyn.
Al-Ṣāḥib faithfully follows al-ʿAyn’s arrangement of letters, its system of 
taqālīb and the division of each chapter (i.e. ḥarf ) into six sections: biliterals, 
sound triliterals, unsound triliterals, lafīf, quadriliterals and quinqueliterals. 
As in al-ʿAyn, under biliterals are listed items which are either not geminate 
(cf. qad), geminate (cf. qadda), or reduplicated (cf. daqdaqa).247 Among other 
inconsistencies is that several lexical items appear both under quadriliterals 
and quinqueliterals, e.g. zabantar (short man or great misfortune).248 Often 
the meanings given to such words in each of the two sections are not alike. For 
example, qafandar is explained in quadriliterals as a synonym of qafdar (ugly), 
but as “short and fleshy” or “huge camel” or “white thing” in ­quinqueliterals.249 
Similarly, ibrinšāq is explained as “flowering” in one location and “joy” in 
another.250
Despite al-Muḥīṭ’s lack of innovation in aspects related to arrangement, the 
content of its lemmata yields a different picture. To start with, al-Ṣāḥib seems 
to have considered al-ʿAyn as a base to which material from other sources 
should be added. This is particularly clear in lemmata which he begins by 
indicating that they do not feature in al-ʿAyn. The expressions he uses in this 
respect (e.g. ahmalahu l-Ḫalīl; muhmal ʿind al-Ḫalīl) are normally followed

<!-- page 331 -->

321
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
by material ascribed to Ḫārzanǧī (d. 348/959),251 obviously taken from his 
Takmilat K. al-ʿAyn.252 Ḫārzanǧī is quoted about 500 times in al-Muḥīṭ, which 
makes his al-Takmila the primary source from which al-Ṣāḥib supplemented 
al-ʿAyn’s material. Other philologists are quoted only sporadically, and usually 
more in the first half of the lexicon than in the second. In descending order of 
the number of times quoted, these philologists include Abū ʿAmr al-Šaybānī 
(19 times), Abū Zayd al-Anṣārī (18), al-Naḍr b. Šumayl (16), Farrāʾ (12), Aṣmaʿī 
(11), Ibn al-Aʿrābī (8), Abū Saʿīd (either al-Ḍarīr or al-Sīrāfī; 8), Ibn al-Sikkīt (7), 
Abū ʿUbayd (6), Abū Ḥātim al-Siǧistānī (6), Ibn Durayd (6), Kisāʾī (5), Ǧāḥiẓ 
(3), Bāhilī (3), Layṯ (2), Abū ʿUbayda (2), Liḥyānī (2), Ibn Qutayba (2), and Abū 
Turāb (2). al-Ṣāḥib evidently had at his disposal, in addition to Ḫārzanǧī’s al-
Takmila, a large number of sources from which he could supplement al-ʿAyn. 
According to one account, the weight of the books he personally owned was 
equivalent to the load of 400 camels.253
As far as authors of other muǧannas lexica are concerned, not a single men-
tion is made of Ibn Fāris (d. 395/1004), who was a teacher and a close friend 
of al-Ṣaḥib’s, and who named his book on philology al-Ṣāḥibī fī fiqh al-luġa 
after him. It is even more strange that al-Ṣāḥib adopted Ḫalīl’s complex sys-
tem of letter arrangement, taqālīb, and division of chapters in preference to 
the alphabetical system and relatively easier arrangement of roots adopted 
by Ibn Fāris in al-Maqāyīs and al-Muǧmal. There may be a remote possibility 
that he knew of neither lexicon, or that, despite the close proximity between 
the dates of the two authors, al-Ṣāḥib completed his lexicon before Ibn Fāris 
finished either of his. More probable however is that al-Ṣāḥib may not have 
found it appropriate, after having decided to emulate al-ʿAyn and supplement 
it with material from Ḫārzanǧī’s al-Takmila, to quote a contemporary of his 
when he had at his disposal several earlier, hence presumably more reliable, 
sources. The same reason may be given to the fact that no mention is made 
either of Qālī’s (d. 356/967) al-Bāriʿ or Azharī’s (d. 370/981) al-Tahḏīb, although 
one cannot exclude the possibility that al-Ṣāḥib did not have access to either 
work since both Qālī and Azharī completed their lexica towards the end of 
their lives, probably not much earlier than al-Ṣāḥib started compiling al-Muḥīṭ. 
Ibn Durayd (d. 321/933), on the other hand, is mentioned six times, in one of 
which al-Ǧamhara is mentioned by name, unlike al-Ṣāḥib’s normal practice of 
mentioning the names of cited philologists but not the titles of their works.254 
251

<!-- page 332 -->

322
chapter 3
al-Ṣāḥib might have sparingly quoted al-Ǧamhara since he had abridged it in 
a separate work titled Ǧawharat al-Ǧamhara.255 It is noteworthy that three of 
the six references to Ibn Durayd by al-Ṣāḥib involve material uniquely reported 
by Ibn Durayd, including two Yamānī lexical items.
al-Ṣāḥib apparently wanted to collect in his lexicon the largest possible 
number of lexical items. To keep the work to a manageable size, he included 
relatively few šawāhid. In the ten volumes,256 which amount to about 4,800 
pages, there are 361 šawāhid from the Qurʾān, 418 from Ḥadīṯ and Aṯar, and 
577 from poetry. There are also 268 references to dialects – much less than in 
al-Tahḏīb, which is replete with dialectal material. The number of proverbs, 
1,048, is relatively large and reflects al-Ṣāḥib’s interest in this speech type (cf. his 
al-Amṯāl al-sāʾira which he collected from Mutanabbī’s poetry). Qifṭī succinctly 
observes that al-Ṣāḥib, by increasing the number of lexical items and reducing 
the number of šawāhid (kaṯṯara fīhi l-alfāẓ wa-qallala fīhi l-šawāhid) was able 
to include in his lexicon a sizeable portion of the language ( fa-štamala min 
al-luġa ʿalā ǧuzʾ mutawaffir).257 Other than cutting down on šawāhid, al-Ṣāḥib 
usually gives brief definitions, avoids citing differences among scholars in 
interpretation of meanings, and makes very few digressions. Lexical items are 
consecutively listed without any discernible order. Given that al-Muḥīṭ lacks 
any innovative aspect in its arrangement, its value lies mainly in preserving 
some of Ḫārzanǧī’s material in al-Takmila and in uniquely citing lexical items 
(including ġarīb ones) which are absent from earlier lexica and which al-Ṣāḥib 
derived from the numerous sources that were available to him. The lexicon’s 
title, which means “encompassing” or “embracing” (or perhaps “ocean”), thus 
conforms well to its content.
2.5	
al-Muḥkam wa-l-muḥīṭ al-aʿẓam by Ibn Sīda (d. 458/1066)
At a time when the phonetic-permutative system was practically abandoned 
by Eastern lexicographers, the Andalusian scholar Ibn Sīda chose to apply 
it in his al-Muḥkam. By his time, three major eastern authors of muǧannas 
lexica, namely, Ibn Durayd (d. 321/933), Ibn Fāris (d. 395/1004), and Ǧawharī 
(d. c. 400/1010) had adopted the alphabetical system of arranging letters and 
(with the exception of Ibn Durayd) given up the permutations of roots. Ibn 
Sīda does not reveal the reasons for his choice, and given that al-ʿAyn is not 
his major source of data but one of several sources which he incorporates into 
his lexicon, one cannot be sure whether he adopted its arrangement in order 
255	
Ibn al-Anbārī, Nuzha 239; Yāqūt, Muʿǧam II, 698; Suyūṭī, Muzhir I, 96.
256	
The numbers in each type of šawāhid are counted based on the indices in volume eleven.
257	
Qifṭī, Inbāh I, 236; cf. Ibn Ḫillikān, Wafayāt I, 230.

---

<!-- page 333 -->

323
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
to use it as a base to which material from other lexica could be added. It may 
be suggested that he followed in the footsteps of another Andalusian scholar, 
Qālī (d. 356/967), whose al-Bāriʿ applies Ḫalīl’s system of arrangement. More 
importantly, he seems to have modeled his lexicon on another Andalusian 
work, Zubaydī’s (d. 379/989) Muḫtaṣar al-ʿAyn, as he closely observed the 
improvements introduced by its author to Ḫalīl’s arrangement of the sections 
included in each chapter (see below).
It has already been pointed out that Ibn Sīda makes a clear distinction 
between mubawwab and muǧannas lexica – two terms which we owe to him in 
referring to onomasiological and semasiological lexica respectively.258 Based 
on the difference between the two types in arranging linguistic material and 
consequently in the needs of their users, he justifies his authoring of two lex-
ica, one of each type, although his sources are largely common to both. He 
explains in the introduction of al-Muḫaṣṣaṣ that whereas the muǧannas type 
(i.e. al-Muḥkam) is devised for the reader who seeks a specific word placed 
in a precise location, the mubawwab one (i.e. al-Muḫaṣṣaṣ) is thematically 
arranged in order to provide the orator (ḫaṭīb) or poet (šāʿir) with several nouns 
(asmāʾ) and adjectives (awṣāf) related to a certain semantic field, thus allow-
ing him to choose the one that best suits his needs or fits the required rhyme.259 
His reference to al-Muḥkam in al-Muḫaṣṣaṣ, however, does not mean that he 
authored it before the latter. Indeed, he also refers in al-Muḥkam’s introduc-
tion to al-Muḫaṣṣaṣ, which he says he authored (allaftu) in a thematic fashion 
(ʿalā l-tabwīb).260 Accordingly, he must have authored both books simultane-
ously, although it is difficult to determine with any certitude which of them he 
finished first since in each he refers to the other as having been accomplished. 
Yet his mention in al-Muḥkam of the introduction of al-Muḫaṣṣaṣ, which he 
says includes the reason for authoring it and a description of its system, may 
indicate that he completed it before al-Muḥkam, given that one would nor-
mally write the introduction after the completion of the text.
Ibn Sīda obviously wanted al-Muḥkam to be as comprehensive as pos-
sible. He proudly declares in its introduction that, in lexicography ( fī hāḏihi 
l-ṣināʿa), it stands as the most comprehensive large-scale lexicon and 
describes it as al-muḥīṭ al-aʿẓam261 – an expression which he annexed to the 
main title, al-Muḥkam. One cannot fail to see in this an indirect suggestion 
that al-Muḥkam’s material is more exhaustive of the language than al-Ṣāḥib’s 
258	
Ibn Sīda, Muḫaṣṣaṣ I, 10, 12; cf. above, 47–48, 276.
259	
Ibid., I, 10.
260	
Ibn Sīda, Muḥkam I, 36.
261	
Ibid., I, 48.

<!-- page 334 -->

324
chapter 3
al-Muḥīṭ fī l-luġa (which shares with Ibn Sīda’s lexicon part of its title), 
although the two lexica are roughly equal in length. As if to highlight the com-
prehensiveness of al-Muḥkam, Ibn Sīda refers even to his own huge thesau-
rus al-Muḫaṣṣaṣ, in the context of justifying the authoring of his two lexica, 
as an abridged work (mulaḫḫaṣ).262 To achieve his purpose of exhaustiveness, 
Ibn Sīda incorporated into al-Muḥkam most of the material of several earlier 
muǧannas lexica; more specifically, Ḫalīl’s al-ʿAyn, Ibn Durayd’s al-Ǧamhara, 
and Qālī’s al-Bāriʿ (the first two of which he specifically mentions in the intro-
duction). He normally does not specify his sources in the lemmata, unlike Qālī, 
Azharī and al-Ṣāḥib, who do so in varying degrees, and unlike his own method 
in al-Muḫaṣṣaṣ of specifying the source of almost every single item he cites. 
Other than muǧannas works, he lists in the introduction the titles of several 
mubawwab sources which he used, in addition to a number of grammatical 
works, such as the books of Sībawayhi, Abū ʿAlī al-Fārisī, Rummānī and Ibn 
Ǧinnī.263 These sources are almost identical with the ones he cites in the intro-
duction of al-Muḫaṣṣaṣ,264 and this makes it more likely, according to Ṭālibī, 
that he simultaneously authored his two lexica by using the same reference 
works.265
But despite Ibn Sīda’s attempt at exhausting lexical items and consecutively 
listing the various meanings that each of them can have, he generally seeks to 
be brief in his explanations and to ignore some šawāhid that are cited in the 
sources he quotes, although the lexicon as a whole contains a large number 
of šawāhid.266 In addition to this, he adopts a few norms which are aimed at 
conciseness as we shall see later. The two apparently contradictory, but in fact 
complementary, features of al-Muḥkam, namely, comprehensiveness (al-išbāʿ 
wa-l-ittisāʿ) and brevity (al-īǧāz wa-l-iḫtiṣār), are succinctly referred to, side by 
side, by Ibn Sīda in enumerating the good features of his lexicon.267 It should 
also be noted that Ibn Sīda frequently dwells on grammatical issues (particu-
larly morphological ones) and to a lesser extent on matters related to prosody.268 
This feature is consistent with his assertion that his mastery of ʿilm al-luġa

<!-- page 335 -->

325
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
(here in the narrow sense of lexicography) is much less than his expertise 
in grammar, prosody, logic and rhetoric – a comment which is by no means 
intended to undermine the lexicographical skills of which he is highly proud. 
Although Ibn Sīda expressly interrelates lexicography with grammar, prosody, 
logic and rhetoric, his comments, most of which belong to grammar and pros-
ody, are simply elucidations of certain intricate patterns and forms. Hence, 
Carter’s suggestion that Ibn Sīda “may have been the first to compile a dic-
tionary within the framework of a unified semantic theory”269 is inconsistent 
with the text of al-Muḥkam. In fact, Ibn Sīda does not propose the elements 
of such a theory in his lengthy introduction; rather, he often demonstrates his 
profound knowledge of grammar and prosody in explaining usage but makes 
no substantial link between lexicography and other linguistic sciences in a 
manner that differs in essence, rather than in degree, from the approach of 
other philologists.
The arrangement of letters in al-Muḥkam is identical with Ḫalīl’s. In this 
respect, Ibn Sīda is closer to Azharī and al-Ṣāḥib than to Qālī, whose al-Bāriʿ 
differs somewhat in its phonetic arrangement from al-ʿAyn.270 It is noteworthy 
that Ibn Sīda, in the section on maqṣūr and mamdūd in al-Muḫaṣṣaṣ, adopts 
Qālī’s method in his al-Maqṣūr wa-l-mamdūd of dividing the material accord-
ing to morphological patterns, but when it comes to the phonetic ordering of 
the letters within each section, he gives up Qālī’s arrangement (which is the 
same as in al-Bāriʿ) in favor of Ḫalīl’s,271 thus making this phonetically arranged 
part of al-Muḫaṣṣaṣ consistent with al-Muḥkam’s arrangement.
As far as the sections within each letter are concerned, Ibn Sīda follows 
Zubaydī’s method of expanding Ḫalīl’s divisions.272 Accordingly, the sections 
that are normally included are: sound reduplicated biliterals, sound triliterals, 
unsound reduplicated biliterals, unsound triliterals, lafīf, quadriliterals and 
quinqueliterals. In a few cases he adds to these sections an eighth section on 
sexiliterals (sudāsī). The first section, which is misleadingly titled bāb al-ṯunāʾī 
al-muḍāʿaf al-ṣaḥīḥ, includes a variety of forms. In the letter hāʾ, for example, 
it includes biliterals which end with quiescence, hence their depiction as ḫafīf, 
such as onomatopoeic hiḫ (sound of expectoration) and hiġ (sound of gar-
gling); biliterals whose second radical is doubled and are thus generally rec-
ognized by morphologists as triliteral, such as haqqa (to escape); words whose 
first and last radicals are identical, as in the expression yā hanāhu (O man); 
269	
Carter (1990: 111).
270	
Cf. above, 305.
271	
Ibn Sīda, Muḫaṣṣaṣ XV, 95 ff; cf. above, 246.
272	
Cf. above, 301.

<!-- page 336 -->

326
chapter 3
and ­reduplicated quadriliterals, such as hazhaza (to shake) and nahnaha (to 
restrain from).273 Although examples of the ḫafīf type are listed under separate 
headings in each combination of two letters (e.g. h+q, h+k, h+ǧ, etc.), redupli-
cated quadriliterals are not systematically arranged since he lists them with 
biliterals that have a doubled second radical (e.g. hazhaza is placed with hazza) 
or with words that have identical first and last radicals (e.g. nahnaha is placed 
with yā hanāhu). The section of unsound duplicated biliterals (bāb al-ṯunāʾī 
al-muḍāʿaf min al-muʿtall) follows the same arrangement as the first section of 
biliterals.274 As for unsound triliterals, Ibn Sīda follows Zubaydī’s suit in sepa-
rately listing roots with hamza, yāʾ and wāw, in that order.275 Sexiliterals occur 
very rarely, as in ḥabaṭiqṭiq (sound made by a horse’s hooves) and šāhasfaram 
(a type of plant; ocimum minimum), which are the last words under ḥāʾ and hāʾ 
respectively.276 Ibn Sīda’s classification of such words as sexiliteral, however, 
does not mean that he violates the consensus of morphologists that no word 
can have more than five radicals; rather, it is a classificatory device intended 
to separate such onomatopoeic or Arabized words from the rest of the corpus 
without having to claim, as morphologists often do, that not all their radicals 
are original and hence append them to shorter patterns.277
Although Ibn Sīda in his lemmata is not as critical as Azharī of the data he 
quotes on the authority of earlier authors, the disparaging comments he directs 
against some of them in his introduction are reminiscent of Azharī’s harsh 
criticism of a number of his predecessors in the introduction of al-Tahḏīb. 
For example, he mentions a few morphological errors made by Ibn al-Sikkīt 
(d. 244/858) and says that his Iṣlāḥ al-manṭiq contains an innumerable number 
of errors.278 He also cites mistakes made in prosodical and morphological mat-
ters by Abū ʿUbayd (d. 224/838) in al-Ġarīb al-muṣannaf.279 His most severe 
attack, however, is aimed at Ibn al-Aʿrābī (d. 231/845) for having cited, as plural 
of ʿaduww (enemy), the forms aʿdāʾ, aʿādī, ʿudāt, ʿidā and ʿudā. Ibn Sīda explains 
that Ibn Aʿrābī’s statement is misleading because some of these forms are the 
plurals of words other than ʿaduww (e.g. ʿudāt is the plural of ʿādī) or represent

<!-- page 337 -->

327
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
a specific type of plural (e.g. aʿādī is secondary plural or ǧamʿ al-ǧamʿ).280 He 
then belittles Ibn Aʿrābī’s knowledge of morphology, which, he says, does not 
even come close to his own profound understanding of morphological forms. 
He also arrogantly declares that only individuals of his status are in a position 
to criticize earlier scholars (wa-hal yaqūm bi-ntiqād hāḏā l-nawʿ illā miṯlī), such 
as Ibn al-Sikkīt, Ibn al-Aʿrābī, Abū Zayd (d. 215/830), Abū ʿUbayda (d. 209/824), 
and Aṣmaʿī (d. 216/831).281 Towards the end of the introduction,282 he repeats 
praise of his own knowledge of philology, grammar and prosody, but more 
modestly admits that, not being a contemporary of the Bedouin fuṣaḥāʾ, he 
should be more liable to error than the philologists who derived their data 
from them. Accordingly, he does not deny the possibility that, out of five thou-
sand cases, he might be wrong in one!
More significant than Ibn Sīda’s above remarks are the few examples in 
which he demonstrates the deficiency that characterizes the formulation of 
data by some of his predecessors, and which offer him the opportunity of 
explaining how his method is superior to theirs. One example is the following 
expression which Abū ʿUbayd cites on the authority of Abū ʿAmr al-Šaybānī 
(d. 206/821): al-unūf yuqāl lahā al-maḫāṭim wāḥiduhā maḫṭim. Ibn Sīda com-
pares this with his own expression al-maḫṭim al-anf (i.e. nose).283 He notes 
that Abū ʿUbayd’s expression not only cites the plural before the singular but 
is unnecessarily long. By citing the singular form maḫṭim, Ibn Sīda explains, 
there is no need to cite the plural form since it can only be the regular pattern 
of words whose singular is of the pattern mafʿil, hence mafāʿil, i.e. maḫāṭim. 
In contrast, the plural form maḫāṭim, which Abū ʿUbayd cites first, is not the 
plural of mafʿil exclusively since it also occurs as the plural of mafʿal, mafʿila, 
mafʿala, etc. Similarly, he criticizes the following expression of Abū ʿUbayd’s in 
explaining tama⁠ʾʾā (to expand, said of a waterskin): tama⁠ʾʾā l-ǧildu tama⁠ʾʾiyan 
miṯālu tamaʿʿā tamaʿʿiyan tafaʿʿala tafaʿʿulan iḏā ttasaʿa. He argues that his own 
expression ma⁠ʾawtu l-ǧilda wa-ma⁠ʾaytuhu fa-tama⁠ʾʾā is better on at least two 
counts, namely, that it cites the simple (basīṭ, i.e. unaugmented) verb from 
which the compound (i.e. murakkab, i.e. augmented) verb is derived, and that 
it links the pattern tafaʿʿala of the reflexive verb tama⁠ʾʾā to the corresponding 
transitive verb (i.e. ma⁠ʾawtu/ma⁠ʾaytu).284 What Ibn Sīda does not mention is

---

<!-- page 338 -->

328
chapter 3
that he also assumes in formulating his expression that he does not need to 
cite the verbal noun tama⁠ʾʾī or its morphological pattern exemplified by substi-
tuting the ʿayn for the alif (as morphologists often do) because both are regular.
Ibn Sīda’s critical approach to the way in which his predecessors formulated 
their definitions is unprecedented and clearly expresses his conviction that the 
task of a lexicographer – or what he refers to as ṣināʿa – is to provide the reader, 
based on a set template, with all the necessary data in the most economic 
manner possible. As far as we are able to determine, he is the first author of a 
muǧannas lexicon to lay in his introduction a plan which includes the norms 
to be followed in the presentation of data. In surveying his plan,285 he men-
tions several types of information which lexicographers often ignore, and 
which he regularly includes in the lemmata. Among these are: the distinction 
among various types of plurals (e.g. plurals, secondary plurals or ǧamʿ al-ǧamʿ, 
and collective nouns); the distinction between two types of hamza elision, the 
substitutional (badalī) and the regular (qiyāsī); the mention of all the forms of 
the plural of paucity (adnā l-ʿadad) or plural of multitude (akṯar al-ʿadad) that 
a word has; the distinction between qalb (mutation) and badal (substitution); 
the citing of irregular forms of gentilic adjectives (nasab), plurals, diminutives, 
verbal nouns, verbs, imāla (fronting and raising of a long or short fatḥa), pat-
terns (abniya), etc.; the mention of the lack of a corresponding verb in cer-
tain passive participles (e.g mafʾūd “cowardly”, but not *fuʾida) and the lack 
of a corresponding verbal noun in certain verbs (e.g. yaḏaru “to leave”, but not 
*waḏr); the linking between verbs which are the result of metathesis (qalb, 
e.g. ayisa<ya⁠ʾisa “to despair”) and those which result from dialectal variation 
(e.g. ǧaḏaba and ǧabaḏa); the mention of the origin of words which may not 
be recognizable due to change in their patterns (e.g. ǧāh “glory, honor” <waǧh 
“appearance”); the distinction between forms with yāʾ or wāw (e.g. qanaytu and 
qanawtu “I acquired”); and the mention of the use of certain words exclusively 
as adverbs.
The economies applied by Ibn Sīda also form part of his plan to present his 
definitions in a systematic manner. He notes that it would be superfluous to 
mention certain forms or patterns because they are regular (qiyāsī, muṭṭarid). 
These include mifʿāl if mifʿal is mentioned; ifʿālla if ifʿalla is mentioned; fuʿālil 
and faʿālil if fuʿalil and faʿalil are mentioned; sound masculine plurals, except 
if irregular (samāʿī; e.g. araḍīn “lands”); regular broken plurals, verbal nouns, 
and nouns of time and place; and admirative verbal patterns, except if derived 
from other than verbs. He also indicates the feminine simply by mentioning 
the suffix hāʾ following the masculine form, unless there is a change in pattern 
(e.g. bint “daughter” <ibn “son”).
285	
Ibid., I, 38–46.

---

<!-- page 339 -->

329
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
In his criticism of earlier authors, Ibn Sīda highlights the importance of 
organizing the elements which make up the definition of any particular lexi-
cal item. He specifically mentions that the singular should be cited before the 
plural, the plural of paucity before the plural of multitude, and the ground or 
unaugmented form of the verb before the augmented derivatives.286 Among 
these, the third condition is the most important since it is also related to the 
internal arrangement of the material within a whole lemma and not merely 
in the definition of a specific lexical item. Unfortunately, Ibn Sīda generally 
does not apply this condition and often cites unaugmented and augmented 
forms in a disorganized manner. For example, the unaugmented form bakā (to 
weep) is mentioned before abkā and bakkā (to cause to weep), but is cited once 
more following them.287 In certain cases, the repetition of unaugmented forms 
after augmented ones is due to semantic reasons. For example, ṣalla (to emit a 
sound) occurs before the semantically related root ṣalṣala (to clatter), but both 
are followed by ṣalla (to become dry, said of a waterskin) and ṣulṣul (portion 
of water left in a pool), both of which belong to a different semantic field.288 
Similarly, ʿaṣara and its derivatives aʿṣara, ʿaṣṣara and iʿtaṣara are intermixed 
based on meaning.289 Nominal derivatives also often occur in no specific order 
and are not clearly separated from verbal forms. Yet there is a distinct effort to 
list together, within each lemma, lexical items that are semantically related – 
a method which Ibn Manẓūr (d. 711/1311) seems to have borrowed from 
al-Muḥkam and which will be discussed in the section on al-Lisān.290
In spite of Ibn Sīda’s unprecedented efforts at systematization and organi-
zation, and after coming so close, at least at the theoretical level, to proposing 
a scheme for the arrangement of the elements of the definition and deriva-
tives of the root, he was largely unsuccessful in the internal arrangement of his 
material and thus could not relieve the user of having to read through much of 
the lemma in order to find the required lexical item.
3	
The Alphabetical System
What is meant by the alphabetical system is the use of the ʾ, b, t, ṯ, ǧ, ḥ, ḫ, etc. 
order of letters in listing lexical items, beginning with the first radical of a 
word’s root (and, less often, the first letter which may be a prefix, rather than 
286	
Ibid., I, 38–39.
287	
Ibid., VII, 115–116.
288	
Ibid., VIII, 266–268.
289	
Ibid., I, 428–432.
290	
Cf. below, 390.

<!-- page 340 -->

330
chapter 3
an original letter). Although works which arrange lexical items based on their 
last letters use the same alphabetical order, they are normally classified as rep-
resentative of the rhyme system (see Section 4 below).
Based on the death dates of their authors, Ḫalīl’s (d. 175/791) K. al-ʿAyn is only 
a few decades earlier than Abū ʿAmr al-Šaybānī’s (d. 206/821) K. al-Ǧīm, the first 
muǧannas lexicon known to us which uses the alphabetical system. Only the 
first letters of words are taken into consideration in their arrangement therein. 
The first muǧannas lexicon to arrange words – at least in most sections –  in full 
alphabetical order (i.e. initial, medial and final radicals) is Ǧamharat al-luġa 
by Ibn Durayd (d. 321/933), although it shares with the lexica that follow the 
phonetic-permutative system the features of taqālīb and divisions based on 
root length. Full alphabetical order is consistently applied for the first time in 
Zamaḫšarī’s (d. 538/1144) Asās al-balāġa, in which the taqālīb and division by 
root length are also abandoned. Ibn Durayd justifies the use of the ʾ, b, t, ṯ, etc. 
order of letters by the fact that it is known both to the ʿāmma (common peo-
ple, generality of people) and the ḫāṣṣa (elite or highly educated people), and 
is distinctly easier than Ḫalīl’s phonetic arrangement.291 A similar argument 
is used by other authors, such as Kurāʿ al-Naml (d. 310/922) in al-Muǧarrad 
fī ġarīb kalām al-ʿArab wa-luġātihā,292 Ibn Wallād (d. 332/944) in al-Maqṣūr 
wa-l-mamdūd,293 and Fārābī (d. 350/961) in Dīwān al-adab.294 Dīnawarī 
(d. 282/895), for his part, compares the alphabetical arrangement of letters 
with the rhyme system and asserts his preference of the former (wa-taṣnīfuhā 
ʿalā ḥurūf awāʾilihā aḥabb ilayya min taṣnīfihā ʿalā ḥurūf awāḫirihā) based on 
ease of use compared not only with the rhyme system but also with any other 
system (ahwan . . . min kull taṣnīf siwāhu).295
In the previous chapter we came across a large number of mubawwab and 
specialized lexica that are alphabetically arranged based either on all the 
radicals of a word or part thereof. Several of these are by third/ninth cen-
tury authors and are thus relatively early. In certain chapters of Abū Zayd 
al-Anṣārī’s (215/830) K. al-Hamz, there seems to be an attempt at arrang-
ing words based on their initial letters.296 In the genre of ḫalq al-insān, Ibn 
Ḥabīb’s (d. 245/860) alphabetical arrangement of words based on first letters

<!-- page 341 -->

331
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
only is probably unprecedented.297 Also in the third/ninth century, Dīnawarī 
arranges the names of plants in the second part of his K. al-Nabāt in alphabeti-
cal order. In this arrangement, which is based solely on the first letter (contrary 
to Seidensticker’s claim that Dīnawarī uses “the fully developed alphabetical 
system”),298 no distinction is made between original letters and prefixes (e.g. 
islīḥ and iġrīḍ are under alif ).299
There are also works in which lexical items are arranged according to 
their first two letters.300 This is often referred to as a two-thirds alphabeti-
cal arrangement, given that most words are triliterals. The earliest author we 
know of to adopt this system is Kurāʿ al-Naml, the last and largest chapter 
of whose al-Munaǧǧad fī l-luġa is alphabetically arranged based on the first 
two letters of the word irrespective of its root.301 Kurāʿ also arranges words 
in his al-Muǧarrad according to their first two letters rather than their roots, 
and does not strictly abide by the third letter although he often does take it 
into account.302 A few authors applied this partial (i.e. two-thirds) alphabeti-
cal arrangement long after the full alphabetical arrangement was adopted in 
works of various genres. One example is Zamaḫšarī (d. 538/1144) in al-Fāʾiq fī 
ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ,303 although in his own muǧannas lexicon, Asās al-balāġa, as 
well as in his lexicon on proverbs, al-Mustaqṣā fī amṯāl al-ʿArab, he follows full 
alphabetical order. For his part, Abū Ḥayyān al-Andalusī (d. 745/1344) adopts 
a two-thirds alphabetical system, but it is based on the first and last radicals 
of the root, in his Tuḥfat al-arīb bi-mā fī l-Qurʾān min al-ġarīb several centu-
ries after Abū ʿUbayd al-Harawī (d. 401/1011) arranged K. al-Ġarībayn fī l-Qurʾān 
wa-l-Ḥadīṯ in full alphabetical order.304 Furthermore, one can describe as a 
three-quarters alphabetical system Muṭarrazī’s (d. 610/1213) arrangement of 
quadriliterals in al-Muġrib fī tartīb al-muʿrib by considering the first, second

which represent the first and second radicals respectively, he arranges the material within 
the chapters by taking the other radicals into account; cf. above, 265–266.
