<!-- page 342 -->

332
chapter 3
and last radicals only (hence the roots DḪRṢ and QRṬQ are placed after DḪS 
and QRF respectively).305
It is thus clear that the use of a partial alphabetical order based on the first 
letter or first two letters and the use of full alphabetical arrangement do not 
represent consecutive stages in the history of mubawwab and specialized 
lexica. In particular, alphabetical arrangement by first letter only is quite com-
mon in several genres. One such genre is proverbs, as exemplified in the previ-
ously discussed works by ʿAskarī (d. after 395/1005), Ibn Rifāʿa (d. after 400/1010), 
Maydānī (d. 518/1124), Ḫuwayyī (d. c. 549/1154) and Bayhaqī (d. 565/1170).306 In 
other genres, the arrangement based solely on the first letter continued till 
much later: it was employed, for example, in the genres of ḫalq al-insān by 
Badr al-Dīn al-Ġazzī (d. 984/1577), muʿarrab by Ḫafāǧī (d. 1069/1659), and ḥurūf 
al-maʿānī by Ibn Nūr al-Dīn (d. 825/1422).307 In tracing the development of the 
alphabetical ordering of words in mubawwab and specialized lexica, it is more 
meaningful to consider each genre on its own, as we did in Chapter 2, than to 
consider the history of lexicographical writing as a whole. Being few in num-
ber, alphabetically arranged muǧannas lexica are considerably easier to exam-
ine from the perspective of the development of their arrangement.
To the following list of alphabetically arranged muǧannas lexica could be 
added Kurāʿ al-Naml’s (d. 310/922) al-Muǧarrad, which would be second in 
chronological order. Having chosen though to discuss it, due to its content, 
under the genre of ġarīb,308 we are left with the following works:
1.	
K. al-Ǧīm by Abū ʿAmr al-Šaybānī (d. 206/821).
2.	
Ǧamharat al-luġa by Ibn Durayd (d. 321/933).
3.	
Maqāyīs al-luġa and Muǧmal al-luġa by Ibn Fāris (d. 395/1004).
4.	
Asās al-balāġa by Zamaḫšarī (d. 538/1144).
3.1	
K. al-Ǧīm by Abū ʿAmr al-Šaybānī (d. 206/821)
This is generally recognized as the first muǧannas lexicon in which lexi-
cal items are alphabetically arranged, though on first-letter basis only. Yet it 
should be remembered that K. al-Ǧīm is not a general lexicon which tries to 
embrace all lexical items of the language since its material is almost restricted 
to ġarīb, as we shall presently see. In this respect, it is similar to Kurāʿ al-
Naml’s (d. 310/922) al-Muǧarrad, which was discussed with books on ġarīb. 
305	
Muṭarrazī, Muġrib 161, 379; cf. 16; see also above, 83.
306	
Cf. above, 113 ff.
307	
Cf. above, 155, 168, 215 respectively.
308	
Cf. above, 90–92.
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 343 -->

333
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
The ­inclusion of K. al-Ǧīm among alphabetically arranged lexica (the rest of 
which attempt to cover the whole language and not only ġarīb) is primarily 
aimed at demonstrating the early appearance of alphabetical ordering in lexi-
cographical writing. In fact, the dates of death of Ḫalīl and Abū ʿAmr may be 
misleading in determining who authored his lexicon first. Abū ʿAmr’s latest 
date given in the sources is 213/828 and he is said to have lived up to the age of 
90, even 119 in the most extreme riwāya.309 Even if we suppose that he died in 
213/828 and was no more than ninety, there still is a chance that he authored K. 
al-Ǧīm at a time very close to that in which Ḫalīl devised the plan of K. al-ʿAyn. 
If, on the other hand, we accept an earlier date of death and a longer life span, 
we may have to conclude that Abū ʿAmr authored his lexicon simultaneously 
with Ḫalīl or even earlier.
Another enigma that surrounds K. al-Ǧīm is its title. Unlike K. al-ʿAyn, which 
begins with the letter ʿayn, it begins with the first letter of the alphabet, alif, 
rather than ǧīm as its title may suggest, and does not include an introduction 
in which Abū ʿAmr, like Ḫalīl, may have cited the reason for his lexicon’s title, 
if he indeed gave it that title and not one of the other titles by which the book 
is referred to (see below). Medieval scholars were puzzled by the title,310 and 
Qifṭī (d. 646/1248) reports that Ibn al-Qaṭṭāʿ (d. 515/1121), author of K. al-Afʿāl,311 
had an explanation for the name, but since no one was willing to pay the hun-
dred dinars he had requested to give away the secret, he took it with him to 
his grave.312 Qifṭī then says that, having heard of Ibn al-Qaṭṭāʿ’s anecdote, he 
decided to search for the correct meaning of the title, and when he found 
the answer, he requested only ten dinars to reveal the truth, but found no 
one whose love for knowledge would prompt him to pay even that amount. 
Unfortunately, Qifṭī does not reveal the truth to his readers either!
Fīrūzābādī (d. 817/1415) tries to explain this enigmatic title by attributing to 
Abū ʿAmr that ǧīm means dībāǧ (decorated garment) and suggesting that he 
wanted the title to be indicative of the book’s fineness (ka⁠ʾannahu šabbahahu 
bi-l-dībāǧ li-ḥusnihi).313 Among contemporary scholars, Haywood suggests 
that the work was called or known as Alif bāʾ ǧīm (The A-B-C Book), but the 
title was then abbreviated.314 More convincing is Naṣṣār’s proposal that the

<!-- page 344 -->

334
chapter 3
letter ǧīm is often associated with ġarīb usage, and hence Abū ʿAmr may have 
called the whole book K. al-Ǧīm to indicate its focus on ġarīb, in a manner 
similar to the present day reference to Arabic as a whole by the expression 
luġat al-ḍād (language of the letter ḍād).315 Naṣṣār also notes that Ibn Fāris 
(d. 395/1004) curiously points out at the beginning of the chapter of ǧīm, and 
not any other letter, in Muǧmal al-luġa that he included in it usage which he 
refers to as clear (al-wāḍiḥ) and correct (al-ṣaḥīḥ) to the exception of what is 
uncouth and objectionable (al-waḥšī al-mustankar).316 Moreover, Naṣṣār cites 
Abū l-ʿAlāʾ al-Maʿarrī’s (d. 449/1057) assertion that he excluded ǧīm, ḥāʾ and 
what he described as similar letters from his book Sayf al-ḫuṭab because he 
wanted the collection of orations it embraces to be restricted to what is simple 
and easy ( yanbaġī an yakūn saǧīḥan sahlan).317 It is also noteworthy that in 
the raǧaz meter, which is closely associated with ġarīb material,318 ǧīm is used 
in the qāfiya (rhyme) much more frequently than in other meters. For example, 
the number of raǧaz verses with ǧīm in their qāfiya in Ibn Durayd’s Ǧamharat 
al-luġa is 129, whereas it occurs in all other meters together no more than 39 
times.319 But whether Abū ʿAmr indeed wanted the title of his lexicon to reflect 
the ġarīb nature of its material is a matter that we will probably never know for 
certain. This also applies to another book by the same title attributed to Abū 
ʿAmr’s contemporary al-Naḍr b. Šumayl (d. 203/819).320 A third book by the 
title K. al-Ǧīm was authored by Šamir b. Ḥamdawayhi al-Harawī (d. 255/869) 
and, according to Azharī (d. 370/981), began with the letter ǧīm.321 It should be 
noted, however, that Azharī did not see Šamir’s book and that his description 
was based on oral reports. Accordingly, we cannot be absolutely sure by which 
letter Šamir’s lexicon began.
In some sources, K. al-Ǧīm is said to have also been known by other titles. 
Ibn al-Nadīm (d. 380/990) refers to it as K. al-Nawādir,322 but it seems that 
this was an independent work.323 The two other titles by which K. al-Ǧīm is

<!-- page 345 -->

335
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
also referred to are K. al-Ḥurūf and K. al-Luġāt.324 Yet we know from Ṣaġānī 
(d. 650/1259) that the former is an independent work since he says that he used 
it, as well as K. al-Ǧīm, in compiling al-Takmila wa-l-ḏayl wa-l-ṣila.325 As for 
K. al-Luġāt, it would hardly be surprising that it could have been an alternative 
title to K. al-Ǧīm given that the latter contains a vast amount of ġarīb and nādir 
data, which often reflect dialectal usage. As one of the earliest Kufan philolo-
gists, Abū ʿAmr was primarily interested in dialectal material and poetry of the 
various tribes. It was previously mentioned in our discussion of the process of 
data collection (ǧamʿ al-luġa) that Abū ʿAmr is reported to have depleted two 
large vessels of ink in recording data taken directly from the Bedouins.326 He is 
also reported by his son ʿAmr to have collected the poetry of more than eighty 
tribes,327 and these seem to have been his major source for data in al-Ǧīm.
al-Ǧīm faithfully reflects Abū ʿAmr’s vast knowledge of ġarīb material, 
particularly in poetry. Although he hardly mentions any contemporary phi-
lologist, his number of ruwāt exceeds 130,328 some of whom are among the 
Bedouin fuṣaḥāʾ mentioned in the sources,329 e.g. Abū l-Ǧarrāḥ al-ʿUqaylī and 
Abū Ziyād al-Kilābī.330 These ruwāt are often referred to by the names of their 
tribes, e.g. al-Asadī, al-Ṭāʾī, al-Muḥāribī, al-Kalbī, al-Ḫuzāʿī, al-ʿUḏrī, al-Bakrī, 
al-Taġlibī, al-Saʿdī, etc., and Abū ʿAmr most probably does not cite their riwāya 
as representative of the dialects of their tribes since he does refer elsewhere 
to tribal dialects in no unclear terms (cf. the expressions: taqūl Banū Asad; 
fī kalām Banī Šaybān; ahl al-Yamāma yusammūnahu; Huḏayl taqūl, and luġa 
li-Ṭayyiʾ).331 Other than the ruwāt, more than 300 poets are quoted by Abū ʿAmr, 
and it is highly significant that he frequently attributes the šawāhid to their 
renderers. The number of ascribed šawāhid is almost 1,900 out of a total of 
about 4,300 lines332 – a very high proportion indeed given that the practice 
of ascribing šawāhid was not very common among early philologists. The most 
likely reason for Abū ʿAmr’s ascription of šawāhid to their renderers is that he 
collected his ġarīb material directly from their dīwāns. This is supported by the 
fact that the order of the šawāhid pertaining to a certain poet often coincides

<!-- page 346 -->

336
chapter 3
with their order in his dīwān. In the case of the pre-Islamic poet Labīd, Diem 
observes that K. al-Ǧīm contains 123 lines of his, the order of which agrees with 
that in his dīwān.333 Given Abū ʿAmr’s interest in ġarīb, it is not surprising that 
a sizeable number of his poetry šawāhid are of the raǧaz meter (971 versus 
2,291 verses in all other meters). That Abū ʿAmr authored his lexicon by extract-
ing ġarīb material from the dīwāns at his disposal is further supported by the 
surprisingly few šawāhid from other genres, in fact only two Qurʾānic verses, 
one ḥadīṯ,334 and fifty-one proverbs.335
Abū ʿAmr was evidently more interested in citing ġarīb usage than explain-
ing it. His definitions are characteristically brief and often restricted to the 
ġarīb word at hand without reference to derivatives which share its root. In 
line with works which focus on ġarīb and nawādir, he often lists synonyms, all 
or most of which are only rarely attested,336 as in al-muǧlaḫidd wa-l-muǧlaʿibb 
wa-l-muḍǧaḥirr wa-l-muslaḥibb wa-l-muṣlaḫidd: al-muḍṭaǧiʿ (one who lies 
down); al-ṣaʿīd wa-l-ḥiṣḥiṣ wal-l-qaḍḍ wa-l-aṯlab wa-l-hayām wa-l-daqʿāʾ: kull 
ḏālika min al-turāb (synonyms of sand).337 On many occasions, he only cites 
a line of poetry without any definition of the ġarīb word it contains.338 This 
demonstrates that his primary purpose is to illustrate the existence of words 
rather than define their meanings, as is sometimes the case in books on ġarīb, 
and does not imply – as Diem suggests339 –  that K. al-Ǧīm as we know it is an 
unfinished draft which the author wanted to improve later. Abū ʿAmr also often 
cites ġarīb words as part of expressions which clarify their meanings, rather 
than merely mention the targeted words; e.g. istuḥǧira and zumna respectively 
in istuḥǧira ʿalayhi fa-lam yatakallam (He tried to speak but could not) and mā 
ra⁠ʾaytuhu munḏu zumnatin (I have not seen him for long).340
Abū ʿAmr’s definitions are normally so brief and confined to the word under 
consideration that one cannot speak of an internal arrangement of material in 
each lemma. The order of the lexical items which share the first letter in each of 
the lexicon’s twenty-eight chapters is largely a result of the fixed order in which 
Abū ʿAmr systematically quotes the dīwāns at his disposal.341 Some lexical

<!-- page 347 -->

337
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
items are also quoted probably based on their association with other items. For 
example, ḥarmā (desiring the male, said of a she-goat) is mentioned immedi-
ately after ḥamḥama, which indicates the uttering of a cry by a he-goat excited 
by lust.342 At times, the mention of a certain lexical item leads to the citing 
of another which shares the same root, as in ḥariǧ (confined, closed), which 
immediately follows ḥirāǧ (woods).343 Furthermore, the lexical items are nor-
mally cited in their forms as they appear in the lines of poetry which Abū ʿAmr 
quotes. Hence these items can be either nouns (singular or plural, masculine 
or feminine) or verbs (past, present or even imperative).344 Abū ʿAmr also cites 
augmented words either according to their roots (e.g. tamaṣṣaḥa under m) or 
the order of their letters including the augments (e.g. iblawlā under ʾ).345 Yet 
some words are not subjected to either criterion of arrangement. For example, 
mutamahhil, which one would expect to be under m either because that letter 
is its prefix or its first root, is oddly placed under its infix, t.346 Another discrep-
ancy is that some roots appear in several places within the same chapter, as 
in the root KMḤ, which is cited in seven different locations under the letter k, 
i.e. akmaḥtuhu, kawmaḥ, takmīḥ, kawmaḥ (for a second time), mukmiḥ, ikmāḥ 
and kamaḥ.347 It is also noteworthy that the order of the last three chapters is 
w, h, y – an order that is also used in some later lexica, e.g. Zamaḫšarī’s (538/1144) 
Asās al-balāġa, whereas others use the order h, w, y, e.g. Ibn Fāris’s (d. 395/1004) 
Muǧmal al-luġa.
Despite their contemporaneity, the difference between K. al-Ǧīm and 
K. al-ʿAyn is indeed remarkable: whereas the latter is based on a system that 
exhausts all the theoretically possible roots of the language, Abū ʿAmr’s book 
was clearly not exhaustive; rather, it focused on ġarīb material found in the 
dīwāns and in various oral riwāyas. This selective nature of K. al-Ǧīm is duly 
noted by Muḥammad b. al-Ḥusayn al-Yamanī (d. 400/1010), who is reported 
to have said that Abū ʿAmr had collected in it uncouth lexical items (al-waḥšī) 
and had not included what was actually used (al-mustaʿmal).348 Furthermore, 
K. al-ʿAyn, in spite of its difficult arrangement, lists roots according to a specific 
order based on phonetic arrangement, number of radicals, and taqālīb, and

<!-- page 348 -->

338
chapter 3
hence these roots are placed exactly where they are expected to be. On the 
other hand, despite the ease of Abū ʿAmr’s alphabetical arrangement, the fact 
that words in his lexicon are arranged solely by their first letters makes it con-
siderably more difficult for the reader to locate the required word therein than 
in al-ʿAyn. Finally, the impact of Abū ʿAmr’s lexicon is nowhere close to that 
of al-ʿAyn’s. Although the major dictionaries and other philological works do 
include quotations from it (more often by mentioning Abū ʿAmr than the lexi-
con’s title),349 its influence on the tradition as a whole is quite limited, perhaps 
in line with the limited scope of its material.
3.2	
Ǧamharat al-luġa by Ibn Durayd (d. 321/933)
The fourth/tenth century witnessed the appearance of more muǧannas lexica 
than any other century. The first of these is Ǧamharat al-luġa, a monumental 
work whose content Ibn Durayd – an Iraqi scholar of Omani descent – dictated 
to his disciple Ismāʿīl b. ʿAbdallāh b. Muḥammad b. Mīkāl (d. 362/972), as he 
mentions in the introduction.350 According to one riwāya, Ibn Durayd dictated 
the whole book in Persia to Ibn Mīkāl in 297/910,351 although it is difficult to 
imagine that a voluminous work such as al-Ǧamhara could be dictated within 
a year. He also is reported to have later dictated it in Basra and Baghdad.352 
If we exclude Šaybānī’s (d. 206/821) K. al-Ǧīm and Bandanīǧī’s (d. 284/897) al-
Taqfiya on the basis that they are not intended to include all possible roots 
of the language, we will be left with al-Ǧamhara as the first large-scale and 
exhaustive lexicon after Ḫalīl’s (d. 175/791) K. al-ʿAyn. This is perhaps one of the 
reasons why al-Ǧamhara is often compared with al-ʿAyn – a subject to which 
we shall return. At the formal level, al-Ǧamhara has a unique characteristic 
among all muǧannas lexica: it is alphabetically arranged and, at the same time, 
retains Ḫalīl’s system of taqālīb. al-Ǧamhara’s unique arrangement may thus 
be referred to as alphabetical-permutative, though it is more feasible to list it 
under the alphabetical system than to place it in a category of its own. It has 
previously been pointed out that Ibn Durayd justifies using the alphabetical 
order by reference to its being known both to the ʿāmma and ḫāṣṣa and its rela-
tive ease in comparison with Ḫalīl’s phonetic arrangement.353
349	
For quotations from K. al-Ǧīm in other works, see Diem (1968: 92–117).

by more than forty years, was born in 270/883 and was thus about twenty-seven years old 
when Ibn Durayd dictated al-Ǧamhara to him.
352	
Yāqūt, Muʿǧam VI, 2492; Suyūṭī, Muzhir I, 94 and Buġya I, 77.
353

<!-- page 349 -->

339
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
The lexicon is titled al-Ǧamhara, as Ibn Durayd explains, because in it is 
recorded the ǧumhūr (i.e. the main body, the bulk) of the speech of the Arabs 
to the exclusion of what is uncouth or barbarous (waḥšī) and objectionable 
(mustankar).354 As previously mentioned,355 Ibn Durayd’s position reflects his 
awareness of the fact that the user needs to learn about words that are in actual 
circulation, rather than words of the ġarīb type that are only rarely attested in 
the speech of some Bedouin fuṣaḥāʾ or in an obscure line of poetry. In applica-
tion, however, al-Ǧamhara is rife with lexical items that can be depicted as rare, 
obsolete or of dubious meaning. Azharī (d. 370/981) criticizes Ibn Durayd from 
this perspective by citing words which are nowhere to be found other than in 
al-Ǧamhara. Such words are most likely to be the reason why he accuses Ibn 
Durayd in al-Tahḏīb’s introduction of falsifying Arabic and introducing neolo-
gisms (iftiʿāl al-ʿArabiyya wa-tawlīd al-alfāẓ),356 although he frequently quotes 
him without criticism. Quadriliterals and quinqueliterals of questionable 
authenticity in al-Ǧamhara, according to Azharī, include ḥardama, ḥurqūf, 
ḥubaqbīq and qalaḥdam.357 However, Ibn Durayd himself expresses his own 
doubt about the authenticity of some lexical items which he cites, often by 
expressions such as laysa bi-ṯabt (not certain or established) or lā aḥuqquhu/
lā adrī mā ṣiḥḥatuhu (I cannot confirm its correctness).358 So frequent are 
these expressions that Suyūṭī (d. 911/1505) cites more than seventy examples in 
which Ibn Durayd uses them to express caution about reported material.359 It 
is significant that some of the words in question in these examples are Yamānī360 
since Ibn Durayd’s frequent citing of the Yamānī dialect (217 times in all, more 
than any other dialect)361 as well as other non-standard dialects, such as Ša⁠ʾāmī 
and Azdī dialects,362 may have been among the reasons for accusing him of 
including items whose authenticity is questionable. The inclusion of these dia-
lects and of words which Azharī describes as unique to al-Ǧamhara is probably 
due to the vastness of the material that Ibn Durayd amassed, so much so that

<!-- page 350 -->

340
chapter 3
he was described as having been the most knowledgeable Basran scholar in 
his time and as having been rivaled only by Ḫalaf al-Aḥmar (d. 180/796) in the 
vastness of poetry riwāya.363 Furthermore, the disproportionately high ratio 
of raǧaz – known for including ġarīb words364 – to šiʿr (i.e. poetry other than 
raǧaz)365 is further proof that Ibn Durayd’s promise to exclude unfamiliar or 
anomalous material was largely unfulfilled.
The introduction of Ibn Durayd starts with a mention of dictating 
al-Ǧamhara to his disciple Ibn Mīkāl, his complaint of the ignorance and inca-
pacity of the people in his time, and a brief outline of the divisions of the lexi-
con. The rest of the introduction is almost fully devoted to matters related to 
phonology and morphology. Much of the material is inspired by Ḫalīl’s intro-
duction to al-ʿAyn and deals with the phonetic characteristics of letters, their 
points of articulation, and the various morphological patterns. But in spite of 
the similarity in topics, the introductions of the two lexica are very different 
in significance: whereas Ḫalīl’s is organically linked to the lexicon’s plan and 
content (cf. for example the relationship between points of articulation and 
the phonetic arrangement of letters), Ibn Durayd’s is largely detached from the 
lexicon itself, particularly in dwelling on the phonetic characteristics of letters, 
whereas the main body of the lexicon is alphabetically arranged. Beginning his 
introduction by praising al-ʿAyn as the reflection of Ḫalīl’s profound intellect,366 
Ibn Durayd seems to have wanted to pay tribute to his predecessor by incor-
porating into his own introduction much of the material in Ḫalīl’s, to which 
he adds some dialectal usage and poetry šawāhid. An interesting and possibly 
original item which Ibn Durayd alludes to in his introduction is the frequency 
of the occurrence of some phonemes in the language, although he furnishes 
no evidence for the partial list that he provides. According to him, w, y, and ʾ are 
the most frequent, whereas the least frequent due to their heaviness (ṯiqal) are 
ẓ, ḏ, ṯ, š, q, ḫ, ʿ, ġ, n, l, r, b, and m (gradually arranged starting with the most infre-
quent, i.e. ẓ).367 Also noteworthy is that the book’s epilogue consists primar-
ily of the method of calculating the theoretically possible roots, and includes 
a representation of radicals as points on a circular diagram.368 In biliterals, 
the number is 784, being the result of multiplying the number of letters, i.e. 
twenty-eight, by itself. The multiplication formula in triliterals yields 15,625 
363

<!-- page 351 -->

341
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
potential roots, and many more in quadriliterals (where the elements of the 
multiplication are given but not its result) and quinqueliterals (where no num-
bers are cited).369
In the division of his lexicon into chapters, Ibn Durayd makes a significant 
departure from Ḫalīl’s method. In al-ʿAyn, the main divisions are based on the 
letters of the alphabet, hence the first chapter is ʿayn, the second ḥāʾ, the third 
hāʾ, and so on. Each chapter is further divided into sections based on the length 
of the root (i.e. biliterals, triliterals, quadriliterals and quinqueliterals). Ibn 
Durayd reverses this arrangement by dividing the material first according to 
root length and then according to alphabetical order. The first major division 
thus begins with biliterals, followed by triliterals, etc. Alphabetical ordering 
is then independently applied in each subdivision (for which see below). For 
example, the section on sound biliterals (al-ṯulāṯī al-ṣaḥīḥ) begins with BTṮ, 
BTǦ, BTḤ (each of which is attested in one combination only, i.e. ṮBT, ǦBT and 
BḤT respectively), BTḪ (attested in ḪBT and BḪT), BTR (attested, in addition 
to BTR, in TBR, BRT, RTB and TRB).370 Although this arrangement is more com-
plex than Ḫalīl’s, it has (despite some irregularities to be discussed later) the 
advantage of listing in the same division all roots that have the same number 
of radicals (i.e. biliterals, triliterals, etc.), rather than having them distributed 
over twenty-eight chapters.
A brief outline of the major divisions of al-Ǧamhara would clearly dem-
onstrate the complexity of its arrangement. The lexicon’s main sections are: 
(1) sound biliterals (I, 53), by which Ibn Durayd refers to roots whose second and 
third radicals are geminated, e.g. BTT (i.e. batta “to cut”); (2) biliterals appended 
to reduplicated quadriliterals (I, 173), e.g. BṮBṮ, followed by hamzated roots 
of the same type (I, 226), e.g. BʾBʾ; (3) unsound biliterals (I, 229), e.g. ṮWY 
(more generally referred to by morphologists as lafīf ) and unsound hamzated 
forms, e.g. BWʾ and ʾWB; (4) sound triliterals (I, 252), which occupy more than 
half of the whole lexicon; (5) triliterals whose second and third radicals are 
alike but not geminated as in Section 1 (II, 999), e.g. BLL (i.e. balal “wetness”); 
(6) triliterals whose middle radical is a vowel (i.e. aǧwaf) or contain two similar 
radicals (II, 1015), e.g. BWB and BYB; (7) triliterals with w, ʾ, y (II, 1016), e.g. ǦBY; 
(8) rare hamzated words (II, 1086), e.g. anata (to groan) and basa⁠ʾa (to like the 
company of); (9) sound quadriliterals (II, 1110), e.g. BḤTR; (10) unsound quadri-
literals (II, 1162), a misleading title under which are listed not only quadriliter-
als which contain y or w, e.g. ĠYṬL and KWMḤ but also quadriliterals which 
have two similar radicals, e.g. DRDQ, and triliterals whose median or final roots 
369	
Ibn Durayd, Ǧamhara III, 1338–1339.
370	
Ibid., I, 252–253.

<!-- page 352 -->

342
chapter 3
are geminated, e.g. dummal (furuncle) and qumudd (tall); (11) quinqueliterals 
(II, 1184), e.g. ʿarandal (tall), as well as words that contain five radicals plus an 
augment (i.e. long vowel), e.g. ʿandalīb (nightingale); and (12) miscellaneous 
topics (III, 1127–1337).
The complexity of this arrangement is far more than that in K. al-ʿAyn, or 
indeed any other muǧannas lexicon, be it of the phonetic-permutative, alpha-
betical or rhyme systems. In particular, the biliterals and triliterals are con-
fusingly divided into subsections based on morphological features, and each 
subsection has its independent alphabetical arrangement. The following 
observations exemplify the inconsistencies characteristic of the lexicon as a 
whole: (1) that there is no distinction between ʾ, w, y in unsound biliterals and 
triliterals, e.g. the roots B+ʾ/W/Y are listed together, as are the roots BT+W/ʾ/Y;371 
(2) that the section of sound triliterals includes some unsound triliterals, e.g. 
ḤBW, a root which also occurs under triliterals with w, ʾ, y;372 (3) that in several 
sections, the first letter of the alphabet is b, whereas roots which begin with 
hamza are mentioned in the distant section devoted to rare hamzated words, 
e.g. the section of biliterals appended to reduplicated quadriliterals begins 
with BTBT, rather than BʾBʾ, which occurs much later;373 (4) that words in the 
section of unsound quadriliterals and that of quinqueliterals are arranged not 
according to their alphabetical order, but by their patterns, e.g. fiyaʿl, fuʿalil, 
faʿallal, faʿaylal, etc.;374 (5) that words of triliteral roots but which end with 
the feminine marker ˗h are often listed with quadriliterals, e.g. ǧaḥma (eye);375 
(6) that quinqueliterals include lexical items whose patterns are not normally 
considered to have five roots, e.g. qindīl (lamp) and širrīr (vicious);376 and 
(7) that roots, particularly unsound, hamzated and reduplicated ones, often 
appear in several parts of the lexicon. On the last point, the root ʾBY, for 
example, is listed under unsound biliterals, unsound triliterals which include 
w, ʾ, or y, and under rare hamzated words.377 It also occurs in four other dispa-
rate locations: in the section of faʿaltu and afʿaltu, twice with dialectal ­material

Ibid., I, 229; II, 1016.
372	
Ibid., I, 286; II, 1017.
373	
Ibid., I, 173; II, 1107.
374	
Ibid., II, 1165, 1167, 1184, 1187.
375	
This feature, in spite of Ibn Ǧinnī’s claim that Ibn Durayd was ignorant of morphology 
(Ḫaṣāʾiṣ III, 288; cf. Suyūṭī, Muzhir I, 93), is justifiable since, as Ibn Durayd explains, such 
words have no masculine, and hence their feminine endings are effectively inseparable 
letters (ka-l-ḥarf al-lāzim). Cf. Ǧamhara II, 135; a similar justification is also cited in II, 893, 
1124, 1140, 1142.
376	
Ibid., II, 1190, 1192.
377

<!-- page 353 -->

343
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
attributed to Abū Zayd (d. 215/834), and in the section on plural forms.378 
Moreover, words of that root are accidently mentioned and explained on two 
other occasions, that is, under other lemmas.379 In fact, Ibn Durayd himself 
apologizes for the error of repetition (takrīr), but justifies it by the fact that the 
lexicon was dictated from memory.380
Several miscellaneous topics curiously occur following the main sec-
tions (i.e. after the quinqueliterals). Among these are certain morphological 
patterns, itbāʿ (alliteration), qalb (metathesis), faʿaltu and afʿaltu, dialectal 
features, nawādir (rare usage), pre-Islamic words, Arabized words, plurals, 
diminutives, etc., as well as a number of specific semantic fields, including 
food, bows and arrows, blades, and footwear. The inclusion of these topics 
clearly demonstrates Ibn Durayd’s conviction that the muǧannas model does 
not annul the need for individual topics that are presented in monograph form 
of the mubawwab type. In other words, alphabetical arrangement by defini-
tion causes lexical items that belong to a certain semantic field to be listed in 
disparate places of the lexicon, unlike mabawwab lexica which group together 
related lexical items. In fact, the section of unsound quadriliterals and that of 
quinqueliterals in the main body of the lexicon already exhibit Ibn Durayd’s 
inclination towards the abandonment of alphabetical arrangement in favor of 
grouping together words that share the same pattern, in the manner we know 
in some books on abniya discussed in Section 10 of the previous chapter. For 
example, grouped together are quadriliterals that contain two similar letters 
or are of the patterns fiʿall, fiʿill, fuʿull, fuʿʿal, fayʿal, fawʿal, fuʿālā, etc., as well as 
quinqueliterals of the patterns faʿanlal, faʿallal, faʿaylal, fiʿlīl, fiʿʿīl, ufʿūl, ufʿūla, 
yafʿūl, etc.381 These subsections, like the miscellaneous topics, do not follow 
any alphabetical arrangement and their inclusion in the book undermines its 
character as a muǧannas lexicon just as the inclusion of the miscellaneous top-
ics is inconsistent with that character.
Like most previously discussed muǧannas lexica, the internal arrangement 
in each lemma follows no specific system. Naṣṣār claims that Ḫalīl more con-
sistently than Ibn Durayd cites plural forms, arranges the perfect, imperfect 
and verbal noun(s) in that order, places the intransitive before the transitive, 
and highlights the derivational links between forms.382 A random comparison 
between a few entries, however, would readily invalidate this proposal, as there

<!-- page 354 -->

344
chapter 3
are entries in which Ḫalīl abides more than Ibn Durayd by these criteria, and 
vice versa. Yet the contents of a lemma in K. al-ʿAyn are normally not repeated 
in any other part of the lexicon, whereas the contents of a large number of lem-
mata in al-Ǧamhara are repeated in several places, often with little or no new 
material or šawāhid.
al-Ǧamhara is replete with šawāhid,383 including 503 Qurʾānic quotations, 
508 ḥadīṯs and aṯars, and 315 proverbs. The largest portion of šawāhid though 
comes from poetry, a total of 5,605 verses, out of which 2,603 are in the raǧaz 
meter alone. The poetry šawāhid (including raǧaz) in al-Ǧamhara differ sig-
nificantly from those in K. al-ʿAyn. For example, there are 630 šawāhid in the 
section of sound biliterals, out of which only 58 occur in K. al-ʿAyn, which in 
turn contains numerous šawāhid not found in al-Ǧamhara or indeed any other 
source.384 Ibn Durayd certainly had several earlier works at his disposal, and 
although only K. al-ʿAyn is mentioned in the introduction, a number of other 
works are cited in the body of the lexicon.385 Among Ibn Durayd’s most prom-
inent sources is Abū ʿUbayda’s (d. 209/824) Maǧāz al-Qurʾān, which is cited 
only twice by name,386 but much of whose material is quoted by Ibn Durayd 
and ascribed to Abū ʿUbayda without reference to the book itself.387 Similarly, 
Ušnāndānī’s (d. 288/900) Maʿānī l-šiʿr – which reached us through Ibn Durayd’s 
riwāya – is mentioned only once,388 but its author is quoted 31 times. Moreover, 
Ibn Durayd is said to have dictated his book from memory and only consulted 
works at his disposal before the sections on hamz and lafīf.389 Abū Zayd’s 
(d. 215/830) K. al-Hamz is mentioned twice by Ibn Durayd,390 but his use of its 
material is obvious in several quotations in which its author, rather than title, 
is mentioned.
Among the other features of al-Ǧamhara is its author’s interest in Arabic 
dialects,391 more specifically, Yamānī, Ša⁠ʾāmī and Azdī dialects as previ-
ously mentioned. Related to dialectal usage are the ninety references made 
to solecism or laḥn al-ʿāmma.392 Moreover, Ibn Durayd’s interest in muʿarrab

<!-- page 355 -->

345
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
is ­evident in the two sections he devotes to Arabized words,393 as well as in 
related comments spread over the whole book.394 Another area of inter-
est for Ibn Durayd is the etymology of proper nouns. Having authored his 
K. al-Ištiqāq,395 to which he refers fourteen times in al-Ǧamhara, he very fre-
quently mentions in the latter proper nouns in their relevant lemmata and 
often cites their ­meanings.396 Accordingly, many of his etymologies of proper 
nouns are quoted in subsequent lexica. As far as definitions are concerned, 
they are mostly brief and frequently supported by šawāhid, which in turn may 
contain words that Ibn Durayd often digresses to explain. As a result, there are 
hundreds of words which are defined not only in the relevant lemmata but 
also in unrelated ones.397 It should also be noted that al-Ǧamhara is one of the 
earliest lexica that often substitute the definition of a lexical item by indicating 
that its meaning is well-known (maʿrūf). This is applicable to place names (e.g. 
Isbīl mawḍiʿ maʿrūf; Kašab ǧabal maʿrūf; Ǧayḥān nahr maʿrūf )398 as well as to 
general items (e.g. alyat al-šāt, al-zubd, al-ḏahab, al-sarab, rikāb al-sarǧ, niṣāb 
al-sikkīn).399
The positions of several subsequent authors of muǧannas lexica vis-à-vis 
al-Ǧamhara have already been alluded to. Ibn Durayd’s own student, Abū ʿAlī 
al-Qālī (d. 356/967), only sparingly quoted him in al-Bāriʿ, although he had 
no reservations about quoting him extensively in literary matters in al-Amālī; 
Azharī (d. 370/981), on the other hand, questioned his reliability and the 
authenticity of certain lexical items which are found only in al-Ǧamhara, 
although he frequently quoted him without any criticism; al-Ṣāḥib b. ʿAbbād 
(d. 385/995) quoted him only six times in al-Muḥīṭ, perhaps because he had 
abridged al-Ǧamhara in a separate work known as Ǧawharat al-Ǧamhara; 
and Ibn Sīda (d. 458/1066) specifically mentioned al-Ǧamhara in the intro-
duction of al-Muḥkam and incorporated most of its material into it.400 A few 
decades after Ibn Durayd, al-Ǧamhara was one of two muǧannas lexica (the 
other being K. al-ʿAyn) among the five works which Ibn Fāris (d. 395/1004) 
primarily relied on.401 It is most probably to these two lexica that Ibn Fāris

<!-- page 356 -->

346
chapter 3
frequently refers as al-kitābayn,402 as if to indicate that these two works in par-
ticular are his main source of data, although he is critical of both in certain 
cases.403 Among later authors, Ṣaġānī (d. 650/1252) in al-ʿUbāb al-zāḫir heav-
ily depended on Ibn Durayd. For example, in the seventy pages of which the 
letter ġ consists, he is mentioned forty-two times.404 As far as Lisān al-ʿArab 
is concerned, al-Ǧamhara is not one of the five sources which Ibn Manẓūr 
(d. 711/1311) incorporated into it, but his frequent references to Ibn Durayd 
(637 in all)405 seem to have caused Ibn Ḥaǧar al-ʿAsqalānī (d. 852/1449), and 
even a philologist like Suyūṭī (d. 911/1505) and a lexicographer like Zabīdī 
(d. 1205/1790), to include it with the sources from which the Lisān is derived.406 
Furthermore, other than Ǧawharat al-Ǧamhara mentioned above, there are 
several works which either abridge al-Ǧamhara, complement it, or explain its 
šawāhid.407
Nifṭawayhi’s (d. 322/935) biased depiction of al-Ǧamhara as a plagiarized, 
yet modified, version of K. al-ʿAyn408 certainly reflected negatively on Ibn 
Durayd’s credibility. Even some contemporary authors judge al-Ǧamhara 
in light of Nifṭawayhi’s accusation. A. M. Šākir, for example, asserts that it is 
extracted (muqtabas) from K. al-ʿAyn,409 and M. Abū l-Faraǧ concludes that 
Nifṭawayhi’s satire proves the role of imitation (taqlīd) in Arabic lexicography.410 
Yet close study of the two works shows that, in spite of some similarities (par-
ticularly in listing words based on root length and taqālīb), the two works are 
quite different, most obviously in the order of letters and in the primary divi-
sion according to the letters in K. al-ʿAyn and to root length in al-Ǧamhara. 
Perhaps more important, as was argued in detail elsewhere,411 is the fact that 
K. al-ʿAyn represents the stage of discovery in the lexicographical tradition; 
hence the theory stipulated that each root had to appear in a specific loca-
tion, and the phonetic system proposed by Ḫalīl in the introduction had to be

<!-- page 357 -->

347
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
implemented in the body of the lexicon. Unfortunately, Ibn Durayd did not 
try to introduce improvements to the elements of Ḫalīl’s theory (apart from 
abandoning the phonetic ordering of letters); on the contrary, his divisions 
are more complex, roots may appear in several disparate locations, and the 
phonetic study in the introduction is totally irrelevant to the book’s arrange-
ment. Unconcerned with laying the foundations of a lexicographical theory or 
with developing Ḫalīl’s in any meaningful way, Ibn Durayd applied himself to 
expanding the material of his lexicon in comparison with K. al-ʿAyn, particu-
larly in the šawāhid and the miscellaneous topics appended to its main body. 
Most certainly, Ibn Durayd praises K. al-ʿAyn in the introduction of al-Ǧamhara 
and quotes Ḫalīl sixty-seven times in the text.412 Yet he relies more on mate-
rial derived from philologists such as Aṣmaʿī, Abū Ḥātim, Abū Zayd and Abū 
ʿUbayda,413 and there are instances in which he sounds his disagreement with 
Ḫalīl’s views or definitions.414 In short, not only does al-Ǧamhara offer the user 
material not found in K. al-ʿAyn – as is obvious in Ibn al-Tayyānī’s (d. 436/1044) 
al-Mūʿab, which derives its material from both lexica415 – but its independent 
“character” is manifest in almost every detail.
3.3	
Maqāyīs al-luġa and Muǧmal al-luġa by Ibn Fāris (d. 395/1004)
Ibn Fāris is one of the most famous and prolific philologists of the fourth/
tenth century, among whose students are al-Ṣāḥib b. ʿAbbād (d. 385/995) and 
Badīʿ al-Zamān al-Hamaḏānī (d. 398/1008). Several works of his were discussed 
in Chapter 2, but his most famous work (which falls outside our scope) is 
al-Ṣāḥibī fī fiqh al-luġa wa-sunan al-ʿArab fī kalāmihā, a pioneering work that 
discusses, among other topics, the fundamental notions and principles of 
philological study. Although Muǧmal al-luġa is the more famous of Ibn Fāris’s 
two muǧannas lexica,416 Ibn Fāris’s real contribution to lexicography is bet-
ter represented by al-Maqāyīs, and it will hence be discussed first. al-Maqāyīs, 
unlike al-Muǧmal, is the product of Ibn Fāris’s application of his philological 
views about uṣūl (basic meanings) and naḥt (blending, i.e. coining a new word 
from two or more words, or by adding an affix to a root), and thus is a more 
­theoretically-based approach to lexicographical writing than al-Muǧmal.
412	
See Ǧamhara’s indices, III, 1521.
413	
Ibid., III, 1511, 1517, 1526, 1532.
414	
E.g. ibid., II, 645, 651, 696, 887, 937; cf. Baalbaki (1998: 53–57).
415	
See above, 263–264.
416	
Note, for example, that al-Maqāyīs neither features in Suyūṭī’s survery of lexica in 
al-Muzhir nor is mentioned anywhere else in that book, contrary to al-Muǧmal; cf. Muzhir 
I, 99–100 and index, II, 648.

<!-- page 358 -->

348
chapter 3
In the introduction of al-Maqāyīs, Ibn Fāris expresses his great admiration 
of Ḫalīl’s K. al-ʿAyn, and attributes to it the highest status among his sources. 
Ḫalīl’s influence is obvious in the division of al-Maqāyīs into twenty-eight 
chapters (albeit alphabetically from alif to yāʾ), each of which is in turn divided 
into sections based on root length. This is in contrast to al-Ǧamhara – the 
other muǧannas lexicon that Ibn Fāris lists among his sources417 – in which 
Ibn Durayd arranges the material first on the basis of root length and then 
alphabetical order. On the other hand, Ibn Fāris shares Ibn Durayd’s alphabeti-
cal arrangement (i.e. ʾ, b, t, ṯ, etc.) in preference to Ḫalīl’s phonetic one. Yet Ibn 
Fāris’s originality is obvious in the division of material as well as in alphabeti-
cal arrangement. With respect to root length, his divisions are much simpler 
than Ḫalīl’s418 since they include (a) biliterals (which embrace, but within the 
same lemma, geminate roots, such as BḤḤ, and reduplicated ones, such as 
BḤBḤ),419 (b) triliterals, and (c) quadriliterals and quinqueliterals together. No 
other subdivisions, such as sound, unsound or hamzated roots, are observed. 
As for the alphabetical order of letters which he shares with Ibn Durayd and 
others, Ibn Fāris follows a unique system unparalleled in any other lexicon as 
far as we know. In the biliterals, he begins the letter ǧ, for example, not with 
ǧ+ʾ, ǧ+b, and so on, but with ǧ+ḥ (since ḥ immediately follows ǧ in the alpha-
bet), ǧ+ḫ, ǧ+d, etc. Following the root that is made up of ǧ and the last letter 
of the alphabet (in this case, w, i.e. ǧ+w, since ǧ+y is not attested), the letters 
which precede ǧ are considered, hence ǧ+ʾ, ǧ+b, etc. In the triliterals, the sys-
tem becomes more complex since the same principle applies not only to the 
relationship between the first and second letters, but also to that between the 
second and third. For example, in triliterals whose first and second radicals 
are ǧ and d, the order is as follows: ǦDR, ǦDS, ǦDʿ, ǦDF, ǦDL, ǦDM and ǦDW/Y, 
followed by ǦDB, ǦDṮ and ǦDḤ. The same also applies to the next group of 
roots, i.e. those which start with ǧ+ḏ, and so on. In the quadriliterals and quin-
queliterals, both of which are cited under the same heading (i.e. mā ǧāʾa ʿalā 
akṯar min ṯalāṯat aḥruf “what exceeds three radicals”), lexical items are listed 
without any order, although Ibn Fāris at times differentiates among certain 
groups of words according to whether they are blended (manḥūt) or not.
Ibn Fāris’s cyclic arrangement is based on envisaging the letters of the alpha-
bet as successive points on a circle, and when a certain letter is chosen, move-
ment has to be unidirectional until all the other letters are exhausted. In line 
417	
Ibn Fāris, Maqāyīs I, 3.
418	
Cf. above, 293–294.
419	
Ibn Fāris, Maqāyīs I, 174–175. In the titles of the chapters, the first type is normally referred 
to as muḍāʿaf and the second as muṭābaq; cf. ibid., I, 337.

<!-- page 359 -->

349
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
with this conception, the taqālīb of both Ḫalīl and Ibn Durayd are abandoned. 
It is interesting to note in this regard that Ibn Fāris’s K. al-Ṯalāṯa, as opposed to 
al-Maqāyīs and al-Muǧmal, is arranged according to a special type of taqālīb 
in which only three lexical items share the radicals of their roots, albeit in a 
different order.420 Yet Ibn Fāris, who uses this peculiar system of taqālīb, in 
triplets rather than sextets (the latter being the highest theoretically possible 
combination formed out of three radicals), must have deemed it too compli-
cated for his two muǧannas lexica that are of a much larger scale. Particularly 
in al-Maqāyīs, whose roots are classified semantically in each lemma as we 
shall see, he may have wanted to avoid giving the impression that the various 
taqālīb of any given biliteral or triliteral root are semantically related. If so, 
this stance would be the exact opposite of that of his contemporary Ibn Ǧinnī 
(d. 392/1002), who, though on a much more modest scale, tries to unveil the 
semantic relationships among roots that share the same radicals irrespective 
of sequence.421
Within the lemmata in biliterals and triliterals, Ibn Fāris follows a specific 
scheme. He begins by determining whether the root has no aṣl (see below), 
one aṣl, or more. He then justifies the lack of an aṣl or specifies the basic mean-
ing of each proposed one. Each aṣl is then exemplified by one or more lexi-
cal items, often supported by attested usage (šawāhid), particularly in poetry, 
though the verses are mostly not ascribed to their renderers. The lexical items 
are briefly explained and not arranged in any specific order, although meta-
phorical usage is normally placed towards the end of any given aṣl. In roots 
that have more than three radicals, the main focus is on naḥt, and no attempt 
is made at assigning uṣūl. For the raw data of the lexicon, Ibn Fāris explains 
in the introduction that he primarily depends on five works which include, 
in addition to K. al-ʿAyn and al-Ǧamhara, three mubawwab ones, namely, 
Abū ʿUbayd’s (d. 224/838) Ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ and Muṣannaf al-ġarīb (i.e. al-Ġarīb 
al-muṣannaf ) and Ibn al-Sikkīt’s (d. 244/858) al-Manṭiq (i.e. Iṣlāḥ al-manṭiq).422 
He also (to a much lesser extent) cites several other authors, such as Sībawayhi 
(d. 180/796), Kisāʾī (d. 189/805), Farrāʾ (d. 207/822), Abū ʿUbayda (d. 209/824), 
Abū Zayd (d. 215/830), Abū Ḥātim (d. 255/869), Ibn Qutayba (d. 276/889), 
and Ṯaʿlab (d. 291/904).423 Unlike some of his contemporaries, such as Azharī 
(d. 370/981) and al-Ṣāḥib b. ʿAbbād (d. 385/995), Ibn Fāris was not interested 
in authoring an exhaustive lexicon and satisfied himself with the lexical

Cf. above, 234.

Ibn Fāris, Maqāyīs I, 3–5; cf. above, 48.

<!-- page 360 -->

350
chapter 3
items that prove the correctness of his uṣūl (or the absence of an aṣl in the 
first place) and illustrate the existence or absence of naḥt. In fact, quotations 
from the lemmata of his two main sources, the lexica of Ḫalīl and Ibn Durayd, 
are normally abbreviated, and lexical items cited therein are often ignored.424 
This notwithstanding, al-Maqāyīs contains about eighty lexical items which, 
according to its editor, are not found in any other lexicon.425
In biliterals and triliterals, Ibn Fāris identifies the uṣūl or basic meanings 
that are associated with the roots. For example, QṢṢ is said to have one aṣl 
(tracing something), whereas QLB has two (the choicest part of something; 
and changing the position of something), and QMʿ three (pouring liquid into 
a funnel; subjugation and repression; and a kind of animal).426 In some roots, 
one aṣl can have several furūʿ (subsidiary meanings)427 or the number of uṣūl 
can be four, five, or six. For example, ṢFR is divided into six uṣūl, namely, a 
color; something empty; a metal; the sound of whistling; a period of time; 
and a plant.428 Out of 4,631 roots in the lexicon, 1,808 are said not to have an 
aṣl, whereas out of the remaining 2,823 roots, 2,346 have one aṣl and only 477 
more than one.429 To illustrate how roots are grouped, we shall consider the 
previously mentioned root QMʿ. Its first meaning is exemplified by the lexi-
cal items qimʿ (funnel), iqtamaʿa (to drink all the liquid in a waterskin, as if 
through a funnel), and aqmāʿ, in the expression aqmāʿ al-qawl (in reference to 
those who hear speech but do not comprehend it, as if their ears were funnels 
that retain nothing). The second aṣl, which indicates subjugation and repres-
sion, is exemplified by qamaʿa (to subjugate) and the proper noun Qamaʿa 
(b. Ilyās), said to have been forced by his father to stay at home. For the third 
aṣl, Ibn Fāris adduces qamaʿ (large blue flies) and aqmaʿa (to repel someone, 
i.e. as one repels a fly). The lemma is concluded with three metaphorical 
usages (e.g. qamaʿ “stiffness in a horse’s knee” – probably related to the second 
aṣl since stiffness would deter or “repress” free movement, although Ibn Fāris 
does not clearly state this relationship) and with qamaʿat māl al-qawm (the 
choicest of people’s possessions), which he identifies as anomalous (šāḏḏ), i.e. 
not related to any of the uṣūl. The acknowledgment of anomalous usage is in 
424

als beginning with qāf (V, 116–119), whereas these amount to 145 in al-Ǧamhara (index, III, 
1632–1637).

<!-- page 361 -->

351
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
line with Ibn Fāris’s assertion that most of the language, though not all of it, 
conforms to qiyās.430
Synonymous with aṣl in the sense of “basic meaning” is the term qiyās. For 
example, the qiyās of the root ǦḤF (i.e. its basic meaning) is said to be “sweeping 
something in its entirety”.431 Yet qiyās also occurs in its more commonly used 
senses of norm, regular usage, analogical extension, etc. (e.g. mawḍūʿ waḍʿan 
min ġayr qiyās; kalimāt ġayr munqāsa; lākinna kalimātihi lā takād tanqās).432 
The term maqāyīs (cf. the book’s title) is also used to refer to the norms that 
govern a certain type of words, as in maqāyīs al-rubāʿī (norms that govern 
quadriliterals), which is used alongside the expression ṭuruq al-qiyās (methods 
of qiyās).433 It should also be noted that roots which do not form an aṣl mostly 
consist of disparate words, often described as having no qiyās (e.g. lam yūḍaʿ 
lahu qiyās wa-kalimātuhu mutabāyina)434 or deemed to be ­questionable.435 In 
certain cases, Ibn Fāris seems not to be sure whether a group of lexical items 
can be assigned an independent aṣl and thus describes these items as form-
ing an uṣayl (diminutive of aṣl).436 It is obvious moreover that Ibn Fāris was 
mindful of the fact that the identification of semantic relationships often leads 
to farfetched interpretations. On one occasion he declares that he detests far-
fetchedness in seeking to demonstrate how a lexical item can be related to a 
specific aṣl.437 How much he abided by this maxim, however, is certainly open 
to question given that the uṣūl which he proposes are frequently arbitrary and 
unconvincing.
Ibn Fāris asserts that his book exclusively contains the correct maqāyīs of the 
language of the Arabs.438 Accordingly, words which he identifies as Arabized 
(muʿarrab) are excluded from the proposed uṣūl.439 Similarly excluded are

it to an aṣl; e.g. ǧuddād (strings of a tent; I, 408). In other instances, he may be unaware 
that a certain word is Arabized and thus assigns to it an aṣl, as in barīd (a mule used for

<!-- page 362 -->

352
chapter 3
words whose roots are the result of a shift in their radicals, as in ibdāl (sub-
stitution; e.g. kadaša<ḫadaša “to cut”),440 qalb (metathesis; e.g. QWF<QFW),441 
and itbāʿ (alliteration; e.g. bayṣa in ḥayṣa bayṣa “confusion”).442 Onomatopoeic 
words (e.g. qahqaha “to guffaw”) and particles443 (e.g. ḥayṯu “where”) are also 
excluded,444 as are place names and names of plants.445
The link that earlier philologists often made between certain roots and the 
basic meaning retained by their derivatives obviously inspired Ibn Fāris to sys-
tematically explore the relationship between root and meaning. He acknowl-
edges Ḫalīl as a master (imām) in this domain and quotes his remark on the 
subtle difference in meaning between ǦḎW and ǦṮW446 as proof of the cor-
rectness of his own approach. Another possibility, suggested by Hārūn,447 is 
that Ibn Fāris derived his method from Ibn Durayd, who in K. al-Ištiqāq reg-
ularly linked the meaning of proper nouns to the uṣūl from which they are 
derived. But if this were true, it is odd indeed that Ibn Fāris does not normally 
include proper nouns in the uṣūl of roots in al-Maqāyīs. For example, he does 
not consider DʿD to be an aṣl in spite of the occurrence of Daʿd as a proper 
noun.448 Quite interesting in this respect is that Ibn Durayd refutes the claim 
of the Šuʿūbiyyūn that the Arabs use proper nouns that have no origin (aṣl) in 
their language449 by demonstrating in al-Ištiqāq how these nouns are related to 
the meanings of their roots. It is conceivable on the other hand that, by orderly 
arranging lexical items in al-Maqāyīs into semantically identifiable groups, Ibn 
Fāris aimed at refuting the accusations of the Šuʿūbiyyūn that Arabic was self-
contradictory and irrational,450 although he makes no direct reference to their 
accusations. Finally on the question of uṣūl, Ibn Fāris’s Istiʿārat aʿḍāʾ al-insān, in 
which he cites the figurative use of about a hundred words that originally refer 
the conveyance of messages), which he claims has the meaning of “to go back and forth” 
(I, 243).

353
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
to body parts,451 may well have been an early attempt on his part to explore the 
relationship between an aṣl and certain derivatives of the same root, albeit in 
a specific semantic field.
The second notion on which al-Maqāyīs rests is naḥt in words whose roots 
are more than three. In this Ibn Fāris also credits Ḫalīl for having been the 
first to illustrate the phenomenon, as in ʿAbšamī<ʿAbd+Šams.452 Frequent ref-
erences by Kurāʿ al-Naml (d. 310/922) to affixation in the formation of quadri-
literals and quinqueliterals in al-Muntaḫab453 may also have inspired Ibn Fāris 
to employ the notion of naḥt in analyzing lexical items which have more than 
three radicals. Yet Ibn Fāris nowhere mentions Kurāʿ in this respect.
Unlike uṣūl, whose systematic application in triliterals alone comprises 
4,631 roots, as previously mentioned, and thus allows one to speak of a seman-
tic theory proposed by Ibn Fāris, the relative fewness of cited quadriliterals 
and quinqueliterals (620 words, out of which 404 are identified as blends of 
two words or of a word and an affix)454 and the briefness of the comments on 
their structure warrant caution in ascribing a full-fledged theory of naḥt to Ibn 
Fāris. It should be noted, however, that in al-Ṣāḥibī he refers to his doctrine 
(maḏhabunā) of interpreting most words whose radicals exceed three as being 
manḥūt and says that he detailed this method (ḏakarnā ḏālika bi-wuǧūhihi) 
in al-Maqāyīs.455 Ṣaġānī (d. 650/1252) also ascribes to Ibn Fāris a book on the 
“science of naḥt” titled al-Mudḫal ilā ʿilm al-naḥt.456 In al-Maqāyīs itself, there 
are passages in which the concept of naḥt is discussed and its types identi-
fied. Since no quadriliterals or quinqueliterals are cited under hamza, Ibn Fāris 
introduces the notion of naḥt for the first time at the beginning of the section 
devoted to words of more than three radicals under bāʾ. He defines naḥt as 
the blending of two words to form a word that retains a part of each.457 On a 
later occasion, he differentiates between two types of naḥt, the first of which 
is the blending that he previously mentioned, and the second is the affixation 
of a letter (or more) to a word so that it becomes similar to (i.e. appended 
to; cf. ulḥiqa) quadriliterals and quinqueliterals.458 As for quadriliterals and

<!-- page 364 -->

354
chapter 3
­quinqueliterals that are not the result of naḥt, i.e. what he describes as having 
been so coined in origin (mawḍūʿ waḍʿan), he admits that in some cases they 
may well be the result of naḥt which he is unable to discover.459
A closer study of naḥt in al-Maqāyīs460 readily reveals several subcategories 
of manḥūt words. Out of the 404 examples in the lexicon, 267 are interpreted 
as blends of two or more lexical items, and 137 as blends of one lexical item 
and an affix.461 The vast majority of blends of the first type are derived from 
two words, e.g. ǧuḏmūr (the lowest part of a cut palm branch) <ǧiḏm (origin) 
+ ǧiḏr (origin), and ʿansal (quick and sturdy she-camel) <ʿanasa (to be sturdy) 
+ nasala (to be quick).462 Rarely are words interpreted as being blended from 
three words, as in saḥbal (wide valley; wide waterskin) <saḥala (to pour) 
+ sabala (derivatives of which indicate moving something into a lower posi-
tion) + saḥaba (to drag along).463 As for affixation, words can have one affix, 
e.g. daġfaqa (to pour) <dafaqa (to pour) + ġ; or two, e.g. šamardal (swift man) 
<ŠMR (a root that indicates swiftness) + d + l; or three, e.g. kanfalīla (large beard) 
<kafl (putting together) + n + l + long vowel.464 At the semantic level, Ibn Fāris 
often cites the influence on meaning of the affixation of a letter or more to a 
triliteral root. For example, affixation can convey taqbīḥ/tašnī ʿ (uglification), 
tahwīl (frightening), ta⁠ʾkīd (corroboration), and mubālaġa (exaggeration),465 
although Ibn Fāris does not attribute to any letter a specific meaning that it 
regularly imparts to the root to which it is affixed.
As mentioned earlier, the theoretical bases represented by the systematic 
application of uṣūl and naḥt in al-Maqāyīs are not applied in al-Muǧmal. One 
can thus argue that since the former represents a more “mature” approach to 
lexicography than the latter, it was probably authored after it, although this 
cannot be ascertained in the absence of solid textual evidence. What is certain 
however is that the two works – in spite of the sameness of their alphabetical 
ordering of letters and division of chapters (i.e. biliterals, triliterals and one 
section for quadriliterals and quinqueliterals) and their sharing of some lexi-
cal items and definitions – are quite different in purpose. In fact, al-Maqāyīs is

<!-- page 365 -->

355
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
unique in the tradition due to its theoretical bases, and in this sense al-Muǧmal 
differs from it as does any other muǧannas lexicon. In his introduction to 
al-Muǧmal, Ibn Fāris highlights two of its features, namely, conciseness and 
ease of use. Considerably shorter than al-Maqāyīs, al-Muǧmal (lit. summed 
up, synopsized) was so titled, as Ibn Fāris explains, because of its conciseness 
and brevity of expression (cf. muḫtaṣar min al-kalām qarīb; aǧmaltu l-kalām 
fīhi iǧmālan; irādata l-īǧāz; bi-awǧaz qawl wa-aqrabihi; ṣiġar ḥaǧmihi).466 On 
the question of ease of use, Ibn Fāris contrasts his lexicon with al-ʿAyn, in 
its complicated style and difficult arrangement, as well as with al-Ǧamhara, 
whose author pretentiously wanted to increase the number of lexical items 
it contained. Among the book’s other features that he points out are its fine 
arrangement (ḥusn tartībihi), simplification and elucidation (al-taqrīb wa-l-
ibāna), and confinement to correct usage (ṣaḥīḥ) to the exclusion of the 
uncouth and objectionable (al-waḥšī al-mustankar).467 He also apologetically 
justifies the inclusion of some ġarīb material by its currency among users (mā 
yatadāwaluhu l-nās) and hence the need to explain it, whether in Qurʾān, 
Ḥadīṯ or poetry.468 Ibn Fāris faithfully applies his plan in the lemmata by 
briefly defining lexical items, avoiding digression and lengthy quotations, and 
cutting down on šawāhid in the sections that deal with roots containing more 
than three radicals, but less so in those on triliterals.469 This notwithstanding, 
al-Muǧmal includes some material which al-Maqāyīs does not. Other than 
lexical items not found in the latter, it often includes proper nouns derived 
from the roots under consideration, and at times Ibn Fāris begins a chapter 
by mentioning some of the phonetic characteristics of the letter to which it is 
devoted.470
Because of its relative conciseness and suitability to the general user who 
is not specifically interested in the analysis of the data on theoretical bases, 
al-Muǧmal understandably gained more currency than al-Maqāyīs. It is quite 
strange, however, that Ibn Fāris’s unique method in al-Maqāyīs of identifying 
the basic meanings of roots (and less importantly his analysis of quadriliter-
als and quinqueliterals with reference to naḥt) was not frequently quoted in 
subsequent lexica, let alone being adopted by their authors as a basis for iden-
tifying the various semantic ranges that a given root has and consequently sep-
arating between derivatives of that root according to meaning. For example,

<!-- page 366 -->

356
chapter 3
Ibn Manẓūr (d. 711/1311), whose Lisān al-ʿArab incorporates five major earlier 
lexica, does not mention a single view of Ibn Fāris’s on the uṣūl of a root or 
the naḥt of a lexical item. A rare mention of the lexicon’s influence occurs in 
Yāqūt’s (d. 626/1229) Muʿǧam al-Udabāʾ, where Bū Ǧaʿfarak (d. 554/1150) is said 
to have incorporated into his Yanābī ʿ al-luġa material taken, among others, 
from al-Maqāyīs.471 Also noteworthy is that al-Rāġib al-Iṣfahānī (d. 502/1108) 
often uses the term aṣl in citing the general meaning of roots.472 Among 
authors of muǧannas lexica, a notable exception is Ṣaġānī (d. 650/1252), who 
quotes Ibn Fāris very frequently in his al-ʿUbāb al-zāḫir and often cites, at the 
end of the relevant lemmata, the basic meanings that he assigns to the roots473 
but stops short of following his method of dividing each lemma based on the 
uṣūl. Some of Ṣaġānī’s quotations of Ibn Fāris were in turn quoted by Zabīdī 
(d. 1205/1790) in Tāǧ al-ʿarūs.474 More meaningful recognition of Ibn Fāris’s 
brilliant principle of uṣūl had to wait till the twentieth century, particularly in 
ʿAlāyilī’s call for its adoption as one of the basic components of a new approach 
to the production of a modern Arabic-Arabic dictionary,475 and in the practice 
of the compilers of al-Muʿǧam al-kabīr of beginning each relevant lemma by a 
quotation from al-Maqāyīs in which Ibn Fāris identifies the basic meaning(s) 
of the root.476
3.4	
Asās al-balāġa by Zamaḫšarī (d. 538/1144)
This lexicon is a pioneering work from two major perspectives: alphabetical 
arrangement and focus on tropical usage (maǧāz). The latter aspect will be 
considered later, but it should be pointed out that, given its focus on maǧāz, 
al-Asās is in a sense a specialized, rather than general, lexicon and is thus pri-
marily helpful for the user who seeks metaphorical and extended meanings. 
This notwithstanding, its 4,010 lemmata477 – which are more than two-thirds 
the lemmata, say, of Ǧawharī’s (d. c. 400/1010) al-Ṣaḥāḥ, a general lexicon that

Yāqūt, Muʿǧam I, 399; cf. Ṣafadī, Wāfī VII, 214; cf. above, 265 for Bū Ǧaʿfarak’s Tāǧ 
al-maṣādir.
472	
Cf. above, 69.
473	
Ṣaġānī, ʿUbāb (ed. Āl Yāsīn, ġ) 22, 48, 60; see also 37, 44–45, 47, 49, 56, 80–84.

<!-- page 367 -->

357
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
contains 5,618 lemmata478 – do include general, i.e. literal or veridical usage, 
but not with the aim of exhausting all the derivatives of any given root.
Contemporary scholars normally refer to Zamaḫšarī in the Asās as the first 
author to arrange roots based on full alphabetical order by considering all the 
root’s components from first to last.479 This position, however, does not take 
into account that Ibn Durayd (d. 321/933) in al-Ǧamhara also arranges the best 
part of his lexicon (e.g. triliterals) alphabetically from the first to the last radi-
cal. The same is true also of Ibn Fāris’s (d. 395/1004) al-Maqāyīs, although its 
alphabetical arrangement has its own peculiarities discussed above.480 What 
Zamaḫšarī should be credited with is hence not the invention of the alpha-
betical arrangement starting with the first radical and followed by the second, 
third, etc., but the consistent application throughout his lexicon of that system, 
which had already been used by Ibn Durayd more than two centuries earlier. 
Both al-Ǧamhara and al-Maqāyīs lack the consistency of application of the 
system, as we have previously seen: Ibn Durayd arranges some of his chapters 
based on patterns rather than succession of radicals, and Ibn Fāris lists quad-
riliterals and quinqueliterals without any alphabetical order. Furthermore, 
Zamaḫšarī should be credited with the application of the alphabetical order 
without any consideration for taqālīb and root length – as did Ibn Durayd – 
and without the cyclic arrangement of letters adopted by Ibn Fāris. He is thus 
truly the first author of a muǧannas lexicon to apply the system of full alpha-
betical ordering, at the same time liberating it from earlier constraints.
From a chronological perspective, Zamaḫšarī, before authoring the Asās, 
had applied what we may call modern dictionary order in his book on proverbs 
known as al-Mustaqṣā, which he authored in 499/1106 at the age of thirty-one. 
In arranging the proverbs, as he explains in the introduction, he went beyond 
the first letter to consider the word’s middle (awāsiṭ) and final (awāḫir) let-
ters successively.481 He even went beyond the first word(s) in arranging prov-
erbs that share the first word(s). On the chronological issue as well, it has been 
erroneously suggested that Muḥammad b. Tamīm al-Barmakī (d. 433/1041) 
in al-Muntahā fī l-luġa – a supplement to Ǧawharī’s (d. c. 400/1010) al-Ṣaḥāḥ 
which, according to Yāqūt, Barmakī completed in 397/1007482 – preceded

5,639.
479	
Haywood (1965: 106); Naṣṣār (1968: II, 710); Ḫalīl (1997: 477); Ǧubūrī (2004: 108).
480	
Cf. above, 348.

arrangement of another book of his, al-Fāʾiq fī ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ, see above, 78.
482

<!-- page 368 -->

358
chapter 3
Zamaḫšarī in adopting full alphabetical order starting with the first letter.483 
However, scholars who examined the extant parts of al-Muntaḥā484 assert 
that it was arranged based on the final, and not the initial, radical of the root. 
Contrary to authors of lexica of the rhyme system to be discussed in the next 
section, Barmakī considers, after the final radical, the one that directly pre-
cedes it until he reaches the first radical, whereas their arrangement progresses 
from the last to the first to the middle radical.
Like Ibn Durayd and several other authors,485 Zamaḫšarī justifies his choice 
of an alphabetical ordering of letters by reference to its popularity and ease 
(ʿalā ašhar tartīb mutadāwalan wa-ashalihi mutanāwalan).486 Apart from 
minor inconsistencies, particularly in listing some derivatives of roots ending 
in wāw with those ending in yāʾ or vice versa,487 and in placing wāw before hāʾ 
except in the final radical of the root (e.g. NBH before NBW and BLH before 
BLW), the application of the alphabetical order is fully observed. Reduplicated 
roots, however, are not arranged in accordance with the succession of all their 
radicals; rather their third radical is not considered, as in DĠDĠ, which is listed 
after DĠṢ and before DĠFL, and NʿNʿ, which is listed after NʿẒ and before NʿF. 
Had the third radical been considered, DĠDĠ would have been placed between 
DʿW and DĠR, and NʿNʿ between NʿM and NʿY. Moreover, reduplicated roots 
are often not listed as separate lemmata, as in ǦMǦM, QLQL and RḤRḤ, which 
are mentioned under ǦMM, QLL and RḤḤ respectively. Contrastively, the tri-
literals DḤḤ and FDN are mentioned under DḤDḤ and FDFD (although FDN is 
also listed separately). As for quadriliterals, their number is merely sixty-two.488 
The reason for this is that in several cases, Zamaḫšarī mentions these under 
the triliterals from which they are presumably derived, as in DRMQ and QRMS, 
which are mentioned in the lemmata DRQ and QRS respectively. Accordingly, 
the user who does not make the derivational connection between certain 
quadriliterals and the corresponding triliterals may wrongly assume that the 
former are not included in the lexicon.
Perhaps more than in the adoption of a full alphabetical order starting with 
the first letter, Zamaḫšarī’s innovative role in the Arabic lexicographical tradi-

<!-- page 369 -->

359
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
tion lies in the content of al-Asās, that is, the distinction between the tropical 
and the literal or veridical usages (ifrād al-maǧāz ʿan al-ḥaqīqa).489 Although 
earlier authors of muǧannas lexica do at times refer to maǧāz in their 
­explanation of certain derivatives (as does Ibn Fāris in al-Maqāyīs in some lem-
mata that include metaphorical usage, normally towards their end), it obvi-
ously is not their main area of interest. In contrast, Zamaḫšarī’s primary aim 
in his lexicon is to highlight maǧāz and demonstrate how certain lexical items 
acquire meanings that can be considered maǧāzī. In this respect, his work is 
reminiscent of that of al-Rāġib al-Iṣfahānī (d. 502/1108), who in al-Mufradāt 
fī ġarīb al-Qurʾān often tries to demonstrate the semantic relationships 
(munāsabāt) between the derivatives of a root and the metaphorical uses of 
words.490 The inclusion of the term balāġa (eloquence, but in a more special-
ized sense rhetoric) in the lexicon’s title is a true reflection of Zamaḫšarī’s pur-
pose. There is also allusion in the introduction to the importance, in writing 
prose and poetry, of familiarity with ʿilm al-maʿānī and ʿilm al-bayān. These are 
two major branches of balāġa, the first of which is concerned with semantics 
and pragmatics, whereas the second deals with simile (tašbīh), trope (maǧāz), 
metonymy (kināya) and metaphor (istiʿāra). This proves that the focus of the 
lexicon is on contextual usage, rather than lexical items in isolation – a conclu-
sion bolstered by Zamaḫšarī’s reference in the introduction to the methods 
of composition (manāhiǧ al-tarkīb wa-l-ta⁠ʾlīf ) and coordination of words in 
structure (sawq al-kalimāt mutanāsiqa), as well as to orations (ḫuṭab), rhymed 
prose, poetry, expressions used by outstanding authors (ʿibārāt al-mubdiʿīn), 
and comprehensive but concise expressions (ǧawāmiʿ/nawābiġ al-kalim).491
Most lemmata are divided into two parts, the first of which includes lexical 
items that are deemed to represent ḥaqīqī usage (although Zamaḫšarī does 
not use the term to introduce this part), whereas the second is normally intro-
duced by the expression wa-min al-maǧāz and includes metaphorical and 
extended meanings. Yet the distinction between the two parts is not always 
clear-cut. To begin with, some lemmata do not include the part on maǧāz, 
but in the one untitled part of which they consist, both ḥaqīqī and maǧāzī 
usages may be cited. Moreover, in some lemmata that consist of both parts, 
it is often difficult to comprehend why a certain usage is classified under 
ḥaqīqa or maǧāz. For example, one would not be surprised if the expression 
ǧamaʿa ḥuṭām al-dunyā (He amassed the perishing earthly possessions), which 
Zamaḫšarī lists with ḥaqīqī usage in the root ḤṬM, were classified as maǧāz. 
489	
Zamaḫšarī, Asās 8.
490	
Cf. above, 69.
491	
Zamaḫšarī, Asās 7–8.

<!-- page 370 -->

360
chapter 3
On other ­occasions, each of two expressions that are closely related semanti-
cally appears in a different part of the relevant lemma as in rasaḫa l-ḥibru fī 
l-ṣaḥīfati (The ink became fixed upon the paper), which is placed in the second 
part of the lemma RSḪ although it differs little from the cited ḥaqīqī mean-
ing of rasaḫa l-šayʾu, explained by Zamaḫšarī as ṯabata fī makānihi rusūḫan 
(It became fixed in its place). Evidently, the distinction between ḥaqīqa and 
maǧāz is often open to interpretation and Zamaḫšarī, more than any other 
author of a muǧannas lexicon, must have exercised his own judgment in order 
to distinguish between the two types, in spite of his avoidance of such distinc-
tion in many lemmata.
Zamaḫšarī uses the term maǧāz in its broadest sense which covers simile, 
trope, metonymy and metaphor. The expression wa-min al-maǧāz, which nor-
mally introduces the second part of the lemma, is often substituted by wa-min 
al-mustaʿār, with no apparent difference in meaning. That the two expressions 
are used interchangeably is supported by the fact that Zamaḫšarī recurrently 
uses the latter expression in certain letters, but hardly at all in others. For 
example, he uses wa-min al-maǧāz in the letters ṭ and ẓ, substitutes it almost 
regularly by wa-min al-mustaʿār/al-istiʿāra in the letter ʿ,492 and reverts to it in 
ġ. Similarly, wa-min al-kināya can substitute wa-min al-maǧāz or appear with 
it in the same lemma,493 often without the distinction in meaning one finds in 
the books on balāġa. In other cases, usage generally classified as kināya (e.g. 
ibn ḏukāʾ “morning”, banāt al-māʾ “cranes”, and ibn al-balad “chameleon”)494 is 
loosely listed under maǧāz. But by and large, the most frequently attested type 
of balāġī usage in the lexicon is istiʿāra, which is either referred to by that term 
or, more generally, by maǧāz.495
In accordance with Zamaḫšarī’s focus on contextual usage, he normally cites 
lexical items, even in the part of the lemma that deals with ḥaqīqa, not as iso-
lated units, but as part of constructions that are typically deemed to require lit-
tle explanation. In this he significantly differs from other authors of muǧannas 
lexica, who normally cite lexical items in isolation except in the šawāhid. In the 
root ʾḪḎ, for example, he introduces aḫḫāḏ, aḫḏ and uḫḏa by respectively cit-
ing the expressions mā anta illā aḫḫāḏun nabbāḏun (You are but someone who 
eagerly takes something and then promptly abandons it), law kunta minnā

361
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
la-aḫaḏta bi-aḫḏinā (Were you one of our numbers, you would have adopted 
our dispositions), and li-fulānatin uḫḏatun tuʾaḫḫiḏu bihā l-nāsa (She has an 
enchantment by which she captivates people). The expressions he cites are fre-
quently rhymed, i.e. in saǧʿ form, and are probably included for the user who 
may wish to incorporate them into his own prose. Two examples are lā yastawī 
man baḏala nuṣratahu li-qawmihi baḏlan wa-man yaḫḏiluhum iḏā stanṣarūhu 
ḫaḏlā (He who truly gives his support to his people cannot be deemed equal 
to him who totally forsakes them when they call for his support) and laysa 
bi-aḫī l-muʾmini man lā yurḫī l-sitra ʿalā maʿāyibihi wa-lā yarmī ʿanhu bi-l-ḥaṣā fī 
maġāyibihi (He is not a brother to the believer who does not veil his shortcom-
ings and does not defend him in his absence).496 Such constructions are remi-
niscent of works that contain rhymed phrases aimed to be used by writers who 
wish to produce a text of an eloquent style, e.g. al-Alfāẓ; al-kitāba wa-l-taʿbīr 
by Ibn al-Marzubān al-Bāḥiṯ (d. c. 330/941) and Ǧawāhir al-alfāẓ by Qudāma b. 
Ǧaʿfar (d. 337/948).497 Among later authors, Zabīdī (d. 1205/1790) in particular 
notes Zamaḫšarī’s rhymed constructions and frequently quotes them in the 
relevant lemmata, normally preceded by the phrase wa-min saǧaʿāt al-Asās.498
Some of the rhymed constructions cited by Zamaḫšarī are certainly taken 
from ḫuṭab, ǧawāmiʿ al-kalim, etc. as he explains in the introduction, whereas 
others may well have been of his own composition, although in most cases it 
is difficult to distinguish between the two types. In certain cases, Zamaḫšarī 
reports usage he has heard and often specifies where, e.g. Ḥiǧāz, Mecca, 
Baghdad, etc.499 His other šawāhid include 128 Qurʾānic verses500 – a sur-
prisingly small number given his remarks in the introduction about Qurʾānic 
balāġa and his unmistakable balāġī interpretation of Qurʾānic verses in 
al-Kaššāf. Šawāhid extracted from Ḥadīṯ are more abundant, and amount 
to 495.501 There is also a total of 115 proverbs.502 Not unexpectedly, the larg-
est number of šawāhid comes from poetry, including raǧaz – a total of 6,234 
verses.503 Some of these šawāhid are ascribed to poets who are generally not

<!-- page 372 -->

362
chapter 3
quoted by philologists based on the temporal classification of poetry.504 It thus 
seems that Zamaḫšarī does not link faṣāḥa exclusively to the early epochs, and 
accordingly cites verses by Abū Nuwās (d. 198/814), Abū Tammām (d. 231/846), 
Mutanabbī (d. 354/965), and Abū l-ʿAlāʾ al-Maʿarrī (d. 449/1057).505
Zamaḫšarī, in line with his focus on maǧāz and his citing of lexical items 
mostly in context, selectively quotes ḥaqīqī usage recorded in earlier lexica 
(e.g. K. al-ʿAyn, al-Ǧamhara and al-Tahḏīb). As for the derivatives cited within 
the lemmata, they are not meant to be exhaustive; rather, Zamaḫšarī, most 
probably lists them so as to help the user establish a link between their ḥaqīqī 
meanings and the extended meaning which maǧāz represents. He does not 
normally indicate that link, but leaves it to the user to make his own conclu-
sions – probably due to his conviction that the numerous constructions that he 
quotes should suffice to demonstrate the semantic development from ḥaqīqa 
to maǧāz. Furthermore, verbs are usually cited in the perfect and are not fol-
lowed by the imperfect or verbal noun(s) like in most other lexica, obviously as 
the inclusion of these forms would contribute little to the main theme of the 
lexicon. Unlike his al-Kaššāf, al-Fāʾiq and al-Mustaqṣā, which explain Qurʾānic 
verses, ḥadīṯs and proverbs consecutively, Zamaḫšarī in al-Asās very frequently 
provides no explanation for the material he cites. This not only includes lexi-
cal items that must have been generally understood, but also less frequently 
used items that may require explanation (e.g. tannūma “a kind of tree” and 
dayǧūr “darkness”), as well as a large number of constructions that illustrate 
the use of words in context. This is further proof that the lexicon is not primar-
ily addressed to the user who needs to find the meaning of certain words, but 
to the one who is interested in the contextual use of words and in eloquent 
expressions that would enhance the style of any writer in that age.
In its two main features, al-Asās was sadly not emulated by subsequent 
authors. The major lexica that were authored after it, such as Ṣaġānī’s (d. 650/ 
1252) al-ʿUbāb al-zāḫir, Ibn Manẓūr’s (d. 711/1311) Lisān al-ʿArab, and Fīrūzābādī’s 
(d. 817/1415) al-Qāmūs al-muḥīṭ, followed the rhyme system rather than 
the alphabetical one. Similarly, no other author of a muǧannas lexicon fol-
lowed Zamaḫšarī in highlighting maǧāz, reference to which remained largely 
as sporadic as it had been in lexica authored prior to al-Asās. Even Zabīdī 
(d. 1205/1790), who frequently quotes maǧāzī usage cited in al-Asās,506 does
