363
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
so only to complement the material, and thus distinction between ḥaqīqa and 
maǧāz is far from being his primary concern. It is also worth mentioning that 
to ʿAbdalra⁠ʾūf al-Munāwī (d. 1031/1622) is ascribed Iḥkām al-Asās, in which 
he is reported to have abridged al-Asās and rearranged it in a fashion similar 
to Ǧawharī’s (d. c. 400/1010) al-Ṣaḥāḥ (i.e. in rhyme order).507 Contemporary 
scholarship has certainly been more appreciative than medieval authors of 
Zamaḫšarī’s pioneering application of the so-called modern dictionary order 
(without consideration for the taqālīb and root length) and his systematic 
attempt at distinguishing between ḥaqīqī and maǧāzī usages.
4	
The Rhyme System
In the canonical form of this system, lexical items are typically arranged in 
alphabetical order on the basis of the radicals of their roots, starting with the 
last followed by the first, then by the intermediate consonants. The similarity 
between this system and the alphabetical one is further demonstrated by the 
fact that, following the last radical which is considered first, the other radicals 
are arranged forward and not backwards (hence 3 + 1 + 2 in triliterals and 4 + 1 + 
2 + 3 in quadriliterals). In the extant parts of Barmakī’s (d. 433/1041) al-Muntahā 
fī l-luġa, however, the last radical is curiously followed in the arrangement by 
the one that directly precedes it, and so the first radical is considered last.508
As previously argued,509 the first lexicon to follow the alphabetical order 
(albeit partially), Abū ʿAmr al-Šaybānī’s (d. 206/821) K. al-Ǧīm, may well have 
been authored very close to the time when Ḫalīl (d. 175/791) devised the plan 
of K. al-ʿAyn (or actually authored the lexicon), i.e. in the second part of the 
second/eighth century. The rhyme system came into use at a later period, but 
we are not sure exactly when. Indeed, Ǧawharī (d. c. 400/1010) was the first to 
apply, in his al-Ṣaḥāḥ, the system in full and with no regard to morphologi-
cal patterns. Yet, the idea of arranging lexical items on the basis of their final 
letters preceded al-Ṣaḥāḥ, and it is quite likely that Ǧawharī was inspired by 
the arrangement adopted by his maternal uncle, Fārābī (d. 350/961), in Dīwān 
al-adab. Fārābī himself states that the taṣnīf of his book is unprecedented, and 
this may be a reference either to the arrangement of morphological patterns or 
to the type of alphabetical arrangement used within each section, i.e. the last 
radical, followed by the first and then intermediate radicals.510 Several decades 
507	
Muḥibbī, Ḫulāṣa II, 415.
508	
Cf. above, 358.
509	
Cf. above, 333.

Fārābī, Dīwān I, 72–73; cf. above, 262.

<!-- page 374 -->

364
chapter 3
before Fārābī, Bandanīǧī (d. 284/897) authored al-Taqfiya fī l-luġa, generally 
considered to be the first attempt of its kind to arrange words based on their 
final letters, although the author does not take the other letters into account 
and hence the items are not listed according to the order of all the radicals of 
their roots. In this respect, al-Taqfiya is similar to the first lexicon of the alpha-
betical system, Abū ʿAmr’s K. al-Ǧīm, whose arrangement does not go beyond 
the first letters of the words. The two works are also similar in that their system 
of arrangement is not devised with the intention of exhausting all used roots 
(let alone unused ones) in the language.
The fact that Bandanīǧī’s al-Taqfiya is the first extant source we know of that 
uses the rhyme system (albeit not in what came to be the canonical fashion 
established by Ǧawharī) does not necessarily mean that it is the first attempt 
at arranging entries based on their last letters. If one goes by death dates, Ibn 
Qutayba’s (d. 276/889) K. al-Taqfiya should be chronologically placed before 
Bandanīǧī’s. Ibn al-Nadīm (d. 380/990) reports that he saw three out of what 
he estimates to be five volumes of Ibn Qutayba’s book and quotes the view that 
it is larger and better than Bandanīǧī’s.511 This comparison leaves little doubt 
that Ibn Qutayba arranged his book in a manner similar to Bandanīǧī’s, and, 
although one cannot be absolutely certain, he most probably did not go beyond 
the last letter at this relatively early stage.512 What is more certain is that our 
knowledge of the first attempts to list lexical items based on their final letters is 
far from complete. We have already quoted Dīnawarī’s (d. 282/895) statement 
in which he indicates his preference for arranging lexical items according to 
their first, rather than last, letters (wa-taṣnīfuhā ʿalā ḥurūf awāʾilihā aḥabb 
ilayya min taṣnīfihā ʿalā ḥurūf awāḫirihā).513 This implies that the latter system 
was sufficiently used to rival the alphabetical one.
It would be naive to suppose that the only works which adopted the 
rhyme system by Dīnawarī’s time were those of his two contemporaries, 
Bandanīǧī and Ibn Qutayba, merely because they are the only ones of which 
we know. In fact, we do have evidence of a rhyme arrangement that is a few 
decades earlier than the time of Ibn Qutayba, Dīnawarī and Bandanīǧī, but 
which seems – as far as I was able to determine – to have largely escaped the 
511	
Ibn al-Nadīm, Fihrist 85.
512	
Another book by the title of al-Taqfiya is mentioned in Qifṭī’s Inbāh I, 360. This is Ibn 
Ḫālawayhi’s (d. 370/980) Taqfiyat Mā ḫtalafa lafẓuhu wa-ttafaqa maʿnāhu, a rearrange-
ment based on the rhyme system of a book by Ibrāhīm b. Yaḥyā b. al-Mubārak al-Yazīdī 
(d. 225/840). It is, however, impossible to tell whether Ibn Ḫālawayhi went beyond the 
final letter in his arrangement.

365
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
notice of modern scholarship.514 I hereby refer to a lengthy chapter in Iṣlāḥ 
al-manṭiq,515 authored by Ibn al-Sikkīt (d. 244/858), one of Bandanīǧī’s teach-
ers, in which lexical items are arranged based on their last letters only. The 
chapter deals with pairs of nouns, one of the pattern faʿl and the other of the 
pattern faʿal, which have different meanings. The first group of such pairs, for 
example, ends with the letter b, e.g. nadb/nadab, ʿaǧb/ʿaǧab, ḍarb/ḍarab, ǧaḏb/
ǧaḏab, karb/karab, ḥarb/ḥarab. The arrangement of the lexical items resembles 
that used by Bandanīǧī in al-Taqfiya (see next section) inasmuch as the items 
share the same patterns as well as final letters. Yet unlike al-Taqfiya the letters 
are not ordered according to the common ʾ, b, t, ṯ arrangement, but occur in the 
following order: b, r, ʿ, q, d, l, n, m, n, m (the last two being repeated), f, ṭ, k, ḍ, ṣ, 
t, ṯ, ǧ, ḥ, ḏ, s. Although this arrangement is most probably haphazard, some of 
its parts bear marked resemblance to the prevalent order (cf. t, ṯ, ǧ, ḥ and per-
haps l, n, m; note also that the first four letters preserve the same arrangement, 
though not consecutively). Why Ibn al-Sikkīt chose to arrange this particular 
chapter of his book in this way, contrary to other chapters, remains a mystery.516
The type of arrangement in al-Iṣlāḥ’s chapter on faʿl and faʿal (and perhaps 
in other contemporary works that have not survived) seems to be the precur-
sor of the rhyme system, in which the arrangement of letters begins with ʾ and 
ends with y. Strongly pointing out in this direction is the striking similarity 
in the arrangement of lexical items between parts of that chapter and those 
sections of al-Taqfiya that deal with the patterns faʿl and faʿal. Ibn al-Sikkīt’s 
chapter is almost totally incorporated into these sections, and what Bandanīǧī 
seems to have done is to separately quote the lexical items of the pattern faʿl 
cited by Ibn al-Sikkīt, often in their exact order of appearance, and then quote 
the items of the pattern faʿal. For example, the lexical items of the pattern faʿl 
514	
ʿAṭiyya (1976: 305) briefly identifies certain passages in Iṣlāḥ al-manṭiq which Bandanīǧī 
utilized in al-Taqfiya, but fails to recognize the importance, from the historical perspec-
tive of the rhyme system, of Ibn al-Sikkīt’s arrangement of these passages on the basis of 
the final letter.
515	
Ibn al-Sikkīt, Iṣlāḥ 37–84.
516	
In the rest of the book, one only sporadically finds listed consecutively certain lexical 
items that are of the same pattern and end with the same letter, i.e. not in the systematic 
fashion in which items are arranged in the chapter on faʿl and faʿal. Examples from vari-
ous parts of the book include ġayl, qayl, ġasl (10–11); amr, ḫaṭr, ḏamr, ḫayr (12); sayf, ḫayf, 
ḍayf, qarf (15); waṯr, ḍarr, ṣarr, sarr, bašr (20–22); ṣirm, ǧirm, ḥirm (34); buḫl, šuġl, ṯukl (86); 
ʿamr, ʿaṣr, ʿaqr (91); šaʿr, ṣaḫr, nahr, baʿr (97); ḥawr, bawr, fawr, nawr (124–125); qarabūs, 
ṭarasūs, qaraqūs, salaʿūs (173); albada, aṣrada, azbada (277); qabaḍa, nafaḍa, ʿaḍada (last 
radical is d, but is pronounced emphatically), ʿaraḍa (329).

366
chapter 3
ending with ḥ in al-Iṣlāḥ are: nazḥ, ṭarḥ, falḥ, ṭalḥ, ṣabḥ, ṣarḥ, naḍḥ and qarḥ.517 
These appear in exactly the same order in al-Taqfiya and are complemented 
by other lexical items not mentioned by Ibn al-Sikkīt.518 Similarly, in the letter 
ʿ, the items mentioned in al-Iṣlāḥ, i.e. ḏarʿ, šarʿ, qamʿ, ṭabʿ, ḍarʿ, farʿ, ḍabʿ, qarʿ, 
ǧarʿ, ṣadʿ, salʿ, qalʿ, ǧazʿ, ḍalʿ and nazʿ,519 appear in the same order in al-Taqfiya 
(with the omission, perhaps inadvertently, of qarʿ since its counterpart, qaraʿ, 
appears with nouns of the faʿal pattern in its expected location between ḍabaʿ 
and ǧaraʿ).520 Given the strong resemblance between Ibn al-Sikkīt’s chapter 
and the corresponding parts of al-Taqfiya, it is highly probable that Bandanīǧī 
derived his method of arrangement from Ibn al-Sikkīt but expanded his own 
material to include patterns other than faʿl and faʿal. In all cases, the rhyme 
system in its initial form is certainly earlier than has been hitherto assumed.
In the rhyme system, the choice of the ʾ, b, t, ṯ alphabetical order is easy 
to explain given the numerous references to its currency and ease of use.521 
However, it is more difficult to explain why the basis of the arrangement 
should be the last radical of the root, particularly since the authors themselves 
offer no explanation for this feature. A rare attempt at justifying the choice of 
the last radical in this system occurs in the introduction of Bandanīǧī’s al-Taq-
fiya. Bandanīǧī’s disciple who wrote that introduction claims that the feature 
which attracted the author’s attention in examining usage (kalām) is that each 
word (kalima) ends with one of the twenty-eight letters of the alphabet.522 It is 
implied that this aspect of word structure is what inspired Bandanīǧī with the 
rhyme arrangement. Obviously, this argument is too naive to be taken seriously, 
and it can be counterargued that it could also not have escaped Bandanīǧī’s 
notice that each word also begins with one of the twenty-eight letters of the 
alphabet. So, why should he choose the final and not the first letter as the basis 
of his arrangement?
The arrangement by last radical is most probably related to rhyme (i.e. 
qāfiya). But before discussing this possibility, we shall point out less convinc-
ing suggestions. ʿUmar, in his introduction to Fārābī’s (d. 350/961) Dīwān al-
adab, concludes that, since arrangement by first letter had already been used 
by earlier lexicographers, Fārābī (to whom he ascribes the invention of the 
rhyme system) must have adopted the last letter as the basis of arrangement of

<!-- page 377 -->

367
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
lexical items for the sake of innovation.523 There is, of course, no way to sub-
stantiate this claim. ʿUmar moreover suggests – as do others524 – that Fārābī’s 
choice may be linked to the fact that the final letters of Arabic words are more 
stable than initial and medial ones, as these are subject to change in certain 
derivatives of the root and are often preceded by affixes that may be confused 
for radicals. Accordingly, he argues, arrangement by last radical is easier for the 
user who is not skillful in morphology, that is, distinguishing between unaug-
mented (muǧarrad) and augmented (mazīd) forms. But other than the fact 
that final letters are themselves also frequently subject to change and the loss 
of their final position due to suffixation or elision,525 ʿUmar’s suggestion about 
facilitating the search carried by the one who is not cognizant of morpho-
logical patterns completely ignores the fact that Fārābī’s book is meticulously 
divided into sections and subsections based on morphological patterns526 and 
is practically useless to the one who is ignorant enough of morphology not to 
distinguish between augmented and unaugmented forms. For his part, Carter 
argues that arrangement by last radical might be due to the significance that 
that radical assumes in distinguishing many near-synonymous roots that share 
their first two radicals (e.g. NBǦ, NBḌ, NBṬ, NBʿ, NBĠ, NBQ, all related to the 
gushing of water).527 It can be argued, however, that since the first two radicals 
are the ones that determine the general meaning of such roots and exclude all 
other possible meanings, their semantic significance exceeds that of the last 
radical which merely differentiates between specialized meanings within the 
same semantic range specified by the first two radicals. Furthermore, given 
that the phenomenon mentioned by Carter is quite limited and has received 
but little attention from Arab morphologists, the likelihood of its giving rise to 
an entire system of classification is evidently feeble. As for Carter’s observa-
tion (which he admits may be a result rather than a cause) that “abbreviations 
often select the last important consonant”, e.g. q for folio (waraqa) and ḍ for 
523	
See ʿUmar’s introduction to Fārābī, Dīwān I, 18–19.
524	
Darwīš (1956: 93–94); A. ʿAṭṭār (1990: 154–155).
525	
Consider, for instance, the words ab, ibn, bint and dam, whose third radical is supposedly 
a weak letter (hence the roots are ʾBW, BNW, and DMW); words ending with t, such as 
sanat(un) (year) and sinat(un) (slumber), which in spite of their apparent similarity, are 
assumed to be derived from SNH and WSN respectively; words such as ʿiẓat(un), zinat(un), 
ʿidat(un), ǧihat(un), etc., whose final t is generally explained as a substitute (badal) for the 
elided initial w (hence the roots are WʿẒ, WZN, WʿD, WǦH, etc.); and jussive verbs whose 
final weak letter is elided in writing, as in yanqaḍī vs. lam yanqaḍi.
526	
Cf. above, 262–263.
527	
Carter (1990: 110–111); cf. Seidensticker (2002: 153).

<!-- page 378 -->

368
chapter 3
al-Ḥāmiḍ (proper name), the contrary is true in several other cases, e.g. ǧ for 
plural (ǧamʿ), m for known (maʿrūf ), and š for explanation (šarḥ).
To return to the question of rhyme, it has been suggested that the purpose 
of the arrangement by final radical is to provide poets with a useful tool that 
would facilitate their search for suitable rhymes when composing poetry (šiʿr) 
or writing rhymed prose (saǧʿ).528 This suggestion rests on more solid grounds 
than the previously mentioned suggestions, particularly given the dominance 
of poetry in the philological tradition as a whole. al-Taqfiya’s introduction in 
fact directly links the rhyme arrangement to poetry. The writer of the introduc-
tion explains that the lexicon’s title (which literally means “rhyming”) refers 
to qawāfī, plural of qāfiya, which he defines as a line of poetry (al-bayt min 
al-šiʿr).529 We also know that Bandanīǧī himself was a poet and that his two 
other books, Maʿānī l-šiʿr and al-ʿArūḍ 530deal with poetry matters. The lexicon’s 
arrangement of lexical items based on their final radicals, as well as the divi-
sion of each chapter into sections each of which representing a specific qāfiya 
(cf. section 4.1 on al-Taqfiya below), might not be conclusive evidence that its 
primary purpose is to facilitate the search for rhymes. Yet it certainly indicates 
that the rhyme system – which effectively produces a rhyming lexicon – has 
its origin in poetry since it is essentially founded upon two main principles 
borrowed from verse, namely, the rawiyy (rhyme letter) and the patterns of 
the words containing that letter. Accordingly, the lexicon’s twenty-eight chap-
ters correspond to the rawiyy, and the divisions into afāʿīl within each chap-
ter correspond to the patterns.531 It is also worth observing that, save those 
that follow no arrangement, the dawāwīn, i.e. collections of poems by specific 
authors, are invariably arranged according to rhyme, starting with ʾ and end-
ing with y, and the similarity between them and rhyming lexica is unmistak-
able. It is thus no coincidence that Ibn al-Sikkīt, whose chapter on the patterns 
faʿl and faʿal is the first example we know in which lexical items are arranged 
according to their final letters, was one of those who collected the dīwāns of 
several Ǧāhilī and Islamic poets, such as Umruʾ al-Qays, Zuhayr, Ǧarīr and Abū 
Nuwās.532 Regardless of the actual arrangement of these dīwāns, focus on the 
rhyme letter in this chapter of al-Iṣlāḥ is certainly linked to the fact that words 
which share the same pattern and end with the same letter may occur in the

369
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
same poem. Further evidence of the relationship between the rhyme system 
and poetry will also be discussed in the next section.
Arrangement by last letter was at times adopted in several mubawwab 
works discussed in the previous chapter. These range from Ibn Ǧinnī’s (d. 392/ 
1002) al-Muqtaḍab min kalām al-ʿArab in the fourth/tenth century to Munšī’s 
(d. 1001/1592) Risālat al-aḍdād in the tenth/sixteenth century. Ṣaġānī (d. 650/ 
1252), for example, arranged according to the last letter four of his monographs, 
namely, Fī asāmī l-ḏiʾb wa-kunāhu, Naqʿat al-ṣadyān fī mā ǧāʾa ʿalā l-faʿalān, 
al-Infiʿāl, and Mā banathu l-ʿArab ʿalā faʿāli.533 These share the arrangement 
of his two most famous muǧannas lexica, i.e. al-ʿUbāb al-zāḫir and al-Takmila 
wa-l-ḏayl wa-l-ṣila, although they differ with other works of his which are 
arranged based on their first letters, e.g. Yafʿūl.534 In muǧannas lexica in partic-
ular, the rhyme system became the norm from Ǧawharī’s time towards the end 
of the fourth/tenth century up to Zabīdī’s (d. 1205/1790) Tāǧ al-ʿarūs, on the 
eve of the modern period. It is quite telling that Zamaḫšarī’s Asās al-balāġa, 
which is arranged on the basis of the first letter, was rearranged by Munāwī 
(d. 1031/1622) according to the rhyme system,535 i.e. in accordance with the sys-
tem of arrangement par excellence in the major muǧannas lexica.
Lexica which follow the rhyme system are listed below. To this list we could 
add Fārābī’s (d. 350/961) Dīwān al-adab, except that it was previously discussed 
with works that include nominal and verbal patterns.536 Its significance in the 
development of the rhyme system will, however, be discussed further in our 
study of al-Ṣaḥāḥ.
1.	
al-Taqfiya fī l-luġa by Bandanīǧī (d. 284/897).
2.	
Tāǧ al-luġa wa-ṣaḥāḥ al-ʿArabiyya (al-Ṣaḥāḥ/al-Ṣiḥāḥ) by Ǧawharī 
(d. c. 400/1010).
3.	
al-ʿUbāb al-zāḫir wa-l-lubāb al-fāḫir by Ṣaġānī (d. 650/1252).
4.	
Lisān al-ʿArab by Ibn Manẓūr (d. 711/1311).
5.	
al-Qāmūs al-muḥīṭ by Fīrūzābādī (d. 817/1415).
6.	
Tāǧ al-ʿarūs min ǧawāhir al-Qāmūs by Zabīdī (d. 1205/1790).
Reference will also be made, wherever possible, to some of the supplements, 
abridgements and rearrangements of these lexica. A few supplements, it

<!-- page 380 -->

370
chapter 3
should be noted, contain extensive material – as in the case of Ṣaġānī’s supple-
ment to al-Ṣaḥāḥ titled al-Takmila wa-l-ḏayl wa-l-ṣila and Zabīdī’s supplement 
to al-Qāmūs al-muḥīṭ, also with the same title as Ṣaġānī’s book – and may to a 
certain degree be regarded as independent lexica in their own right.
4.1	
al-Taqfiya fī l-luġa by Bandanīǧī (d. 284/897)
Several of the issues related to this lexicon were discussed above in the context 
of the beginnings of the rhyme system. al-Taqfiya is the first extant work that 
arranges lexical items based on their last letters (though other letters are not 
taken into consideration) and includes, in twenty-eight chapters, all the let-
ters of the alphabet, starting with ʾ and ending with y. The ascription to Ibn 
Qutayba (d. 276/889) of a book by the same title, al-Taqfiya, indicates that other 
contemporaries of Bandanīǧī’s also employed the rhyme system, although it is 
impossible to tell whether their modes of arrangement were identical to his. 
We also established (perhaps for the first time) that arrangement by final letter 
predates al-Taqfiya by several decades in a lengthy chapter in Ibn al-Sikkīt’s 
(d. 244/858) Iṣlāḥ al-manṭiq. The fact that Bandanīǧī incorporated the mate-
rial of that chapter into his lexicon and almost fully retained its arrangement 
of lexical items strongly suggests that – as far as our knowledge of the sources 
goes – the origin of the rhyme system lies in Ibn al-Sikkīt’s book, which might 
have inspired Bandanīǧī with the idea of applying the system to the whole 
alphabet and to a more extensive list of lexical items.
al-Taqfiya bears some resemblance to the first lexicon of each of the two pre-
viously discussed systems, the phonetic-permutative and the alphabetical. To 
start with the latter, it resembles Abū ʿAmr al-Šaybānī’s (d. 206/821) K. al-Ǧīm in 
following the ʾ, b, t, ṯ system and taking only one letter of the root into account. 
More significantly, both authors adopt a system of arrangement that is not 
intended to encompass all Arabic roots.537 In these features, both lexica differ 
from Ḫalīl’s (d. 175/791) K. al-ʿAyn, which is phonetically arranged based on all 
radicals of the root and which aims at exhausting all Arabic roots, whether 
actual or theoretical. Yet, regardless of arrangement and content, al-Taqfiya’s 
introduction, though much shorter, is reminiscent of K. al-ʿAyn’s introduction 
on two counts: the author’s views are reported by one of his disciples in the 
third person, at times appearing in the first person,538 and a justification is 
given to the system of arrangement. Just as Ḫalīl is said to have put his mind 
537	
Cf. above, 364.
538	
Note that al-Taqfiya must have been dictated since Bandanīǧī was blind; cf. Yāqūt, Muʿǧam 
VI, 2844; Qifṭī, Inbāh IV, 79; Ṣafadī, Nakt 312 and Wāfī XXIX, 52.
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3

<!-- page 381 -->

371
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
to work (aʿmala fikrahu)539 in order to arrange the letters of the alphabet in a 
manner that would exhaust all roots, Bandanīǧī is said to have contemplated 
speech (naẓara fī l-kalām)540 and, having concluded that every word should 
end with one of the twenty-eight letters of the alphabet, arranged the mate-
rial according to this principle. Given that Bandanīǧī adopted Ibn al-Sikkīt’s 
arrangement of lexical items in one of al-Iṣlāḥ’s chapters, his disciple’s com-
ment concerning the lexicon’s arrangement – irrespective of what it is meant 
to impart to the reader concerning an innovative approach by Bandanīǧī – 
can only be considered correct if it means that he systematically applied an 
already existing system of arrangement to all letters of the alphabet from ʾ to 
y. As for the relationship between this system and poetry, it has already been 
discussed in the previous section, but further light will be shed on it in the 
remaining discussion.
Unlike Abū ʿAmr, whose only criterion for arrangement of lexical items is 
their first letter, Bandanīǧī introduces qāfiya as another criterion which nar-
rows down the search for the required word.541 He thus refers in the introduc-
tion to the afāʿīl, by which he means the rhyming patterns that are exemplified 
by a group of lexical items and are designated as qāfiya in the text. To take the 
short chapter on ẓ as an example, it starts with an untitled group of words of 
the patterns fiʿl (e.g. ḥifẓ), fuʿl (e.g. ruʿẓ) and faʿl (e.g. ġanẓ). Following this are 
three sections, each of which is called qāfiya. The first of these includes words 
whose final radical is preceded by -a (e.g. ġilaẓ and nakaẓ); the second words 
whose final radical is preceded by -ī or -ū (e.g. ġalīẓ, taqyīẓ and ǧuḥūẓ); and the 
third words whose final radical is preceded by -ā (e.g. ġilāẓ, iǧʿāẓ and iġtiyāẓ).542 
Accordingly, the afāʿīl in each group do not represent a single morphological 
pattern, but have one feature in common – their suitability to be used as qāfiya 
in the same poem. In other words, ġalīẓ, taqyīẓ and ǧuḥūẓ are of the patterns 
faʿīl, tafʿīl and fuʿūl respectively, but can be appropriately used as last words in 
lines of the same poem (e.g. the kāmil and raǧaz meters). The same applies 
to nabīḏ, ḫinḏīḏ, ʿūḏ and maǧḏūḏ, all of which are cited under one qāfiya in 
539	
Ḫalīl, ʿAyn I, 47.
540	
Bandanīǧī, Taqfiya 36.
541	
Contrary to all other letters, the chapter of alif is divided into sections, some of which 
embrace more than one qāfiya. Four sections are included and deal with words ending 
with alif mamdūda (e.g. abāʾ), hamza (e.g. naba⁠ʾ), hamza preceded by quiescence (e.g. 
sirʾ), and alif maqṣūra (e.g. abā), but words of one type are often placed in a section 
devoted to another.
542	
Bandanīǧī, Taqfiya 526–529.
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM

---

<!-- page 382 -->

372
chapter 3
the letter ḏ.543 Moreover, no distinction is made in the same qāfiya between 
geminated and ungeminated words (cf. sabb, ḍabb and ġabb with sa⁠ʾb, ʿatb and 
naḥb),544 since both types are of the same qāfiya (i.e. can occur as last words 
in the same poem). These principles of arrangement provide yet another 
argument in favor of the existence of a firm link between poetry and the rise 
of the rhyme system. Two other features of al-Taqfiya are also significant in 
this respect. The first is that it cites only lexical items (exclusively nouns and 
verbal nouns), but not roots. Had roots been cited instead, or had they been 
placed at the beginning of each entry (i.e. to be followed by their derivatives), 
search for the required rhyming words would have become more difficult. 
Secondly, Bandanīǧī frequently includes within each group commonly used 
words and does not explain them, e.g. ṣabīḥ, malīḥ, šaḥīḥ, ṣaḥīḥ, madīḥ, naǧīḥ, 
qabīḥ, ḏabīḥ, ǧarīḥ and sāʿa, biḍāʿa, iḍāʿa, ǧamāʿa, šafāʿa, manāʿa, iḏāʿa, išāʿa, 
šaǧāʿa.545 Such words – which Bandanīǧī depicts in the introduction as both 
eloquent and understood by the generality of people (al-kalām al-faṣīḥ allaḏī 
lā yaǧhaluhu l-ʿawāmm)546 – are apparently included not for any semantic rea-
son, but in order to increase the corpus of rhyming words from which the user 
can choose. Yet irrespective of whether Bandanīǧī indeed adopted the rhyme 
system primarily as an aid to poets and writers of rhymed prose, his book does 
indeed provide such aid to the user who needs it.
Apart from Ibn al-Sikkīt’s Iṣlāḥ al-manṭiq, whose influence on al-Taqfiya 
was demonstrated above, Bandanīǧī derives some of his material from Ḫalīl’s 
(d. 175/791) K. al-ʿAyn, Abū ʿUbayd’s (d. 224/838) Ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ, and several of 
Ibn Qutayba’s (d. 276/889) works, but does not acknowledge these sources.547 
As for šawāhid, the lexicon includes 250 Qurʾānic verses, 121 ḥadīṯs and aṯars,548 
and 1,354 lines of poetry (including raǧaz), but only fourteen references to dia-
lectal usage.549

in comments incorporated into al-Taqfiya’s text.

This relatively large number of ḥadīṯs is in line with our previous argument (see above, 
29–30) that early lexicographers did not share the restrictions laid by early grammarians 
on quoting Ḥadīṯ.

<!-- page 383 -->

373
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
4.2	
Tāǧ al-luġa wa-ṣaḥāḥ al-ʿArabiyya (al-Ṣaḥāḥ/al-Ṣiḥāḥ) by Ǧawharī 
(d. c. 400/1010) 
This lexicon is a major milestone in the lexicographical tradition not only 
because of its arrangement but also because of its author’s attempt to include 
in it only material which he deems to be correct. In the words of Blachère, 
it represents “presque une révolution dans l’histoire de la lexicologie arabe”.550 
For at least four centuries, i.e. until Fīrūzābādī (d. 817/1415) authored al-Qāmūs 
al-muḥīṭ, it was the standard work, that is, the most widely used lexicon, and, 
according to Zabīdī (d. 1205/1790), continued to be so even after the most 
comprehensive lexica, such as al-Lisān and al-Qāmūs were authored.551 To 
compare it with works on Ḥadīṯ having comparable titles552 – its authoritative-
ness in its field matched that of the Ṣaḥīḥs of Buḫārī (d. 256/870) and Muslim 
(d. 261/875) in theirs. Yet it should be remembered that in the two most impor-
tant aspects of his lexicon, i.e. arrangement and correctness of lexical items, 
Ǧawharī did not start from scratch; rather, he developed or standardized cer-
tain lexicographical principles established by earlier scholars.
Previous attempts at arranging lexical items according to the last letters have 
already been discussed.553 In the earliest of these attempts – Ibn al-Sikkīt’s 
(d. 244/858) lengthy chapter on faʿl and faʿal in Iṣlāḥ al-manṭiq and Bandanīǧī’s 
al-Taqfiya – lexical items are arranged based on final letter only, but whereas 
Ibn al-Sikkīt’s items are not arranged according to the ʾ, b, t, ṯ system, al-Taqfiya 
consists of twenty-eight chapters, successively arranged from ʾ to y. The next 
stage in the development of the rhyme system is associated with Dīwān al-
adab by Isḥāq b. Ibrāhīm al-Fārābī (d. 350/961), who for the first time, as far as 
we know, also took into consideration the first and then the second radical of 
the roots of the cited words. Yet the many sections and subsections into which 
Dīwān al-adab is divided based on morphological grounds554 dictated that the 
one who seeks a certain word first should determine under which verbal or 
nominal pattern to look, and then alphabetically search for the radical of that 
word within the chapter devoted to that pattern. Ǧawharī’s greatest contribu-
tion is that he adopted in the arrangement of roots a single criterion, namely, 
the sequence of their radicals, starting (as did Fārābī) with the last, then the 
first, and then the intermediate radicals (e.g. 3 + 1 + 2 in triliterals; 4 + 1 + 2 + 3 
550	
Blachère (1975: 27–28).
551	
Zabīdī, Tāǧ, Introduction I, 13.
552	
Note that Zabīdī (ibid., loc. cit.) points out that Ǧawharī’s condition of correctness for 
including material in al-Ṣaḥāḥ is equivalent (naẓīr) to Buḫārī’s method in his Ṣaḥīḥ.
553	
Cf. above, 363–366.
554	
Cf. above, 262–263.

---

<!-- page 384 -->

374
chapter 3
in quadriliterals). Like Fārābī he ignored the taqālīb used in some earlier 
works, such as K. al-ʿAyn and al-Ǧamhara; he also neither took into consider-
ation the length of the root (like several earlier lexica did) nor the morphologi-
cal patterns of words (as did Fārābī). The adoption of a single principle in this 
arrangement was a huge step forward since it greatly facilitated search for the 
required roots. Thus in order to determine the location in the lexicon of a given 
word, the only morphological skill required from the user is to determine its 
root. The confusion in some earlier lexica in the arrangement of reduplicated, 
unsound and hamzated roots, or in the listing of quadriliterals and quinquelit-
erals under one section, was totally avoided due to this new system. Evidently, 
the fact that al-Ṣaḥāḥ is so practical to use in comparison with earlier lexica 
satisfied a substantial need that they did not address satisfactorily, and this 
explains the huge popularity that it enjoyed for several centuries.
It is very likely that Ǧawharī was influenced in the arrangement of his 
lexicon by Fārābī, who was both his maternal uncle and one of his principal 
­teachers.555 Although Ǧawharī in the few lines that make up his introduction 
does not mention his uncle, it is difficult to believe that he independently 
invented the rhyme system in its full form knowing that it is systematically 
applied in Dīwān al-adab, albeit within each section on its own. That he was 
familiar with the system of arrangement of Dīwān al-adab is certain since 
Yāqūt (d. 626/1229) mentions that he saw in Tibrīz a copy of it in Ǧawharī’s 
own handwriting,556 and since close comparison between it and al-Ṣaḥāḥ 
reveals that he often borrows word for word from his uncle’s book, but without 
any acknowledgement.557 Accordingly, Ǧawharī’s claim that the arrangement 
of al-Ṣaḥāḥ is unprecedented (ʿalā tartīb lam usbaq ilayhi)558 most probably 
refers to the adoption of the order of letters as the only criterion in arrange-
ment, and not to the consideration, following the last radical, of the other radi-
cals of the root. Credit for the latter should go to Fārābī, as far as our knowledge 
of the sources goes. True, Fārābī’s previously quoted statement in which he 
describes the taṣnīf of his lexicon as unprecedented559 may be a reference to 
its arrangement of morphological patterns, but it may alternatively refer to the 
arrangement of roots from last to first to middle.
al-Ṣaḥāḥ consists of twenty-eight abwāb or sections, the first twenty-seven 
of which are arranged from ʾ to w/y (the last two in one bāb), whereas the last is

Ibn al-Anbārī, Nuzha 252; Ṣafadī, Wāfī IX, 112.
556	
Yāqūt, Muʿǧam II, 658.
557	
Cf. ʿUmar (1995: 151–160, esp. 158).
558	
Ǧawharī, Ṣaḥāḥ I, 33.
559	
See above, 262, 363.

<!-- page 385 -->

375
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
devoted to particles, such as ā, iḏā and ilā, which end in a long alif (apparently 
listed separately because Ǧawharī did not find it morphologically sensible to 
include them in the chapter on w/y, which he devoted to words whose final 
alif or hamza is originally w or y). The abwāb themselves are divided into fuṣūl 
or chapters based on the first radical of the root. For example, the section of 
b starts with the chapter of ʾ (e.g. ʾBB), followed by b (e.g. BWB), t (e.g. TRB), 
ṯ (e.g. ṮʾB), etc. Although the bāb before last lists together roots ending with 
either w or y, Ǧawharī lists w before y as well as h at the level of fuṣūl and roots 
therein (e.g. WʾB, WǦB . . . HBB, HDB . . . YBB, YLB; LWN, LHN, LYN; MWN, MHN, 
MYN; but surprisingly RHB, RWB, RYB). In hamzated and defective (i.e. final 
weak) roots, Ǧawharī upholds the principle of considering the last letter, fol-
lowed by the first and the second, unlike Fārābī who exceptionally arranges 
these roots based on their second radical and followed by the first given that 
they share their last (i.e. hamza in hamzated roots and a weak letter in defec-
tive ones). Thus, in the defective roots of the pattern faʿala/yafʿulu in Dīwān 
al-adab, the roots ṢBW and ḌBW occur before RTW and ŠTW560 because the 
last letter is not considered, but by systematically applying the rhyme system, 
Ǧawharī restores the order to RTW, ŠTW, ṢBW, ḌBW.
Within the lemmata, Ǧawharī either begins with verbs or nouns, but tends 
to separate between verbal and nominal patterns. There is also marked orga-
nization in the presentation of the derivatives, e.g. the perfect is followed by 
the imperfect, the verbal noun(s), and assimilate adjective (ṣifa mušabbaha); 
the singular by its dual and plural; and transitivity and intransitivity are noted. 
Similar features are encountered in various degrees in earlier lexica, such as 
al-ʿAyn and al-Ǧamhara,561 and although al-Ṣaḥāḥ on the whole is somewhat 
more systematic than earlier works in its internal organization, it lacks a rigor-
ous system of organizing data that would help the user find the required word 
without often having to read the whole lemma.
In addition to its arrangement, al-Ṣaḥāḥ also acquired its reputation from 
its inclusion only of what its author deemed to be correct or authentic usage, 
as its very title indicates. In the initial sentence of his short introduction, 
Ǧawharī indirectly justifies the title of his lexicon by referring to the correct-
ness of its material (mā ṣaḥḥa ʿindī min al-luġa).562 To give credence to his 
claim, he asserts towards the end of that introduction that he conversed with 
the pure or genuine Arabs in their desert abodes (wa-mušāfahatī bihā l-ʿArab 
al-ʿāriba fī diyārihim bi-l-bādiya). If his claim is true, then Ǧawharī is one of few 
560	
Fārābī, Dīwān IV (pt. 1), 70–71.
561	
Cf. above, 298, 343–344.
562	
Ǧawharī, Ṣaḥāḥ I, 33.

<!-- page 386 -->

376
chapter 3
philologists who have derived linguistic data from the Aʿrāb at this relatively 
late period, i.e. towards the end of epochs of reliable usage (ʿuṣūr al-iḥtiǧāǧ).563 
Reports have it that he traveled to Ḥiǧāz and to the abodes of Muḍar and Rabīʿa 
in pursuit of linguistic usage by the Aʿrāb.564 But, unlike his contemporary, 
Azharī (d. 370/981), who frequently quotes material he directly heard from 
the Aʿrāb,565 Ǧawharī only rarely quotes such material. One example appears 
in the lemma NḪS, in which he mentions that he initiated a conversation in 
Naǧd with an Aʿrābī of the Tamīm tribe in order to establish whether niḫās or 
niḥās is the correct word for the small piece of wood inserted in the groove of 
a ­pulley.566 Ǧawharī’s other sources of data include, in addition to the mono-
graphs authored by early philologists of the second/eighth and third/ninth 
centuries, earlier muǧannas lexica – in particular K. al-ʿAyn, al-Ǧamhara and 
al-Tahḏīb – from which he derives much of his data, but mostly without speci-
fying the source.
Other than the brief reference to the pure Arabs in the introduction, 
Ǧawharī does not mention any criterion according to which he identifies usage 
as ṣaḥīḥ. Unfortunately, his introduction is devoid of the sort of critique one 
finds in al-Tahḏīb, particularly Azharī’s comments on the reliability of some 
material included by earlier lexicographers. Suyūṭī nevertheless tries to justify 
al-Ṣaḥāḥ’s title by saying that Ǧawharī was the first author to include in his 
lexicon ṣaḥīḥ material to the exclusion of what is not ṣaḥīḥ (iltazama l-ṣaḥīḥ 
muqtaṣiran ʿalayhi),567 but still fails to point out any criterion for Ǧawharī’s 
distinction between the two types. Ǧawharī’s reliance in his puristic approach 
on his own judgment, rather than clear objective criteria, is probably the 
reason for the charges laid against him of excessively eliminating roots and 
words from his lexicon – so much so that Ṣaġānī’s (d. 650/1252) supplement 
al-Takmila wa-l-ḏayl wa-l-ṣila runs into six large volumes, and that Ibn Manẓūr 
(d. 711/1311) describes al-Ṣaḥāḥ as a drop in the ocean of language.568 For his 
part, Fīrūzābādī (d. 817/1415) estimates Ǧawharī’s excluded material to be half 
the language or more.569 Lexicographers after Ǧawharī mostly favored author-
ing extensive lexica to Ǧawharī’s method of limiting the material to ṣaḥīḥ 
usage – not a surprising development given that, several centuries after ʿuṣūr

---

<!-- page 387 -->

377
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
al-iḥtiǧāǧ, such authors were no longer in a position to judge correctness other 
than through reliance on much earlier authors. Accordingly, the 5,618 roots 
which al-Ṣaḥāḥ comprises are considerably fewer than the 9,273 in al-Lisān 
and 11,978 in al-Tāǧ.570
As an aid to the user, Ǧawharī – like his predecessor Qālī (d. 356/967) in 
al-Bāriʿ571 – specifies the correct vowels or cites their patterns, wherever this 
is deemed necessary for correct reading. For example, raǧibtuhu bi-l-kasr indi-
cates the vowel of the second radical of the verb; yaḥriṣu bi-l-kasr indicates 
the distinguishing vowel of the imperfect (i.e. ʿayn al-muḍāriʿ); and al-kalab 
bi-l-taḥrīk indicates that the second radical is not quiescent. At times, vow-
els are meant to differentiate between intransitive and transitive verbs of the 
same root – as in qaruba l-šayʾu bi-l-ḍamm (for the intransitive) and qaribtuhu 
bi-l-kasr (for the transitive) – or between nouns that differ in one vowel only – as 
in al-huyām bi-l-ḍamm, al-hayām bi-l-fatḥ, and al-hiyām bi-l-kasr. That a verb is 
in the passive form may also be pointed out, following its fully vocalized from, 
by the expression ʿalā mā lam yusamma fāʿiluhu (cf. buṭina l-raǧulu). Nominal 
patterns on the other hand may be specified either by sighting their morpho-
logical measure (wazn), e.g. ḫiṭʾa ʿalā fiʿla and al-tasyār tafʿāl min al-sayr, or by 
citing another word of the same pattern, e.g. al-hilbawṯ miṯāl al-firdaws and 
širrīr miṯāl fissīq. Furthermore, to avoid taṣḥīf, letters with diacritical marks 
may be described, as in tahatta⁠ʾa l-ṯawbu . . . bi-l-tāʾ muʿǧama bi-nuqṭatayni 
min fawq. Among other features of al-Ṣaḥāḥ is its author’s interest in mor-
phological and syntactical issues. This is hardly unexpected from a scholar 
among whose most important teachers were the renowned grammarians Sīrāfī 
(d. 368/979) and Abū ʿAlī al-Fārisī (d. 377/987).572 In fact, the last chapter, 
which is devoted to the soft alif (al-alif al-layyina) deals with the grammatical 
peculiarities of a number of particles that end with a long alif. The lexicon also 
includes ample dialectal variants as well as material on muʿarrab (Arabized 
words), aḍdād (words with two contradictory meanings), itbāʿ (alliteration), 
and proper nouns.
The immense impact that al-Ṣaḥāḥ left on the lexicographical tradition 
is best demonstrated by the large number of supplements, commentaries, 
abridgements and rearrangements it inspired (not to mention translations 
570	
Cf. Mūsā & Šāhīn (1973: 9); Mūsā (1989–1990; 149); see also above, 357, n. 478. Zabīdī’s 
estimate (cf. Tāǧ I, 23) that al-Ṣaḥāḥ contains 40,000 mādda thus most probably refers to 
the number of lexical items, rather than roots or lemmata. For Ǧawharī’s poetry šawāhid, 
see Ǧawharǧī (1432 A.H.).
571	
Cf. above, 310.
572	
Ibn al-Anbārī, Nuzha 252; Yāqūt, Muʿǧam II, 656; Ṣafadī, Wāfī IX, 112.

<!-- page 388 -->

378
chapter 3
into Turkish and Persian). The number of such works in ʿAṭṭār’s list is ninety-
seven,573 and we shall briefly examine the most important of the ones that 
have survived. But it is interesting first to note that, according to two riwāyas 
reported by Yāqūt, Ǧawharī reviewed the draft of al-Ṣaḥāḥ up to the letter ḍād 
only574 (i.e. less than half of the lexicon). According to one of the two riwāyas, 
the blatant errors that occur in the letters after ḍād are to be blamed on the 
copyist, Ibrāhīm b. Ṣāliḥ al-Warrāq. This conclusion is reminiscent of earlier 
reports that mistakes in K. al-ʿAyn are not Ḫalīl’s responsibility since the lexi-
con’s material was supplied by his disciples, and after that faulty copies came 
into circulation.575 Such claims probably emanate from a desire both to spare 
highly esteemed scholars harsh criticism and to relieve critics of the embar-
rassment of ascribing error to them. In the case of al-Ṣaḥāḥ, these claims did 
not deter critics from pointing out mistakes that occur in the earlier parts of the 
lexicon, which was supposedly reviewed by Ǧawharī, as is clear in the works 
of Ibn Barrī, Ṣaġānī and Ṣafadī that are discussed below. More importantly, the 
fact that al-Ṣaḥāḥ was the focus of so many commentaries and abridgements – 
which sensibly followed its system of arrangement so as to facilitate the com-
parison between the original text and the subsequent ones – certainly contrib-
uted to the rhyme arrangement becoming the standard system in muǧannas 
lexica for the rest of the medieval period.
The two most important works that are based on al-Ṣaḥāḥ are Ibn Barrī’s 
(d. 582/1187) al-Tanbīh wa-l-īḍāḥ ʿammā waqaʿa fī l-Ṣaḥāḥ, better known as 
Ḥawāšī/Amālī Ibn Barrī, and Ṣaġānī’s (d. 650/1252) al-Takmila wa-l-ḏayl wa-l-
ṣila. The importance of the former work is largely due to the fact that it is one 
of the five sources from which Ibn Manẓūr (d. 711/1311) derived the material 
of Lisān al-ʿArab. According to Ṣafadī (d. 764/1363), Ibn Barrī only reached the 
entry WQŠ (in his estimation, one quarter of the whole book), and the rest was 
then authored by ʿAbdallāh b. Muḥammad al-Basṭī (d. after 622/1225).576 The 
text that reached us indeed ends with WQŠ, but Ibn Manẓūr persists in quoting 
the Ḥawāšī and attributing the quotations to Ibn Barrī till the end of al-Lisān. 
It is therefore likely that Ibn Barrī authored the whole book,577 although one 
cannot exclude the possibility that Ibn Manẓūr might have only conveniently 
referred to it, after the letter š, as Ibn Barrī’s Ḥawāšī or Amālī, rather than 
573

574

See further evidence for the validity of this argument in the editor’s introduction to Ibn 
Barrī, Tanbīh I, 9–11.

<!-- page 389 -->

379
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
­mention Basṭī (whom he does not mention in the introduction either) after 
having recurrently mentioned Ibn Barrī in earlier parts. This matter aside, 
al-Ḥawāšī is arranged in the same manner as al-Ṣaḥāḥ and focuses primarily 
on the attribution, amendment and elucidation of its šawāhid as well as on 
what Ibn Barrī deems to be incorrect explanations of certain words or faulty 
grammatical interpretations. Ṣaġānī in al-Takmila shares Ibn Barrī’s concern 
for the šawāhid, particularly in correcting their riwāya or amending their 
taṣḥīf (alteration of diacritical points). But Ṣaġānī’s most important contribu-
tion in this lexicon is the inclusion of lemmata ignored by Ǧawharī as well as 
lexical items not cited in other lemmata included by him. In this, he preceded 
Fīrūzābādī (d. 817/1415), one of whose primary concerns in al-Qāmūs al-muḥīṭ 
was to include material left out by Ǧawharī. A host of lemmata in al-Takmila’s 
six large volumes begin by the expression ahmalahu l-Ǧawharī, followed by the 
relevant lexical items(s). To achieve the purpose of supplementing al-Ṣaḥāḥ, 
Ṣaġānī says that he consulted more than one thousand works. Of these, he 
names several mubawwab lexica and works on philology and literature, as 
well as most of the earlier muǧannas lexica, namely, K. al-ʿAyn, K. al-Ǧīm, 
al-Ǧamhara, al-Tahḏīb, al-Muḥīṭ, al-Maqāyīs and al-Muǧmal. In another lexi-
con of his, Maǧmaʿ al-baḥrayn, Ṣaġānī combined the text of al-Ṣaḥāḥ with the 
amendments and supplements of his own al-Takmila and added to them his 
other work, Mā ahmalahu l-Ǧawharī min al-luġa, which contained material 
ignored by Ǧawharī and recorded by Ṣaġānī on the margins of al-Takmila.578 As 
for Barmakī’s (d. 433/1041) al-Muntahā fī l-luġa, it has already been mentioned 
above.579
Better known as a biographer, Ṣalāḥ al-Dīn al-Ṣafadī (d. 764/1363) authored 
two books related to al-Ṣaḥāḥ. The first, Ġawāmiḍ al-Ṣaḥāḥ, is quite interesting 
because it highlights one of the major shortcomings of al-Ṣaḥāḥ (and indeed 
the lexicographical tradition as a whole), namely, that lexical items are often 
difficult to find because they are assigned roots that the inept user may not be 
able to guess. As if to put himself on an equal footing with the user who has 
no expertise in determining which letters of the word are radicals (uṣūl) and 
which are augments (zawāʾid), Ṣafadī confesses his ignorance of the morpho-
logical skills required to allocate some of al-Ṣaḥāḥ’s lexical items,580 although 
his erudite introductory chapter on ḥurūf attests to the contrary. Accordingly, 
he lists these items (which include many proper nouns of foreign origin) based 
on the succession of their letters (i.e. not supposed roots). He ­interestingly 
578

579	
See above, 357–358.
580	
Ṣafadī, Ġawāmiḍ 47.

<!-- page 390 -->

380
chapter 3
begins with the first letter, perhaps because he might have thought that this 
would be an easier mode of arrangement for the reader. Words such as abābīl, 
Abraha, ibrīsam, Ibrāhīm, utruǧǧa and usṭuwāna are thus listed under hamza, 
and not under ʾBL, BRH, BRSM, BRHM, TRǦ, and SṬN as in al-Ṣaḥāḥ. Similarly, 
kunādir and kunduš are listed in al-Ġawāmiḍ based on the succession of all 
their radicals, and not under KDR and KDŠ, since the reader may not assume 
that n is an augment in both. Many words beginning with m are also placed 
under that letter since they may be difficult to find in al-Ṣaḥāḥ, as in māristān, 
māwiyya, maṯānī, marzubān and mumānāt, which Ǧawharī places under MRS, 
MWH, ṮNY, RZB and MNW. Ṣafadī’s other book, Nufūḏ al-sahm fī mā waqaʿa 
li-l-Ǧawharī min al-wahm, as he explains in its introduction, is a by-product of 
al-Ġawāmiḍ and another book of his, titled Ḥulā l-nawāhid ʿalā mā fī l-Ṣaḥāḥ 
min al-šawāhid. This is because, in authoring the latter two works, he came 
across numerous errors in al-Ṣaḥāḥ, which he then collected in Nufūḏ al-
sahm.581 Surviving manuscripts of the book end with the letter q,582 and it is 
quite likely that Ṣafadī did not finish authoring it. More concise than Ibn Barrī’s 
Ḥawāšī, as Ṣafadī himself says in the introduction, the book records mistakes 
of Ǧawharī’s in morphology, explanation of lexical items, riwāya of poetry, and 
taṣḥīf, and frequently draws on Ibn Barrī’s criticism of Ǧawharī.
The criticism to which it was subjected notwithstanding, al-Ṣaḥāḥ was gen-
erally very highly appreciated by later scholars. Ǧawharī’s approach of elimi-
nating what he could not establish to be correct meant that its material was 
considerably shorter than many earlier lexica. This, as well as the ease of its 
use due to the adoption of one single criterion for arrangement, certainly 
encouraged lexicographers to produce abridgements which could in turn 
enjoy the popularity of the original work, perhaps at a wider scale since these 
were addressed to a broader base of users, those who require a reference that 
is both reliable and easily searchable. Among the numerous abridgements of 
which we know (cf. the seventeen titles listed by ʿAṭṭār),583 Muḥammad b. ʿAbī 
Bakr al-Rāzī’s (d. after 666/1268) Muḫtār al-Ṣaḥāḥ is by far the most famous. 
Published several times in a single volume, al-Muḫtār naturally cuts down on 
the number of lexical items in al-Ṣaḥāḥ (particularly uncommon usage and 
proper nouns) as well as on the names of cited philologists and on šawāhid. Yet 
Rāzī also added material into it, which he derived, as he says in the introduc-
tion, from Azharī’s al-Tahḏīb and other sources. He also reinstated the verbal 
581	
Ṣafadī, Nufūḏ 42.
582	
Cf. the editor’s introduction, 25.
583	
A. ʿAṭṭār (1990: 233–242; nos. 72–89, with the exclusion of 77, a contemporary arrangement 
of Muḫtār al-Ṣaḥāḥ); cf. Naṣṣār (1968: II, 503–511).

<!-- page 391 -->

381
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
nouns of the triliteral verbs and the patterns of triliteral perfect verbs, and, 
more often than Ǧawharī, noted the difference between transitive and intransi-
tive verbs.584 Rāzī’s book itself gave rise to other works, which either expanded 
some of its material, as did Muḥammad b. Ḥasan al-Adranawī (d. 866/1462) in 
al-Ǧāmiʿ and al-Rāmūz,585 or abridged it further, as did ʿAbdalraḥmān b. ʿĪsā 
al-Muršidī (d. 1037/1628) in Ṣafw al-rāḥ min Muḫtār al-Ṣaḥāḥ.
Contrary to al-Ṣaḥāḥ’s method, most later lexica, as previously noted, aimed 
at exhaustiveness although they followed its rhyme system of arrangement 
which only took into account the order of the radicals in the root. Accordingly, 
Ǧawharī’s influence on the tradition was primarily related to the formal aspect, 
rather than the essential feature of excluding incorrect or doubtful material, 
irrespective of the fact that he nowhere explains the bases on which he relied 
in the choice of his material. The reinstatement by later authors, for the sake 
of exhaustiveness, of the bulk of lexical items ignored by Ǧawharī, caused 
the distance between their works and contemporary usage to grow increas-
ingly wider.
4.3	
al-ʿUbāb al-zāḫir wa-l-lubāb al-fāḫir by Ṣaġānī (d. 650/1252)
Author of several mubawwab lexica encountered in Chapter 2 (e.g. al-Šawārid 
fī l-luġa, Fī asāmī l-ḏiʾb wa-kunāhu, Asmā l-ġāda fī asmāʾ al-ʿāda, al-Aḍdād, 
Naqʿat al-ṣadyān fī mā ǧāʾa ʿalā l-faʿalān, al-Infiʿāl, Yafʿūl, and Mā banathu 
l-ʿArab ʿalā faʿāli),586 Ṣaġanī authored, other than al-ʿUbāb, three muǧannas 
works based on Ǧawharī’s al-Ṣaḥāḥ, the most famous of which is al-Takmila 
wa-l-ḏayl wa-l-ṣila (discussed in the previous section). The most comprehen-
sive of the three, however, is Maǧmaʿ al-baḥrayn, which combines the texts 
of al-Ṣaḥāḥ and al-Takmila as well as that of his other work, Mā ahmalahu 
l-Ǧawharī min al-luġa.587 Yet al-ʿUbāb is certainly his greatest contribution to 
lexicography. It differs from his earlier lexica in that it is an independent work 
that does not take al-Ṣaḥāḥ as its starting point, although Ṣaġānī in authoring 
it benefited tremendously from his other lexica that had centered on al-Ṣaḥāḥ. 
More importantly, al-ʿUbāb is methodologically superior to Ṣaġānī’s earlier 
works, particularly as far as the arrangement of data within the lemmata is 
concerned. This comes as no surprise given that Ṣaġānī authored it towards 
the end of his rich scholarly life. In fact, he died having finished what could

(1968: II, 504–506); Aḥmad (1969: 91–97).

<!-- page 392 -->

382
chapter 3
be estimated as three-quarters of the material since is it reported that he only 
reached the root BKM.588
Ṣaġānī’s lengthy introduction to al-ʿUbāb is reminiscent of the introduc-
tion of Tahḏīb al-luġa on two counts: the survey of the sources and the critical 
approach to some of the earlier lexica. Following an alphabetical list of the 
most important philologists and lexicographers, Ṣaġānī gives a detailed list of 
the sources from which he derived al-ʿUbāb’s material.589 This is almost exactly 
the same list found in the ḏayl of al-Takmila,590 although Ṣaġānī does not men-
tion in al-ʿUbāb that his list comprises more than a thousand titles. In spite 
of its inclusion of a large number of references, the list may not be exhaus-
tive, and it has been demonstrated, for example, that Ṣaġānī copies in al-ʿUbāb 
material from Muʿǧam al-Buldān by his contemporary Yāqūt (d. 626/1229), 
without any acknowledgment.591 The abundance of sources used by Ṣaġānī is 
not surprising given that, at a time when the Bedouin fuṣaḥāʾ on whom earlier 
sources relied for correct usage were something of the distant past, the only 
recourse authors had in their task of checking the validity of data was the mas-
sive philological literature available to them.
That Ṣaġānī was dissatisfied with earlier lexica is clear in the initial part of his 
introduction where he points out that earlier authors often did not distinguish 
between prophetic Ḥadīṯ and the Aṯar ascribed to the Prophet’s Companions 
or Successors, and mistook proverbs for Ḥadīṯ and vice versa.592 His detailed 
critique of his predecessors, however, is deferred to the latter part of his intro-
duction.593 Other than Ibn al-Sikkīt’s (d. 244/858) mubawwab work Iṣlāḥ 
al-manṭiq – which itself needs iṣlāḥ (amendment), according to Ṣaġānī – four 
muǧannas lexica are discussed. Ironically enough, Azharī (d. 370/981), who has 
so harshly criticized earlier lexicographers, is the first author of a muǧannas 
lexicon to be criticized by Ṣaġānī, who claims that al-Tahḏīb contains more 
than a thousand instances of erroneous attribution of poetry šawāhid. Ṣaġānī’s 
criticism of al-Ṣaḥāḥ is more credible given his earlier lexica that are based on 
it. He alleges that it includes more than two thousand instances either of incor-
rect riwāya of poetry and Ḥadīṯ or of lexical items that are erroneously cited 
or vocalized. Despite this, he follows al-Ṣaḥāḥ in its system of arrangement. 
588	
Suyūṭī, Muzhir I, 100 & Buġya I, 520.
589	
Ṣaġānī, ʿUbāb (ed. Ḥasan) I, 7–9.
590

591

<!-- page 393 -->

383
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
al-Muǧmal by Ibn Fāris (d. 395/1004) and al-Muḥīṭ by al-Ṣāḥib b. ʿAbbād 
(d. 385/995) are also criticized on similar grounds. Although Ṣaġānī ends the 
introduction apologetically by showing gratitude to his predecessors from 
whose works he benefited and by blaming errors in their works on scribes, one 
cannot but see in this introduction an attempt at demonstrating the superi-
ority of his own book compared with previous works. In fact, Ṣaġānī himself 
declares that, whereas others may be accused of forgery (tazyīf ) or corruption 
(taṣḥīf and taḥrīf),594 he should be exempt from such criticism due to the scru-
tiny to which he subjected his sources.
Ṣaġānī obviously wanted al-ʿUbāb to be more exhaustive than earlier lex-
ica. In addition to listing an impressive number of works which he had at his 
disposal in authoring his lexicon, he clearly indicates in the beginning of its 
introduction that it brings together material that is dispersed in earlier works 
and encompasses the bulk of the speech of the Arabs (ʿāmmat mā naṭaqat bihi 
l-ʿArab), except for what is obsolete (mā ḏahaba bi-ḏahāb ahlihā) and what he 
describes as erratic and rare (al-šāḏḏ al-nādir).595 But later in the introduc-
tion, he boasts that his book – upon the request of its sponsor, Muḥammad b. 
Aḥmad Ibn al-ʿAlqamī (d. 656/1258) – encompasses even anomalous and obso-
lete material (ǧāmiʿan . . . šawāridahā . . . wa-awābidahā).596 The lexicon’s com-
prehensiveness is further highlighted by saying that his book is intended to 
leave nothing small or great without noting it down (wa-lā yuġādir minhā siwā 
l-muhmala ṣaġīra wa-lā kabīra illā wa-huwa yuḥṣīhā) – an exceptionally strong 
statement which is directly borrowed from the description of the Qurʾān in the 
sūra of al-Kahf (18: 49), and which, by excluding al-muhmala (obsolete lexical 
items), contradicts the inclusion of awābid mentioned in the same sentence. 
Irrespective of this contradiction, al-ʿUbāb is certainly one of the most com-
prehensive Arabic lexica, in spite of the fact that it is largely free from digres-
sion and from citing the various conflicting views of the philologists in riwāya 
and interpretation of meaning. Although it ends with the lemma BKM, as pre-
viously mentioned, it consists, according to Ṣafadī, of twenty volumes, whereas 
Maǧmaʿ al-baḥrayn (which combines the texts of al-Ṣaḥāḥ, al-Takmila and Mā 
ahmalahu l-Ǧawharī min al-luġa) is in twelve volumes only.597 A quick com-
parison between al-Takmila and al-ʿUbāb readily reveals that although the lat-
ter does not contain many more lemmata than the former, it visibly expands 
the material within each lemma. For example, in the letter ġ, Ṣaġānī only adds

<!-- page 394 -->

384
chapter 3
to al-ʿUbāb six lemmata that do not feature in al-Takmila, namely, ǦLĠ, DNĠ, 
ḌMĠ, ĠWĠ, NHBĠ and HBĠ.598 He also splits ṢWĠ (which includes ṢYĠ as well 
in al-Takmila) to ṢWĠ and ṢYĠ in al-ʿUbāb. But by comparing the contents 
of the lemmata, one easily discovers how much broader in scope al-ʿUbāb is 
compared with al-Takmila. To cite but one example, the lemma BDĠ in al-Tak-
mila includes only two nominal forms, i.e. badiġ and badaġ, and the verb of 
the fourth form, abdaġa. In al-ʿUbāb, on the other hand, the lemma includes 
the following nominal forms: badaġ, badiġ, Badiġ/Bidġ (proper noun), badġ, 
badāġa, bidġ and Abdaġ (place name), and the verbal forms badiġa, baduġa 
and abdaġa. In several cases, the patterns of the words are indicated (e.g. badiġ 
bi-wazn katif ) in order to ensure proper reading. The lemma also contains a 
poetry šāhid attributed to Ruʾba, quotations from several philologists includ-
ing Layṯ, Ibn Durayd and Ibn al-Aʿrābī, as well as the full text of the lemma as 
it appears in Ibn Fāris’s al-Maqāyīs. As noted earlier, Ṣaġānī very frequently 
ends the lemmata by citing the views of Ibn Fāris on the uṣūl of triliteral roots 
or simply pointing out the basic meaning of the tarkīb, i.e. the sequence of 
radicals forming the root.599
Perhaps the most significant feature of al-ʿUbāb – which Ṣaġānī surpris-
ingly does not mention in the introduction – is the internal organization of 
the lemmata. This is clearly attested in Ṣaġānī’s systematic separation between 
the verb in its ground or unaugmented form and the other verbal patterns. 
The same principle is also applied to nominal forms. Hence, the ones that 
are related to the ground form of the verb are listed with it, and those that 
are derived from augmented verbs are cited with their corresponding verbal 
forms. For example, the lemma FRĠ may be divided into six sections based on 
the verbal patterns and the nouns associated with them. They appear in the 
following order, starting with the ground form; (1) faraġa/fariġa/faruġa, and 
the nouns fariġ, fāriġ, farāġ, furūġ, farġ, Farġāna, Farġān, Afrāġ, Afrāġa (place 
names), Farġān (proper noun), farīġa, farīġ, firāġ, afriġa, farāġa, firġ, furrāġ, 
afraġ and farġāʾ; (2) afraġa (nouns: ifrāġ, mufraġ); (3) farraġa (nouns: tafrīġ; 
Mufarriġ [proper noun]); (4) istafraġa (nouns: mustafriġ, istifrāġ); (5) iftaraġa; 
and (6) tafarraġa. The order of augmented verbs, however, varies from one 
lemma to another. Compared with FRĠ above, the order in FQʾ and NBʾ is as fol-
lows: afqa⁠ʾa, tafaqqa⁠ʾa, infaqa’a, faqqa’a and nabba⁠ʾa, anba⁠ʾa, tanabba⁠ʾa, nāba⁠ʾa, 
598	
Contrary to the norm, the root ŠZĠ occurs in al-Takmila but not al-ʿUbāb. It should be 
noted, however, that the only word under that root, namely, šazġ (frog), is mentioned as 
a variant of šarġ in the lemma ŠRĠ in al-ʿUbāb. Because of the possibility of taṣḥīf, Ṣaġānī 
seems to have cited šazġ with šarġ and not in a separate lemma.
599	
Cf. above, 356.
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 395 -->

385
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
istanba⁠ʾa respectively. Nouns are generally not listed in any specific order 
within each section of the lemma. But despite such inconsistencies, Ṣaġānī’s 
separation between the ground form and the nouns related to it on the one 
hand, and the augmented verbs and their corresponding nouns on the other is 
a major step in the arrangement of material and certainly facilitates the search 
for the required lexical items within each lemma. This aspect of al-ʿUbāb is 
most probably related to Ṣaġānī’s interest in the study of patterns (witness 
his monographs dealing with the patterns faʿalān, infiʿāl, yafʿūl and faʿāli). It 
is noteworthy that, prior to Ṣaġānī, Ibn Sīda in the introduction of al-Muḥkam 
stressed the importance of introducing the ground form before the augmented 
verbs, although he did not strictly adhere to this condition, and his lemmata 
are often disorganized in this respect.600 One would have only wished that, 
having come so close to the systematic ordering of verbal and nominal pat-
terns, Ṣaġānī had adopted a fixed order which would guide the user in locating 
each pattern, particularly in lengthy lemmata.
Several later authors did not fail to appreciate Ṣaġānī’s effort in authoring 
al-ʿUbāb and thus ranked it among the best lexica in the whole tradition. It 
is quite telling that Fīrūzābādī (d. 817/1415) and Suyūṭī (d. 911/1505) single out 
al-ʿUbāb and al-Muḥkam for their ultimate praise. Fīrūzābādī says that before 
authoring al-Qāmūs he started to work on al-Lāmiʿ al-muʿlam al-ʿuǧāb al-ǧāmiʿ 
bayna l-Muḥkam wa-l-ʿUbāb, in which he intended to combine the two ear-
lier lexica, given that they were the best in the genre.601 For his part, Suyūṭī 
describes al-Muḥkam and al-ʿUbāb as the best two lexica authored after the era 
of Ǧawharī.602 The most obvious reason for the link established by Fīrūzābādī 
and Suyūṭī between these two lexica is their exhaustiveness. Another reason 
may well be the system of arrangement of material in both, albeit in the case 
of al-Muḥkam largely at the theoretical level advanced in its introduction, in 
contrast to al-ʿUbāb, whose internal arrangement represents a landmark in the 
lexicographical tradition.
4.4	
Lisān al-ʿArab by Ibn Manẓūr (d. 711/1311)
With the exception of the much later Tāǧ al-ʿarūs by Zabīdī (d. 1205–1790), 
Lisān al-ʿArab, which was completed in 689/1290,603 is the most comprehen-
sive Arabic lexicon ever authored. In this respect, it stands in sharp contrast 
to other works by Ibn Manẓūr (also known as Ibn Mukarram al-Ifrīqī al-Miṣrī), 
600	
Ibn Sīda, Muḥkam I, 39; cf. above, 329.
601	
Fīrūzābādī, Qāmūs, Introduction I, 3.
602	
Suyūṭī, Muzhir I, 100.
603	
The date is recorded by Ibn Manẓūr at the end of al-Lisān XV, 494.
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 396 -->

386
chapter 3
which typically are abridgements of voluminous literary books. So fond was Ibn 
Manẓūr of epitomizing earlier works that Ṣafadī (d. 764/1363) said that he was 
hardly aware of any literary work that Ibn Manẓūr did not epitomize, including 
al-Aġānī, Zahr al-ādāb, al-Ḥayawān, al-Yatīma, al-Ḏaḫīra, Nišwār al-muḥāḍara, 
Mufradāt Ibn al-Bayṭār and Tārīḫ Ibn ʿAsākir.604 Ibn Manẓūr certainly makes 
no claim of originality in authoring al-Lisān for he says in its introduction that 
the responsibility for the material, be it sound or defective, rests with the origi-
nal author from whom it was derived ( fa-ʿuhdatuhu ʿalā l-muṣannif al-awwal).605 
Yet he proudly states that al-Lisān, by comprising five major lexica, assumes 
the status of source (aṣl) whereas its original components may be viewed as 
subsidiaries ( furūʿ). In retrospect, Ibn Manẓūr’s statement has proven to be 
quite precise since al-Lisān overshadowed most earlier lexica and became the 
most authoritative and comprehensive source of classical Arabic usage.
The five sources which Ibn Manẓūr incorporated into al-Lisān are, in the 
order in which they are mentioned in the introduction: Azharī’s (d. 370/981) 
al-Tahḏīb, Ibn Sīda’s (d. 458/1066) al-Muḥkam (the only Maġribī source of the 
five), Ǧawharī’s (d. c. 400/1010) al-Ṣaḥāḥ, Ibn Barrī’s (d. 582/1187) al-Ḥawāšī (i.e. 
al-Tanbīh wa-l-īḍāḥ), and Ibn al-Aṯīr’s (d. 606/1210) al-Nihāya fī ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ 
wa-l-Aṯar. In evaluating these works, particularly the first three, Ibn Manẓūr 
refers to two basic notions, namely, inclusion of material (ǧamʿ) and system of 
arrangement (waḍʿ) – both notions obviously being behind the choice of his 
sources. On the one hand, he notes that from the perspective of ǧamʿ, al-Tahḏīb 
and al-Muḥkam are unrivalled in their comprehensiveness, but when it comes 
to waḍʿ, they are extremely difficult to use, and hence people have abandoned 
them to the extent that their manuscripts became almost nonexistent. The 
reason cited by Ibn Manẓūr for the difficulty characteristic of the two works 
is the confusion in dealing with certain morphological aspects (as in lafīf and 
defective roots). But although no specific mention is made either of their pho-
netic arrangement or taqālīb, it is implied, given the contrast with al-Ṣaḥāḥ, 
that these two aspects largely contribute to the complexity of both works. 
al-Ṣaḥāḥ, on the other hand, is commended by Ibn Manẓūr for the simplicity 
of its arrangement (suhūlat waḍʿihi), but criticized for its conciseness (cf. Ibn 
Manẓūr’s reference to it as muḫtaṣar instead of citing its title). These three lex-
ica are obviously the ones that Ibn Manẓūr had in mind in stating that authors 
who succeeded in ǧamʿ failed in waḍʿ, and vice versa. Furthermore, Ibn Barrī’s 
al-Ḥawāšī is included because it amends some of Ǧawharī’s mistakes, whereas 
the inclusion of al-Nihāya is evidently motivated by Ibn Manẓūr’s desire to 
604	
Ṣafadī, Wāfī V, 56; cf. Ibn Ḥaǧar, Durar IV, 263; Suyūṭī, Buġya I, 248.
605	
Ibn Manẓūr, Lisān, Introduction I, 8.

<!-- page 397 -->

387
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
incorporate the corpus of Ḥadīṯ into his lexicon. It is also noteworthy that Ibn 
Manẓūr did not include among his sources one of the most comprehensive 
lexica, namely, Ṣaġānī’s (d. 650/1252) al-ʿUbāb, but it is difficult to determine 
whether he intentionally did so or did not know of al-ʿUbāb in the first place.
It goes without saying that Ibn Manẓūr’s five sources had in turn derived 
much of their material from earlier works, as noted in our previous discus-
sions. This is most obvious in Ibn Manẓūr’s recurrent reference, based on 
these sources, to early philologists and grammarians, as well as to authors 
of both mubawwab and muǧannas lexica. In almost every lemma in al-Lisān 
one encounters the names of early scholars such as Abū ʿAmr b. al-ʿAlāʾ 
(d. 154/770), Ḫalīl (d. 175/791), Sībawayhi (d. 180/796), Kisāʾī (d. 189/805), al-Layṯ 
b. al-Muẓaffar (d. 190/805), ʿAlī b. al-Mubārak al-Liḥyānī (fl. in the second half 
of the second/eighth century), Farrāʾ (d. 207/822), Abū ʿUbayda (d. 209/824), 
Abū Zayd al-Anṣārī (d. 215/830), Aṣmaʿī (d. 216/813), Abū ʿUbayd (d. 224/838), 
Ibn al-Aʿrābī (d. 231/845), Ibn al-Sikkīt (d. 244/858), Abū Ḥanīfa al-Dīnawarī 
(d. 282/895), Ṯaʿlab (d. 291/904), Kurāʿ al-Naml (d. 310/922), Zaǧǧāǧ (d. 311/923), 
Ibn Durayd (d. 321/933), etc. In the case of al-Ǧamhara, it was often mistakenly 
thought to be among Ibn Manẓūr’s primary sources – a claim that is most prob-
ably attributable to the frequent quotations from Ibn Durayd in al-Lisān.606
The number of roots (hence lemmata) in al-Lisān is 9,273, second only to 
al-Tāǧ, which contains 11,978 roots.607 Compared to most other lexica (again 
with the exception of al-Tāǧ), the content of its lemmata is much more exten-
sive and elaborate. This is obviously due to Ibn Manẓūr’s keenness on incor-
porating into his lexicon the bulk of lexical items, explanations and šawāhid 
which his sources contain. As a result, various conflicting views in the expla-
nation of a single lexical item are often quoted with the supporting evidence 
in each case. Given that ǧamʿ was one of Ibn Manẓūr’s two principal criteria 
for authoring a lexicon that surpasses earlier ones in its comprehensiveness, 
it is not surprising that he chose for his sources some of the most extensive 
lexica in the tradition. This certainly applies to al-Tahḏīb and al-Muḥkam, 
and, though to a lesser extent, al-Ṣaḥāḥ, whose material is supplemented by 
Ibn Barrī’s al-Ḥawāšī. As for al-Nihāya fī ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ, its very title is indic-
ative of its being intended to amass more material than any earlier work in 
its genre.608 It is important to note here that famous, though significantly 
606

contains 80,000 mādda – most probably a reference to lexical items, and not to roots or 
lemmata; cf. above, 377, n. 570, and below, 401.

<!-- page 398 -->

388
chapter 3
shorter, lexica – such as Ibn Fāris’s (d. 395/1004) al-Muǧmal and al-Maqāyīs 
and Zamaḫšarī’s (d. 538/1144) Asās al-balāġa – were most probably excluded 
merely on the grounds of their relative conciseness.
As a result of Ibn Manẓūr’s plan, al-Lisān may truly be described as an ency-
clopedic dictionary which contains a vast amount of material related to fields 
such as Qurʾānic readings, exegesis, prophetic tradition, Ayyām al-ʿArab (Battle-
days of the Arabs), plants, animals, natural phenomena, grammar, morphology, 
prosody, rhetoric, proverbs, Arabized words, solecism, place names, proper 
nouns, etc. In addition to this, Ibn Manẓūr includes, immediately following 
his introduction, two chapters: one on the letters at the beginning of some 
Qurʾānic sūras (similarly discussed by Azharī in a separate chapter at the end 
of al-Tahḏīb)609 and another on the phonetic as well as medical and enigmatic 
characteristics of letters. Also indicative of al-Lisān’s comprehensiveness is the 
mention, at the beginning of the section devoted to a specific letter (i.e. ʾ, b, t, ṯ, 
etc.), its phonetic and often morphological characteristics. Although in certain 
cases this may be as short as one line (cf. the letter f ), it can extend over several 
pages in other letters (cf. hamza) and thus include more information than in 
the introductory chapter, referred to above.
The above features of comprehensiveness in al-Lisān notwithstanding, 
Ibn Manẓūr’s reliance in his corpus on no more than five sources necessarily 
means that al-Lisān would not normally contain any material (such as roots, 
lexical items and šawāhid) not recorded in these sources but found in others. 
It has previously been noted, for example, that many of K. al-ʿAyn’s šawāhid do 
not occur in any later lexicon,610 and this includes al-Lisān. A more flagrant 
example has to do indirectly with al-Ṣaḥāḥ, which, along with Ibn Barrī’s com-
mentary (i.e. al-Ḥawāšī), is among al-Lisān’s sources. Because Ibn Manẓūr 
did not include in his sources Ṣaġānī’s (d. 650/1252) extensive additions to 
al-Ṣaḥāḥ in his al-Takmila wa-l-ḏayl wa-l-ṣila, the Lisān is devoid of a large 
number of roots with which Ṣaġānī complemented al-Ṣaḥāḥ. To take the let-
ters ẓ and ʿ as examples, the following roots recorded in al-Takmila do not fea-
ture in al-Lisān:611 BNẒ, BWẒ, ǦMḤẒ, ǦMẒ, ǦMʿẒ, ǦYẒ, ḤRBẒ, ḤMẒ, ĠẒẒ, KRẒ, 
LʿẒ, LWẒ, MḤẒ, WḤẒ, ʾṮʿ, ʾʿʿ, ʾLʿ, BLKʿ, TRBʿ, TQʿ, TNʿ, ṮḪṬʿ, ǦSʿ, ǦNʿ, ḪTRʿ, ḪRŠʿ, 
ḪSʿ, ḎWʿ, ZDʿ, ZRBʿ, SQRFʿ, SLṬʿ, SMFʿ, ŠṬʿ, ʿKʿ, ʿHḪʿ, FRZʿ, FYʿ, QRḎʿ, QNṮʿ, QYʿ, 
KRFʿ, LBʿ, LṮʿ, LYʿ, HLMʿ and YṮʿ.
609

610

from al-Lisān, and at times from al-Tāǧ.

<!-- page 399 -->

389
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
For the arrangement of roots, Ibn Manẓūr’s five sources offered all three dif-
ferent models used in the tradition of muǧannas lexica, namely, the phonetic-
permutative (al-Tahḏīb and al-Muḥkam), the alphabetical (al-Nihāya), and the 
rhyme (al-Ṣaḥāḥ and al-Ḥawāšī) systems. He obviously avoided the first type 
due to its complexity, which he harshly criticized in the introduction, and thus 
effectively had the two other systems from which to choose. As far as al-Nihāya 
is concerned, Ibn Manẓūr notes that Ibn al-Aṯīr often did not list lexical items 
according to their roots, but took augments into consideration.612 Yet had Ibn 
Manẓūr been inclined to choose the alphabetical arrangement, it would have 
been easy to relocate such lexical items based on their roots. Accordingly, the 
reason for avoiding this choice must lie elsewhere. First of all, al-Nihāya is a spe-
cialized dictionary and includes only the roots of words which occur in Ḥadīṯ, 
and to supplement its material by roots taken from al-Ṣaḥāḥ and al-Ḥawāšī 
after rearranging them on first-letter basis would be significantly more strenu-
ous than the other way round. Theoretically, Ibn Manẓūr might have assessed 
the available muǧannas lexica that are arranged according to either the alpha-
betical or the rhyme system. If so, he could hardly have adopted al-Ǧamhara 
as a model since, despite its alphabetical arrangement for the most part, its 
structure makes it one of the most complicated lexica in the tradition and 
hence does not satisfy his criterion of waḍʿ. The other lexica, i.e. al-Maqāyīs, 
al-Muǧmal and al-Asās, are either specialized or too concise to be the starting 
point for a comprehensive lexicon. In contrast, the rhyme system must have 
represented a viable choice for Ibn Manẓūr primarily because it was adopted 
in several general and extensive lexica, such as Ṣaġānī’s works. Even al-Ṣaḥāḥ, 
whose conciseness is criticized by Ibn Manẓūr, is more extensive than most 
alphabetically arranged lexica, and is furthermore supplemented (primarily in 
its šawāhid, though not in its roots) by Ibn Barrī’s al-Ḥawāšī. Hence if indeed 
Ibn Manẓūr compared between the alphabetical and rhyme systems to deter-
mine which to adopt, the latter not only would have been more in line with 
the tradition of the longer lexica, but also easier to follow given that al-Ṣaḥāḥ, 
rather than the specialized al-Nihāya, could be the basis for listing the roots 
and then filling in the missing ones from the sources. In fact, Ibn Manẓūr 
follows al-Ṣaḥāḥ’s arrangement in almost every detail, including the lack of 
separation between radicals ending with w/y – a feature which he admits in 
the introduction of the chapter on w/y to be borrowed from Ǧawharī. Perhaps 
the only exception to Ibn Manẓūr’s adoption of Ǧawharī’s arrangement 
is that he places h before w at the level of chapters (fuṣūl) and roots (hence, 
for ­example, HBB, HDB . . . WʾB, WǦB . . . YBB, YLB; LHN, LWN, LYN; MHN, MWN, 
612	
Cf. above, 80–81.

<!-- page 400 -->

390
chapter 3
MYN).613 As far as content is concerned, the meanings of lexical items as well 
as the šawāhid, be they from Qurʾān, Ḥadīṯ, poetry, proverbs, dialects, etc., are 
derived in their entirety from Ibn Manẓūr’s five sources and thus bear the mark 
of these sources. This often leads to repetition as well as to contradictory or 
irreconcilable definitions. There are very few and far between additions by 
Ibn Manẓūr from other sources, and these at times are taken from comments 
recorded on the margins of manuscripts available to him.614 Contrastively, he 
frequently does not mention the names of philologists and narrators and omits 
digressions, such as those in which poetry šawāhid are explained at length.
Ibn Manẓūr’s reliance on his sources, however, should not deter one from 
acknowledging his efforts at organizing the content of his lemmata. Like his 
predecessors he does not adopt a unified method in listing lexical items (e.g. 
lemmata can begin either with verbs or nouns, and no specific order is followed 
in quoting nominal and verbal patterns), yet he does try to separate seman-
tically related lexical items within the lemma from other items in the same 
lemma. In this, he seems to be influenced primarily by Ibn Sīda in al-Muḥkam. 
To give but one example, lexical items in the lemma ʿQR are listed in groups 
or word families that have the following general meanings: (1) barrenness, e.g. 
ʿāqir (barren); ʿuqr (a drink that causes barrenness); ʿuqārāt al-ʿilm (barrenness 
of knowledge, i.e. forgetfulness); (2) wounding, e.g. ʿaqara (to wound); ʿaqīra 
(a cut leg); sarǧ miʿqār/miʿqar/muʿqir/ʿuqara/ʿuqar/ʿāqūr (a saddle that galls 
the back); kalb ʿaqūr (a dog that wounds); (3) abode or place, e.g. ʿuqr/ʿaqr (the 
principal part of a people’s abode); ʿuqr/ʿuqur (the hind part of a trough); nāqa 
ʿaqira (a she camel that drinks only from the hinder part of a trough); ʿaqār 
(real property); (4) holding to something, e.g. ʿāqara (to hold or stick to); ʿuqār 
(wine, said to take hold of the intellect or to be confined in the jar for a long 
time); muʿāqara (addiction); (5) medicinal plants, e.g. ʿaqqār, ʿiqqīr and ʿuqqār; 
(6) place names and proper nouns. The lemma contains a few anomalies – 
e.g. ʿaqr/ʿuqr (palace), which is not listed with the third group as one would 
expect, but after the fourth – and several isolated items that do not belong to 
any specific group – e.g. qaṣr (white cloud); otherwise it is distinctly divided 
into the above groups of meanings, and Ibn Manẓūr often explains or justifies 
the semantic relationship between words in the same group. By comparing 
al-Lisān with its sources, it becomes clear that the structure of this lemma is in 
line with its counterpart in al-Muḥkam,615 and that Ibn Manẓūr supplements 
613	
Compare the order in al-Ṣaḥāḥ as quoted above, 375.
614	
See, for example, KRK and ĠZW, where material is derived from the margins of the manu-
script of Ibn Barrī’s lexicon.
615	
Ibn Sīda, Muḥkam ʿQR (I, 182–187).
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
