---

<!-- page 401 -->

391
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
the text of the latter with material from his other sources by placing additional 
lexical items within the semantic groups to which they belong. Accordingly, 
he avoids the type of disorganization characteristic of both al-Tahḏīb and 
al-Ṣaḥāḥ. For example, in the former, the meaning of “barrenness” occurs in 
the beginning of lemma, is interrupted by other meanings (e.g. abode, hinder 
part of trough, place name, wine, saddle that galls the back, etc.), and then 
resumed, as in “barren woman” and “drink that causes barrenness”. Other 
related meanings also occur in disparate parts of the lemma.616 Similarly, in 
al-Ṣaḥāḥ, muʿāqara (addiction) is not quoted next to ʿaqār (wine), and several 
place names and proper nouns occur sporadically within the lemma. 
Obviously, Ibn Manẓūr’s arrangement of lexical items based on mean-
ing within each lemma is helpful to the reader in finding the word he seeks, 
although he may have to search through much of the lemma to find it due to 
the lack of a fixed scheme of arranging verbs, nouns, derivatives, etc. As far as 
place names and proper nouns are concerned, they normally appear towards 
the end of the lemma, as do reduplicated roots that have corresponding gemi-
nate roots (hence RBRB and ṢBṢB are listed towards the end of RBB and ṢBB; 
but in some other cases as in ḌʾḌʾ and LʾLʾ, such roots are listed separately). Yet 
in spite of Ibn Manẓūr’s effort in grouping together semantically related words, 
they often are listed with those belonging to a different semantic field. This phe-
nomenon takes place in certain lemmata whose structure follows al-Muḥkam 
and are supplemented by lexical items from other sources, but are not as sys-
tematically organized as the lemma ʿQR. In the lemma LBS, for example, Ibn 
Manẓūr follows Ibn Sīda’s617 separation between words related to clothing – 
e.g. labūs (clothes, coat of mail) and albasat al-arḍ (The land became covered 
with plants) – and confusion – e.g. labs/labas (confusion, dubiousness) and 
iltabasa (to become confused) – but confusingly places milbas (what covers 
one or wraps him), which he derives from al-Tahḏīb,618 with words that are 
related to the second meaning. Consequently, there is no guarantee that the 
user will find the word he seeks if he only reads the section of the lemma in 
which words that are semantically related to it are listed.
4.5	
al-Qāmūs al-muḥīṭ by Fīrūzābādī (d. 817/1415)
A famous lexicographer of Persian origin, Fīrūzābādī authored several mubaw-
wab lexica, some of which were discussed in the previous chapter (e.g. Taḥbīr 
al-muwaššīn fī l-taʿbīr bi-l-sīn wa-l-šīn and al-Durar al-mubaṯṯaṯa fī l-ġurar 
616	
Cf. Azharī, Tahḏīb ʿQR (I, 215–221).
617	
Ibn Sīda, Muḥkam LBS (VIII, 510).
618	
Azharī, Tahḏīb LBS (XII, 444).

Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM

---

<!-- page 402 -->

392
chapter 3
al-muṯallaṯa).619 He is best known, however, for his muǧannas lexicon, 
al-Qāmūs, whose name is taken from the Arabized form of the Greek word 
Ωκɛανός, used by the Arabs as a synonym for sea, or, more specifically, its deep-
est part or its main body of water.620 So popular was this lexicon that the word 
qāmūs came to denote “lexicon” in general.
Fīrūzābādī, as he says in his introduction, sought a lexicon that was both 
comprehensive and simple (altamis kitāban ǧāmiʿan basīṭan), but could not 
find one.621 By comprehensiveness, he further explains, is meant that it should 
include common words as well as less common ones (al-fuṣuḥ wa-l-šawārid). 
Simplicity, on the other hand, is probably a reference to brevity of expression 
and explanation, as well as to arrangement. To take al-Ṣaḥāḥ as an example, 
it would satisfy the condition of simplicity of arrangement (and to a lesser 
extent brevity of expression and explanation), but certainly not the condition 
of comprehensiveness. To achieve the latter aim, Fīrūzābādī started author-
ing a lexicon that combines Ibn Sīda’s (d. 458/1066) al-Muḥkam and Ṣaġānī’s 
(d. 650/1252) al-ʿUbāb, and appropriately gave it the title of al-Lāmiʿ al-muʿlam 
al-ʿuǧāb al-ǧāmiʿ bayna l-Muḥkam wa-l-ʿUbāb. It is not clear how much he 
accomplished of that lexicon, but he seems to have soon realized that it 
would run into sixty volumes and thus be inaccessible to users. Accordingly, 
he decided to condense the material which would have occupied each thirty 
volumes into no more than one, hence al-Qāmūs.
Although Fīrūzābādī praises al-Muḥkam and al-ʿUbāb only in very general 
terms and describes them as the best of their kind ( fa-humā ġurratā l-kutub 
al-muṣannafa fī hāḏā l-bāb), his choice is obviously based on two factors: 
comprehensiveness and organization. The two lexica, as we have noted ear-
lier, are among the most well-organized in the tradition. They are also among 
the most voluminous and contain material taken from a large number of 
earlier works: Ibn Sīda incorporated into his lexicon most of the material of 
several muǧannas lexica, and in particular al-ʿAyn, al­-Ǧamhara and al-Bāriʿ;622 
al-ʿUbāb, on the other hand, expanded the material of Ṣaġānī’s own al-Takmila 
relying on a host of sources, including al-Ṣaḥāḥ (which was at the core of three 
other lexica by Ṣaġānī), al-Maqāyīs (from which he derived the bulk of his com-
ments on uṣūl), in addition to al-ʿAyn, al-Ǧīm, al-Ǧamhara, al-Tahḏīb, al-Muḥīṭ 
and al-Muǧmal.623 According to Saḫāwī (d. 902/1497), Fīrūzābādī was a great 
619	
Cf. above, 225, 250.
620	
Cf. above, 50.
621	
Fīrūzābādī, Qāmūs, Introduction I, 3.
622	
Cf. above, 324.
623	
Cf. above, 379, 382.

via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 403 -->

393
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
admirer of Ṣaġānī and imitated him as an author; he even took up his habit of 
residing in Mecca for long periods.624 Based on Fīrūzābādī’s own description 
of the plan he envisaged for al-Lāmiʿ, it was destined to incorporate the mate-
rial of both al-Muḥkam and al-ʿUbāb with supplements from other sources. In 
fact, this is also the essence of his plan in al-Qāmūs. A comparison between 
the material of the latter and earlier lexica shows that al-Muḥkam and al-ʿUbāb 
are its two main sources, but that Fīrūzābādī sporadically adds lexical items 
or meanings derived from other muǧannas lexica which he consulted. Yet the 
additions (ziyādāt) to al-Muḥkam and al-ʿUbāb form only a modest part of 
the material of al-Qāmūs and hardly substantiate its author’s claim that it 
contains the digest of two thousand works (ṣarīḥ alfay muṣannaf).625 Most of 
these additions are names of Companions of the Prophet, scholars of Ḥadīṯ, 
poets, place names and plants, in addition to medical terms which he seems 
to have recorded in Yemen, where he lived the last twenty years or so of his 
life.626 In particular, proper nouns of all types occur much more frequently in 
al-Qāmūs than in any other lexicon and are estimated to be three times more 
than in al-Lisān and twenty times more than in al-Ṣaḥāḥ.627
It is remarkable that in his attempt at comprehensiveness, Fīrūzābādī nei-
ther mentions al-Lisān nor seems to have included it in his sources. Zabīdī 
(d. 1205/1790) argues that Fīrūzābādī had no access to al-Lisān (lam yaṭṭaliʿ 
ʿalayhi) or else he would have used some of its material.628 He estimates 
that al-Lisān comprises 80,000 mādda (here, lexical item),629 compared with 
60,000 in al-Qāmūs. But it is unlikely that Fīrūzābādī could not have known of 
a famous lexicon such as al-Lisān, which was authored about a century earlier. 
It is more conceivable that he overlooked al-Lisān because it was not an origi-
nal work, rather the result of the merger of five earlier lexica, one of the most 
important of which was al-Muḥkam, from which Fīrūzābādī derived much of 
his own material. But just as Ibn Manẓūr could have added many lexical items 
had he consulted Ṣaġānī’s al-ʿUbāb and al-Takmila, Fīrūzābādī could have ben-
efited, had he referred to al-Lisān, from the material which Ibn Manẓūr had 
derived from some of his sources, particularly Ibn al-Aṯīr’s al-Nihāya. As for 
the comparison between al-Qāmūs and al-Ṣaḥāḥ, Zabīdī notes that the former 
includes 20,000 mādda that do not feature in the latter. Moreover, Fīrūzābādī

<!-- page 404 -->

394
chapter 3
himself compares his lexicon with al-Ṣaḥāḥ – which he says has rightfully 
become so popular – by noting that Ǧawharī excluded half the language or 
more, both in terms of roots and ġarīb meanings. To highlight the fact that his 
lexicon makes up for this deficiency in al-Ṣaḥāḥ, he marked, in red, material 
which Ǧawharī had ignored. However, he arranged his sections (abwāb) and 
chapters (fuṣūl), as well as the roots, on the model that had been perfected 
by Ǧawharī and had long since largely become the standard in muǧannas lex-
ica. Contrary to common practice however Fīrūzābādī, as he explains in the 
introduction, observed the separation between wāw and yāʾ (taḫlīṣ al-wāw min 
al-yāʾ) – an issue which, he says, has confused earlier authors.630 Although he 
gives no further clarification, this can only be a reference to final weak verbs, 
which he divided, wherever applicable, into those ending with either wāw or 
yāʾ. FLW and QLW, for example, are listed separately from FLY and QLY, in con-
trast to earlier works, such as al-Ṣaḥāḥ and al-Lisān, which list them in one 
lemma, hence FLW/Y and QLW/Y.631
Fīrūzābādī had to face the challenge of achieving comprehensiveness with-
out producing a lexicon that was too lengthy to be easily accessible to users 
as al-Lāmiʿ would have been. To achieve his goal, he adopted two strategies: 
summarizing the material derived from the sources and presenting it in a 
highly condensed fashion. To begin with, it should be realized that compre-
hensiveness is a relative notion, and thus one finds – in spite of Fīrūzābādī’s 
claim to recording the full range of meanings of lexical items (iltizām itmām 
al-maʿānī) – that he often ignores meanings listed in his two main sources, 
al-Muḥkam and al-ʿUbāb. Yet he was able to include in his lemmata the vast 
majority of lexical items and meanings contained in earlier lexica. By com-
paring al-Qāmūs with both al-Muḥkam and al-ʿUbāb, it becomes clear that 
Fīrūzābādī has summarized their content, not only by retaining the most 
essential parts of the explanation of meanings, but also by avoiding repetition 
and digression, and by omitting the šawāhid, the names of philologists and, in 
the case of al-ʿUbāb, the uṣūl quoted by Ṣaġānī from al-Maqāyīs. In this respect, 
Fīrūzābādī differs from Ibn Manẓūr, who quotes his sources at more length, 
with little consideration for the size of the lexicon. As far as the manner of 
presentation of the summarized material is concerned, Fīrūzābādī adopts a 
template according to which he exhausts the various meanings of the verb or 
630	
Fīrūzābādī, Qāmūs, Introduction I, 4.
631	
See Ḫalīl’s (1997: 286) erroneous explanation of Fīrūzābādī’s statement concerning taḫlīṣ 
al-wāw min al-yāʾ. He claims that this applies to hollow verbs such as ǦWR, failing to real-
ize that earlier lexica did separate between hollow verbs with wāw or yāʾ (i.e. ǦWR and 
ǦYR are two different lemmata in al-Ṣaḥāḥ and al-Lisān).

<!-- page 405 -->

395
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
noun at hand before proceeding to another. This normally ensures that each 
lexical item is mentioned only once and is not repeated within the lemma, as 
is often the case in earlier lexica. For the sake of economy, Fīrūzābādī consis-
tently applies a number of rules which he briefly mentions in the introduc-
tion. He thus ignores the plural of the pattern fāʿil in hollow verbs if it is of the 
regular pattern faʿala(tun) – as in (our examples) bāʿa(tun) and sāda(tun) – 
except in the likes of ǧawala(tun) and ḫawala(tun) (but note that neither is 
mentioned under ǦWL and ḪWL respectively); indicates the feminine by the 
expression wa-hiya bi-hāʾ, which he places after the masculine form without 
citing the feminine form itself; mentions only the verbal noun or the perfect 
verb, but not the imperfect, in verbs of the type kataba (i.e. faʿala/yafʿulu); and 
mentions the imperfect without specifying its pattern if it is of the type ḍaraba 
(i.e. faʿala/yafʿilu).632 Although similar economies were used by some earlier 
authors (e.g. Ibn Sīda in al-Muḥkam),633 Fīrūzābādī applies his more consis-
tently. Moreover, he introduces the following abbreviations for further brevity: 
ʿ for mawḍiʿ (place name), d for balad (town), t for qarya (village), ǧ for ǧamʿ 
(plural), ǧǧ for ǧamʿ al-ǧamʿ (plural of the plural, e.g. azāhīr, pl. of zahr and 
azhār “flowers”), and m for maʿrūf (well known). Through concise definitions 
and multifaceted economies, Fīrūzābādī is thus able to produce a lexicon that 
is extremely rich in vocabulary, but considerably shorter in size and easier to 
consult than long-winded works, in particular al-Lisān.
Other than listing all the meanings of any lexical item, be it a verb or a noun, 
before proceeding to another, the lemmata are normally well organized in that 
the ground form of the verb precedes the derived patterns; the pattern of the 
verb in the perfect and imperfect, as well as its verbal noun, are specified by cit-
ing a common verb that represents the general type (i.e. ḫaba⁠ʾahu ka-manaʿahu 
means the verb is of the type faʿala/yafʿalu/faʿlan, hence ḫaba⁠ʾa yaḫba⁠ʾu ḫabʾan); 
the feminine and plural(s) are cited systematically based on the template 
applied in all lemmata; and proper nouns are cited at the end of the lemma 
or with the lexical item to which they are identical in pattern (hence the poet 
ʿAǧǧāǧ is mentioned with ʿaǧǧāǧ “clamorous”). But Fīrūzābādī’s great concern 
for brevity prompted him to mention, with the ground form, the augmented 
forms which are identical to it in meaning, and hence these do not occur with 
other augmented forms in the same lemma. In the lemma ḪBʾ, for example, 
ḫabba⁠ʾahu and ihṯaba⁠ʾahu are mentioned with ḫaba⁠ʾahu because they share its 
meaning, whereas ḫāba⁠ʾtuhu occurs towards the end of the lemma because it 
differs in meaning from the ground form. Obviously, Fīrūzābādī owes much in 
632	
Fīrūzābādī, Qāmūs, Introduction I, 4.
633	
Cf. above, 328.

<!-- page 406 -->

396
chapter 3
the internal organization of his lemmata to al-Muḥkam and al-ʿUbāb, the latter 
in particular. As for ensuring the correct reading of words, he is much more 
methodical than earlier authors, including Ibn Sīda and Ṣaġānī. In almost 
every lemma, words are either followed by mention of the necessary vowels 
or by a word of the same pattern. For example, in ʿLǦ, a lemma of no more 
than ten lines, the following aids are mentioned: al-ʿilǧ bi-l-kasr . . . ʿaliǧ ka-katif 
wa-ṣurad wa-ḫullar (i.e. ʿulaǧ and ʿullaǧ) . . . wa-bi-l-taḥrīk (i.e. ʿalaǧ) . . . wa-l-
ʿulǧān bi-l-ḍamm . . . wa-bi-l-taḥrīk (i.e. ʿalaǧān) . . . wa-banū l-ʿUlayǧ ka-Zubayr 
wa-banū l-ʿIlāǧ bi-l-kasr . . . wa-l-ʿalaǧāna muḥarraka. It is also noteworthy that 
Fīrūzābādī adopts the same method and almost the same illustrative words in 
his book on muṯallaṯāt, i.e. al-Durar al-mubaṯṯaṯa,634 as in arāb wa-urāb wa-irāb 
ka-saḥāb wa-ġurāb wa-kitāb; azafa l-ǧurḥu wa-azufa wa-azifa ka-naṣara wa-
karuma wa-fariḥa; al-aqiṭ ka-katif wa-l-aquṭ ka-ʿaǧuz wa-l-aqaṭ ka-ǧamal wa-l-
iqiṭ ka-ibil.635 Fīrūzābādī is also keen on avoiding taṣḥīf by sporadically spelling 
out the dotting of the relevant letter(s) (e.g. tāʾuhu l-muṯallaṯa; cf. ʿṮR) – a prac-
tice that is well known in earlier works.
As in the case of al-Ṣaḥāḥ, the Qāmūs gave rise to a large number of com-
mentaries, criticisms, abridgements and supplements,636 and was similarly 
translated into Persian and Turkish. One of the most detailed commentar-
ies is Iḍāʾat al-rāmūs wa-iḍāfat637 al-nāmūs ʿalā iḍāʾat al-Qāmūs, in which 
Muḥammad b. al-Ṭayyib al-Šarqī al-Fāsī (d. 1170/1756) raises several issues 
related to Fīrūzābādī’s methods. Among these is criticism of the latter for cit-
ing Aʿǧamī words, i.e. words that are neither Arabic nor Arabized (muʿarrab), 
and for including technical terms and proper nouns whereas these, according 
to Ibn al-Ṭayyib, are not part of the language proper.638 Many of Ibn al-Ṭayyib’s 
comments on al-Qāmūs were incorporated into Tāǧ al-ʿarūs by his student, 
Zabīdī (d. 1205/1790), who properly acknowledged in his introduction the work 
of his teacher.639 The Tāǧ itself is also based on the Qāmūs, as is Zabīdī’s other 
lexicon al-Takmila wa-l-ḏayl wa-l-ṣila li-mā fāta ṣāḥib al-Qāmūs min al-luġa 
(for both lexica, see next section). More recently, Aḥmad Fāris al-Šidyāq

<!-- page 407 -->

397
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
(d. 1304/1887) authored his famous al-Ǧāsūs ʿalā l-Qāmūs, a remarkably eru-
dite treatise written on the eve of the modern period. In addition to an intro-
duction and an epilogue, the book is divided into twenty-four chapters, each 
of which is devoted to one type of criticism (naqd) of al-Qāmūs. Criticisms 
include the unintelligibility of some of Fīrūzābādī’s expressions mostly due 
to their brevity – a view shared by Lane in the introduction to his lexicon640 – 
disorganization in citing some augmented verbs, inaccurate definitions, rep-
etitions, taṣḥīf, excessive citing of technical terms and place names, and a 
number of morphological issues.
4.6	
Tāǧ al-ʿarūs min ǧawāhir al-Qāmūs by Zabīdī (d. 1205/1790)
At the end of al-Tāǧ,641 Zabīdī tells us that he had spent more than fourteen 
years in authoring it by the time he finished it in 1188/1774. In terms of chro-
nology, al-Tāǧ is not strictly medieval, but it is certainly so in its methods, and 
crowns the “classical” period of Arabic lexicographical writing as the most 
comprehensive lexicon in the tradition – a lexicon that combines, more than 
any other, the contents of earlier muǧannas lexica and other works, and whose 
material is so vast that even the fourteen years spent by Zabīdī in its compila-
tion may seem too short in comparison to the effort required for its completion.
The similarity between al-Lisān and al-Tāǧ is unmistakable as both aim 
at completeness by merging the material of a number of earlier works. But 
whereas Ibn Manẓūr relies on five lexica for this purpose, Zabīdī supplements 
Fīrūzābādī’s al-Qāmūs – which he describes as the best and most popular in 
the genre, but whose concise expressions need to be expanded642 – by material 
derived from 116 sources which he lists in the introduction.643 Like Ibn Manẓūr, 
Zabīdī does not claim any originality, and even borrows verbatim Ibn Manẓūr’s 
assertion that any sound or defective material in his lexicon is strictly the 
responsibility of the original author ( fa-ʿuhdatuhu ʿalā l-muṣannif al-awwal).644 
Yet unlike Ibn Manẓūr, who omits some material from the sources he quotes,645 
Zabīdī painstakingly preserves Fīrūzābādī’s text (even with its abbreviations), 
but interrupts it whenever it needs to be explained, expanded or amended. For 
the reader’s convenience, Fīrūzābādī’s words are put between brackets, and if 
read uninterruptedly stand for the full text of al-Qāmūs. In the ziyādāt, i.e. the 
640

645

<!-- page 408 -->

398
chapter 3
expansion of the original text, however, Zabīdī mostly intervenes in the origi-
nal texts by bringing them in line with Fīrūzābādī’s method of writing. al-Tāǧ 
also includes an extensive introduction, which is made up, in addition to a 
list of sources, of material related to a number of general philological issues 
quoted verbatim from Suyūṭī’s (d. 911/1505) al-Muzhir.
In his ziyādāt, Zabīdī primarily restores the material neglected by Fīrūzābādī 
for the sake of conciseness. This material includes the names of authorities 
to whom data is ascribed, the šawāhid and their renderers, and the missing 
roots, lexical items and meanings.646 There are also frequent aids to the user 
to ensure the correct reading of certain words for which Fīrūzābādī did not 
include such aids. Occasionally, Zabīdī provides information derived from his 
own experience in Egypt and Yemen and even records some Egyptian vernacu-
lar usage (cf., for example, RWʾ, ṢNṬ, LṬʿ). By expanding the text of al-Qāmūs in 
this manner, Zabīdī compiled a lexicon that was probably equivalent to what 
would have been al-Lāmiʿ al-muʿlam al-ʿuǧāb had Fīrūzābādī actually authored 
it instead of the much shorter al-Qāmūs.647 Following the text of al-Qāmūs 
and the supplements that Zabīdī inserted within it, most lemmata end with 
a section on mustadrak (supplemented material), containing not only the 
roots that do not occur in al-Qāmūs, but also the lexical items and meanings 
which Fīrūzābādī had failed to mention and which Zabīdī did not include in 
the expansion of the text of al-Qāmūs. The ziyādāt also include Zabīdī’s cor-
rection to alleged inconsistencies and errors of explanation, vocalization and 
taṣḥīf made by Fīrūzābādī, and often refutation of the latter in ascribing error 
to the author of al-Ṣaḥāḥ. There are also two other types of ziyādāt, each of 
which is derived from a specific source. The first is ultimately from al-Maqāyīs 
and pertains to the uṣūl or basic meaning(s) of the root in accordance with Ibn 
Fāris’s method in that lexicon. Zabīdī, however, seems to derive this material 
from Ṣaġānī’s (d. 650/1252) al-ʿUbāb al-zāḫir and not directly from al-Maqāyīs 
since his wording is closer to the former than to the original text.648 The other 
type is taken from Zamaḫšarī’s (d. 538/1144) Asās al-balāġa and is normally 
introduced by the phrase wa-min saǧaʿāt al-Asās, followed by rhymed con-
structions cited by Zamaḫšarī. Zabīdī may have in this respect followed his 
teacher, Muḥammad b. al-Ṭayyib al-Šarqī (d. 1170/1756), who frequently quotes 
646

šawāhid, grammatical content and Arabized words; cf. ʿAlam (2002).
647	
Fīrūzābādī, Qāmūs, Introduction I, 3; cf. above, 392.
648

(ḌĠḌĠ) and Zabīdī, Tāǧ (ḌĠḌĠ); see also above, 356.

<!-- page 409 -->

399
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
in his Iḍāʾat al-rāmūs (for which see the previous section) Zamaḫšarī’s rhymed 
quotations.649
The large number of sources used by Zabīdī demonstrates his keenness on 
authoring a lexicon that is more comprehensive then earlier ones. Although 
the diversity of these sources is apparent in the introduction of al-Tāǧ, a simi-
lar, though shorter, list cited by Zabīdī at the end of his al-Takmila wa-l-ḏayl 
wa-l-ṣila (cf. below) is more indicative of this diversity, given that it is divided 
according to genre.650 The list includes works on (1) language (luġa), includ-
ing what he describes as the six main lexica (uṣūl): al-Ǧamhara, al-Ṣaḥāḥ, 
al-Tahḏīb, al-Muḥkam, al-ʿUbāb and al-Takmila (by Ṣaġānī), in addition to 
al-Asās, al-Lisān and a number of mubawwab sources, e.g. Laysa by Ibn 
Ḫālawayhi; (2) strange or unfamiliar usage (ġarīb), e.g. Ibn al-Aṯīr’s al-Nihāya 
and Zamaḫšarī’s al-Fāʾiq; (3) genealogies (ansāb), e.g., Ibn al-Kalbī’s Ǧamharat 
al-nasab; (4) names that are dubious due to resemblance (muštabih), e.g. Ibn 
Mākūlā’s al-Ikmāl and Ibn Ḥaǧar’s Tabṣīr al-muntabih; (5) biographies, e.g. Ibn 
Ḥibbān’s al-Ṯiqāt; (6) Companions of the Prophet, e.g. Ibn Ḥaǧar’s al-Iṣāba; 
(7) words with two contradictory meanings (aḍdād), e.g. Abū l-Ṭayyib’s 
al-Aḍdād; (8) place names, e.g. Yāqūt’s Muʿǧam al-Buldān; (9) Egyptian place 
names, e.g. Ibn Mammātī’s Qawānīn al-dawāwīn; (10) animals, e.g. Damīrī’s 
Ḥayāt al-ḥayawān al-kubrā; (11) miscellaneous topics, e.g. Ibn Durayd’s [Ṣifat] 
al-sarǧ wa-l-liǧām, Rāzī’s al-Zīna, and Ibn ʿAsākir’s Tārīḫ Dimašq; (12) proverbs, 
e.g. the books of ʿAskarī, Maydānī and Zamaḫšarī; (13) medicinal use of plants, 
e.g. works of Ibn al-Bayṭār; (14) stones and metals, e.g. Tīfāšī’s Azhār al-afkār; 
(15) poets, e.g. Iṣfahānī’s al-Aġānī and Ḍabbī’s al-Mufaḍḍaliyyāt; (16) abbrevi-
ated and prolonged patterns (al-maqṣūr wa-l-mamdūd), e.g. Qālī’s book; and 
(17) grammar books and poetry collections (dawāwīn).
Zabīdī asserts in al-Tāǧ’s introduction that he derived his material directly 
from his sources and not from works in which they were quoted (wa-naqaltu 
bi-l-mubāšara lā bi-l-wasāʾiṭ ʿanhā).651 Indeed, this is apparent in material not 
found in earlier muǧannas lexica, but directly taken from specialized works, 
as in the case of technical terms, especially in medicine, and the large number 
of names of individuals – e.g. Companions of the Prophet, scholars, eminent 
figures, etc. – and place names, particularly in Egypt (note that Zabīdī himself 
refers to the high frequency of proper nouns and place names in his other lexi-
con, al-Takmila, but justifies it by its usefulness to the reader).652 Furthermore, 
649

Zabīdī, Tāǧ, Introduction I, 3.
652

<!-- page 410 -->

400
chapter 3
Zabīdī incorporated into al-Tāǧ the bulk of the material in Ibn Manẓūr’s 
al-Lisān, obviously by direct copying from it since there was no lexicon that 
included all the material of al-Lisān and hence could have served as a second-
ary source in this respect. The inclusion of the phonetic and morphological 
characteristics of letters at the beginning of each section is probably inspired 
by al-Lisān as well. Yet the more important question is whether Zabīdī indeed 
consulted all his other sources directly, including those used by Ibn Manẓūr. 
In one case, he admits that his quotations from Ibn Barrī’s al-Ḥawāšī, one of 
Ibn Manẓūr’s five sources, are not taken directly from it.653 We have also noted 
previously that Zābīdī’s references to the uṣūl (i.e. basic meanings) are derived 
from Ṣaġānī’s al-ʿUbāb and not directly from al-Maqāyīs. In fact, Zabīdī does 
not mention al-Maqāyīs in his list of sources either in al-Tāǧ or al-Takmila. Also 
absent from these sources are three major muǧannas lexica, namely, al-ʿAyn, 
al-Bāriʿ and al-Muḥīṭ. Given Zabīdī’s eagerness to mention among his sources 
even relatively minor philological works (such as Lablī’s Buġyat al-Āmāl fī 
maʿrifat mustaqbalāt al-afʿāl)654 as well as general literary and historical works, 
the absence of these three lexica most probably means that they were not 
among his sources and could have been quoted only indirectly.
The internal arrangement of the lemmata is of course determined by the 
text of al-Qāmūs, which Zabīdī quotes in full and continuously interrupts to 
explain or expand. In the sections of mustadrak, i.e. the lemma’s latter part 
that is not based on al-Qāmūs, lexical items are not arranged in any specific 
order. The ziyādāt which occur both in the expansion of Fīrūzābādī’s text and 
under the mustadrak form the basis of another lexicon by Zabīdī, al-Takmila 
wa-l-ḏayl wa-l-ṣila li-mā fāta ṣāḥib al-Qāmūs min al-luġa – a title obviously 
borrowed from Ṣaġānī’s al-Takmila in which the text of al-Ṣaḥāḥ is expanded 
and corrected.655 Zabīdī explains that just as Fīrūzābādī in his al-Qāmūs had 
written in red ink the material left out by Ǧawharī he did the same with what 
Fīrūzābādī himself had left out, with the intention of disproving the wide-
spread belief that the latter covered the whole language in his lexicon.656 al-
Takmila also includes additions of its own, mainly in the names of individuals 
653	
The expression wa-mā ʿazawtuhu ilā Ibn Barrī fa-bi-wāsiṭat hāḏā l-kitāb is linked in Zabīdī’s 
text (Takmila VIII, 454) to Urmawī’s (d. 723/1323) Tahḏīb al-Tahḏīb (cf. above, 318), but 
appears in a different location of the text as copied by the editor of the first volume in his 
introduction (I, 15). Accordingly, that editor suggests that hāḏā l-kitāb refers to al-Lisān, 
although the latter is not mentioned by Zabīdī.
654	
Cf. above, 260–261.
655	
Cf. above, 378.
656	
Zabīdī, Takmila I, 71.

<!-- page 411 -->

401
Muǧannas (Semasiological) Lexica
and places, and Zabīdī often adds to unvocalized words cited in al-Tāǧ their 
correct vowels or indicates the patterns that determine their correct reading.
By expanding al-Qāmūs from a host of sources and incorporating into his 
text the bulk of al-Lisān, Zabīdī compiled a truly encyclopedic work that can 
be described as the most comprehensive muǧannas lexicon in the whole tra-
dition. Compared with al-Lisān’s 9,273 roots, al-Tāǧ boasts 11,978.657 These 
include more than a thousand triliteral roots not found in al-Lisān (i.e. 7,597 
compared with 6,538 in al-Lisān, an increase of 16.2%). But the highest pro-
portion of additions occurs in quadriliterals and quinqueliterals (respectively 
4,081 and 300, compared with 2,548 and 187 in al-Lisān, an increase of 60% in 
each type). Given its completeness and its author’s scrutiny of earlier works, 
it is not surprising that al-Tāǧ was applauded by orientalists and effectively 
passed into the western tradition with Lane drawing from it most of the con-
tent of his voluminous English-Arabic lexicon.658
657	
Mūsā & Šāhīn (1973: 9); Mūsā (1989–1990: 149); cf. above, 387. See also Mrayati (1987: 
88), where the total number of roots in al-Ǧamhara, al-Tahḏīb, al-Muḥkam, al-Lisān and 
al-Qāmūs is said to be 11, 347.
658	
Cf. Lane (1863–93: I, xix).

Epilogue
For several centuries, the medieval Arabic lexicographical tradition embraced 
a wide variety of topics and models of arrangement. Based on the introduction 
of Ibn Sīda (d. 458/1066) to al-Muḫaṣṣaṣ, the terms mubawwab and muǧannas 
have been adopted to refer to the onomasiological and semasiological types 
respectively. The first type, in which meaning leads to sign, mostly comprises 
a vast number of specialized dictionaries that deal with a specific theme, in 
addition to a few multithematic works or thesauri. On the other hand, gen-
eral, unspecialized lexica in which sign leads to meaning and which repre-
sent the muǧannas type are considerably fewer in number but posed for their 
authors more serious challenges, not only in the arrangement of lexical items, 
but also in the internal arrangement of the lemmata and in the extent of the 
corpus to be included. This epilogue provides a synthesis of some of the basic 
issues related to the lexicographical tradition as a whole and to the various 
approaches and strategies adopted by lexicographers to address these issues.
Occasionally, one finds reference by lexicographers to the merits or demerits 
of the formal and thematic arrangements. A particularly illuminating example 
as early as the third/ninth century is found in Dīnawarī’s (d. 282/895) al-Nabāt. 
Adopting an alphabetical arrangement in its second part, Dīnawarī notes that 
this formal criterion means that no distinction can be made among the various 
plants cited, such as their division into large and small trees, or into trees, herbs 
and shrubs. Yet for Dīnawarī the choice of an alphabetical arrangement was 
worthwhile because it would facilitate the use of the book, particularly since in 
its first part distinction had already been made between the different types of 
plants under discussion. In contrast, the merits of the thematic arrangement 
is evident in a number of genres. For instance, most works on ḫalq al-insān are 
thematically arranged so that lexical items that belong to one part of the body 
are listed together, and there can be a logical ordering of these parts (e.g. head, 
scalp, ears, beard, face, etc.) and of the different stages of human life from birth 
to old age. This logical aspect is of course shattered in alphabetically arranged 
works of the genre, such as Ibn Ḥabīb’s (d. 245/860) Ḫalq al-insān fī l-luġa, 
although one can more easily locate in it a specific word than in thematically 
arranged works of the genre. Another genre in which thematic arrangement 
is dominant is laḥn al-ʿāmma, obviously because authors were interested in 
classifying linguistic change into specific types (mainly vocalic, consonantal 
and semantic) – a feature that is lacking in works of the genre that are alpha-
betically arranged, such as Taqwīm al-lisān by Ibn al-Ǧawzī (d. 597/1201), who 
explains that he opted for an alphabetical arrangement in order to facilitate 
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 413 -->

403
epilogue
the use of his book although he had intended to arrange the material based on 
types of error. The same argument about the ease of alphabetical arrangement 
is frequently encountered in other lexica and is interestingly linked by Abū 
l-Ṭayyib al-Luġawī (d. 351/962) in al-Aḍdād to the fact that people of his own 
time preferred it and that their ardor was confined to it (iḏ kānat himam ahl 
zamāninā maqṣūra ʿalayhi wa-qulūbuhum māʾila ilayhi; cf. above, 197).
In spite of the usefulness of the distinction between the mubawwab and 
muǧannas types for classification purposes, the boundaries between the two 
types are far from steadfast. Other than the issue of the contemporaneity of 
the two types, many specialized works of the former are arranged according to 
form (lafẓ) rather than subject, as one finds in genres such as Qurʾānic ġarīb, 
proverbs and Arabized words, among others. Yet for the sake of studying each 
genre as an indivisible unit, such works have to be discussed under the mubaw-
wab type although their material is formally arranged. Hence the distinction 
between mubawwab and muǧannas works rests primarily in that the former is 
specialized in one or more topics, whereas the latter normally aims at exhaust-
ing the roots of the language and, in the case of the more comprehensive lexica, 
the bulk of the derivatives of these roots. In this respect, it is of essence to note 
that one of the earliest muǧannas lexica, Ibn Durayd’s (d. 321/933) Ǧamharat 
al-luġa, includes in its final parts several miscellaneous topics reminiscent of 
short single-topic monographs – a feature which reflects the conviction that 
the muǧannas type does not abolish the need for a mubawwab type of arrange-
ment, given that each of the two types serves a specific purpose. Similarly, 
Qālī’s (d. 356/967) al-Bāriʿ is reported to have contained at its end a chapter 
on Arabized words, and appended to Azharī’s (d. 370/981) al-Tahḏīb are several 
chapters dealing with phonological, morphological and syntactic issues.
The interrelatedness between the mubawwab and muǧannas types is further 
demonstrated by the fact that, in certain works, an argument can be made for 
their classification under either type. Perhaps the most obvious example is Abū 
ʿAmr al-Šaybānī’s (d. 206/821) K. al-Ǧīm, which, because it is essentially a book 
of ġarīb, resembles other works in that genre. Yet its alphabetical arrangement 
beginning with the first letter at such an early stage (note that it was authored 
contemporaneously with K. al-ʿAyn, or even earlier if riwāyas of an earlier date 
of death and a longer life span for Abū ʿAmr are correct) argues for its inclu-
sion with muǧannas lexica as this would be more meaningful for the study 
of the beginning of this type of arrangement and for its comparison with the 
phonetic-permutative system. A similar issue of classification is encountered 
in Bandanīǧī’s (d. 284/897) al-Taqfiya and Fārābī’s (d. 350/961) Dīwān al-adab. 
From another perspective, the mubawwab genre of mutarādif, which goes back 
to the earliest period of lexicography, greatly resembles muǧannas works in 
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 414 -->

404
epilogue
that it chiefly aims at providing synonyms for the cited lexical items, albeit 
within a thematic arrangement. But whereas books on mutarādif typically give 
several synonyms for each word, muǧannas lexica normally give only one, and 
at times words are explained even without mention of any synonym.
Perhaps the most important aspect of the interrelatedness between the 
mubawwab and muǧannas types is that authors of each incorporated into their 
lexica material from the other. Understandably, specialized works, particularly 
from the early period, formed a major source of data for authors of muǧannas 
lexica. Much of the material of the genre ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ, for example, was 
incorporated into muǧannas works: Azharī mentions in the introduction of 
al-Tahḏīb several works of the genre which he used; Ibn Fāris (d. 395/1004) 
adopts Abū ʿUbayd’s (d. 224/838) Ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ as one of his five sources; 
Ibn Sīda generally refers in the introduction of al-Muḥkam to šurūḥ al-Ḥadīṯ 
(commentaries on Ḥadīṯ); and Ibn Manẓūr (d. 711/1311) fully incorporates Ibn 
al-Aṯīr’s (d. 606/1210) al-Nihāya fī ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ into al-Lisān. The same is true 
of general ġarīb works. For instance, Abū Misḥal’s (d. 231/845) al-Nawādir and 
Kurāʿ al-Naml’s (d. 310/922) books, in particular al Muǧarrad, are frequently 
quoted in the muǧannas lexica of Azharī, Ibn Sīda, Ibn Manẓūr and Zabīdī 
(d. 379/989). Similarly, books on proverbs were extensively used by most 
authors of muǧannas lexica. On the other hand, some authors of mubawwab 
works availed themselves of material available in muǧannas lexica. This is most 
clearly demonstrated by Ibn Sīda’s thesaurus al-Muḫaṣṣaṣ, which abounds 
with material derived from K. al-ʿAyn and al-Ǧamhara, and in Zamaḫšarī’s 
(d. 538/1144) al-Fāʾiq fī ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ and Ibn al-Aṯīr’s al-Nihāya fī ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ, 
both of which derive much of their material from Azharī’s al-Tahḏīb. Among 
other genres, ibdāl (assimilation) visibly illustrates the dynamic relationship 
between mubawwab and muǧannas lexica, for not only the material of mono-
graphs on ibdāl is frequently found cited in muǧannas lexica, but there are also 
numerous quotations from K. al-Ǧīm in one of the most important works of 
the genre, Abū l-Ṭayyib al-Luġawī’s al-Ibdāl.
Other than the wide variety of topics characteristic of mubawwab lexica, 
the lexicographical tradition as a whole exhibits considerable variety in the 
arrangement of lexical items. Although the rhyme system is alphabetical in 
nature, the term “alphabetical” is reserved for lexica that adopt the ʾ, b, t, ṯ etc. 
order based on the first letters of roots or words. As for the phonetic system, it 
is unique since the basis of its arrangement differs from the other two types. 
In a rare instance of comparison between the phonetic and the alphabetical 
arrangements, Ibn Wallād (d. 332/994) in the introduction of al-Maqṣūr wa-l-
mamdūd, whose arrangement is alphabetical by the first letter only, notes that 
his arrangement begins with alif (i.e. hamza), unlike K. al-ʿAyn, which begins 
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 415 -->

405
epilogue
with ʿayn. But his most telling observation is the admission that some readers 
might blame him for not having avoided to begin with a weak letter due to 
the problems caused by its instability. One is strongly reminded here of Ḫalīl’s 
argument against beginning with alif, and hence Ibn Wallād’s comments may 
well reveal the conviction by the proponents of the phonetic arrangement 
of its superiority, at least in this respect. In fact, the phonetic system is not 
restricted to muǧannas lexica, and among its examples in mubawwab ones 
are Qālī’s al-Maqṣūr wa-l-mamdūd and Ibn al-Qūṭiyya’s (d. 367/977) al-Afʿāl. 
As for the system of permutations or taqālīb, which is firmly linked to pho-
netic arrangement, it also features in some mubawwab lexica, such as Ḥarbī’s 
(d. 285/898) Ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ and Ǧarbāḏaqānī’s (fl. In the second half of the 
fourth/tenth century) al-Rawḥa on the subject of ḍād and ẓāʾ. Permutations 
were also adopted by Ibn Durayd in al-Ǧamhara, making it the only alpha-
betically arranged muǧannas lexicon to follow this system. Furthermore, 
there is variety even within the phonetic system: Saraqusṭī’s (d. after 400/1010) 
arrangement of letters in al-Afʿāl differs from that of Ḫalīl’s, and Ibn al-Qūṭiyya 
uniquely introduces in the arrangement of his al-Afʿāl an additional factor to 
the points of articulation, namely, that several letters are placed consecutively 
due to the similarity of their written forms. In works that adopted the alpha-
betical system, variety is also noted in Ibn Fāris’s peculiar arrangement of radi-
cals in a cyclical manner, i.e. roots begin with the first radical, followed by the 
radicals that are after it in the ʾ, b, t, ṯ order, and then by those that precede it 
in that order. Another form of diversity is the use of the Maġribī arrangement 
by some authors, e.g. al-Qazzāz al-Qayrawānī (d. 421/1021) in al-Muṯallaṯ and 
Mālaqī (d. 702/1302) in Raṣf al-mabānī fī šarḥ ḥurūf al-maʿānī.
As far as the relationship between the arrangement by first or last latter is 
concerned, Dīnawarī, further to his previously quoted observation concern-
ing the thematic versus formal arrangement of lexical items, declares that he 
prefers arrangement according to the first letter to that by the last. The impli-
cation of this remarkable statement is that in Dīnawarī’s time in the second 
half of the third/ninth century, arrangement by the last letter was sufficiently 
common as a model that rivals arrangement by the first. This seriously chal-
lenges our knowledge of the sources since by Dīnawarī’s time we only know 
of one lexicon which is arranged according to final letters, namely, al-Taqfiya 
by Dīnawarī’s contemporary, Bandanīǧī (d. 284/897). Yet the present book has 
drawn attention, perhaps for the first time, to an earlier attempt at arranging 
lexical items according to final letters. This occurs in the lengthy chapter on 
the nominal patterns faʿl and faʿal in Ibn al-Sikkīt’s (d. 244/858) Iṣlāḥ al-manṭiq, 
and it is quite interesting that this chapter is almost totally incorporated into 
al-Taqfiya and in almost the exact order of the appearance of lexical items. 
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3

<!-- page 416 -->

406
epilogue
Bandanīǧī seems to have adopted the arrangement of his lexicon from this 
chapter of al-Iṣlāḥ but expanded the latter’s material to include patterns other 
than faʿl and faʿal. The mention by Ibn al-Nadīm (d. 380/990) of a book from 
the same period as Bandanīǧī’s by Ibn Qutayba (d. 276/889), also entitled 
al-Taqfiya, which did not reach us, is also a stern reminder that our knowledge 
of the early sources that are so essential for our understanding of the rise and 
development of the field is far from complete.
Also on the relationship between the arrangement by first or last letter, one 
frequently finds that a single author might use one system in a certain work 
of his and a second in another. For example, Ibn Fāris, whose two muǧannas 
lexica al-Maqāyīs and al-Muǧmal are arranged according to first letter, chooses 
to arrange lexical items in al-Itbāʿ wa-l-muzāwaǧa by their final letters. In this 
case, there is good reason for the divergence since it is essential in the allitera-
tive phenomenon of itbāʿ that the second word end with the same letter as the 
first. In other cases, however, there is no apparent reason for the diversity of 
systems adopted by the same author. Zamaḫšarī thus arranges Asās al-balāġa 
alphabetically but opts for arrangement by last letter in Muqaddimat al-adab. 
For his part, Ṣaġānī (d. 650/1252) adopts the rhyme system in his two volumi-
nous muǧannas lexica al-ʿUbāb and al-Takmila as well as in several mono-
graphs, such as Naqʿat al-ṣadyān fī mā ǧāʾa ʿalā l-faʿalān, al-Infiʿāl, Fī asāmī 
l-ḏiʾb wa-kunāhu and Mā banathu l-ʿArab ʿalā faʿāli. Yet his Yafʿūl, Aḍdād and 
Asmā l-ġāda fī asmāʾ al-ʿāda are alphabetically arranged. This phenomenon is 
encountered as late as the ninth/fifteenth century in the works of Fīrūzābādī 
(d. 817/1415) whose al-Qāmūs is one of the main representatives of the rhyme 
system, but who arranges his Taḥbīr al-muwaššīn fī l-taʿbīr bi-l-sīn wa-l-šīn and 
al-Durar al-mubaṯṯaṯa fī l-ġurar al-muṯallaṯa based on first letters.
In the muǧannas type, the rhyme system became the dominant form 
of arrangement, particularly in the extensive lexica. This is largely due to 
Ǧawharī’s (d. c. 400/1010) al-Ṣaḥāḥ not only because it was very well received 
for its arrangement of roots solely on the sequence of their radicals (not to 
mention its author’s pursuit of correct usage), but also because the numer-
ous commentaries and supplements based on it naturally followed its system 
of arrangement. Furthermore, Ibn Manẓūr followed the system of al-Ṣaḥāḥ, 
one of his five sources, and Fīrūzābādī authored his authoritative al-Qāmūs 
by closely following al-Ṣaḥāḥ’s text and highlighting in red his own additions. 
In turn, Zabīdī expanded al-Qāmūs but retained its arrangement. Yet the pre-
dominance of the rhyme system did not preclude a certain degree of diversity 
since in one of al-Ṣaḥāḥ’s supplements, al-Muntahā fī l-luġa by Muḥammad b. 
Tamīm al-Barmakī (d. 433/1041), the radicals were arranged from last to mid-
dle to first, and not from last to first to middle. On a matter that pertains to 
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 417 -->

407
epilogue
both the rhyme and the alphabetical systems, Ṣafadī (d. 764/1363) in Ġawāmiḍ 
al-Ṣaḥāḥ rearranged the lexical items which he selected from al-Ṣaḥāḥ accord-
ing to the succession of their letters rather than their supposed roots (as he 
also did in his Taṣḥīḥ al-taṣḥīf ). At the same time, he abandoned arrangement 
by the last letter in favor of the first. Ṣafadī’s motive for arranging these items 
based on all their letters (i.e. including augments) was to make them easier for 
the reader to find, as he himself explained. The same reason may well apply to 
their arrangement, against the general trend, by first rather than last letter. If 
so, this rearrangement would be highly significant since it would indicate that 
some were not convinced of the practicality of the rhyme system in spite of its 
widespread use (cf. Fīrūzābādī’s use of the alphabetical system in mubawwab 
works contrary to al-Qāmūs in which he had to follow al-Ṣaḥāḥ’s order so as to 
facilitate comparing its text with his own). 
Although Ṣafadī’s alphabetical rearrangement of the items he selected from 
al-Ṣaḥāḥ is a very rare occurrence in the tradition, his listing of lexical items 
according to all their letters and not the radicals of their roots represents, in 
mubawwab lexica, a recurring practice that has not yet received the attention 
it merits. The importance of this practice is that it is not in line with one of the 
basic principles which Ḫalīl applied in authoring al-ʿAyn and which formed 
one of the main characteristics of the lexicographical tradition, namely, the 
arrangement of lexical items according to their roots without considering 
the augments. Among the numerous mubawwab works in which augments 
are taken into account in the arrangement of some or all lexical items are: 
Dīnawarī’s (d. 282/895) al-Nabāt, Kurāʿ al-Naml’s (d. 310/922) al-Munaǧǧad and 
al-Muǧarrad, Muḥammad b. ʿUzayr al-Siǧistānī’s (d. 330/941) Ġarīb al-Qurʾān, 
Ibn Wallād’s (d. 332/944) al-Maqṣūr wa-l-mamdūd, Ibn Suhayl al-Naḥwī’s 
(d. fifth/twelfth century) al-Ḍād wa-l-ẓāʾ, Ibn al-Šaǧarī’s (d. 542/1148) Mā 
ttafaqa lafẓuhu wa-ḫtalafa maʿnāhu, Ibn al-Ǧawzī’s (d. 597/1201) Taqwīm 
al-lisān, al-Ḥasan b. Aḥmad b. ʿAbdalraḥmān’s (sixth/twelfth century?) 
Ḫalq al-insān fī l-luġa, Ibn al-Aṯīr’s (d. 606/1210) al-Nihāya fī ġarīb al-Ḥadīṯ, 
Muṭarrazī’s (d. 610/1213) al-Muġrib, Ṣaġānī’s (d. 650/1252) Asmā l-ġāda fī 
asmāʾ al-ʿāda, Ṣafadī’s (d. 764/1363) Taṣḥīḥ al-taṣḥīf, Fīrūzābādī’s (d. 817/1415) 
Taḥbīr al-muwaššīn and al-Durar al-mubaṯṯaṯa, and Ibn Bālī al-Qusṭanṭīnī’s 
(d. 992/1584) Ḫayr al-kalām fī l-taqaṣṣī ʿan ġalaṭ al-ʿawāmm. Many of the above 
authors – such as Ibn al-Aṯīr and Ṣafadī – justify the inclusion of the augments 
in their alphabetical arrangement by referring to the difficulty encountered 
in searching for words by the user who cannot differentiate between origi-
nal radicals and augmented letters. It is remarkable however that Fīrūzābādī, 
whose famous al-Qāmūs is based on roots (obviously because he followed the 
arrangement of al-Ṣaḥāḥ and tried to demonstrate, almost in each lemma, 
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 418 -->

408
epilogue
how much material its author ignored) resorts to this practice in some of his 
mubawwab works and even goes as far as to list imperfect verbs under the let-
ter yāʾ or tāʾ, i.e. the prefixed pronoun, as did Ibn ʿUzayr al-Siǧistānī before 
him. It may be that he authored these mubawwab works before al-Qāmūs, but 
it remains that some authors must have felt that arrangement by root does 
have certain disadvantages in spite of its dominance in the tradition. In mod-
ern times, there have been several dictionaries that completely abandoned the 
notion of root in their arrangement. The first of these was al-Rāʾid by Ǧubrān 
Masʿūd, first published in 1965. It is often argued that the arrangement based 
on pronunciation and not root is the most user-friendly model, particularly for 
beginners, and some even praise it for being the closet to the European model. 
Yet the merits of listing all the derivatives of the root in one lemma in a Semitic 
language such as Arabic by far outweigh its disadvantages, since it is in line 
with the derivational nature of the language and thus makes much more sense 
semantically. Each of the two models, of course, targets a different user and 
thus both continue to be employed.
The varieties and subvarieties of arrangement discussed above do not cor-
respond to successive eras of development. Any periodization attempt in this 
respect is bound to be challenged by the continuity of one model long after 
the establishment of another. This not only applies to the three major models, 
i.e. the phonetic, alphabetical and rhyme systems, but also to more specific 
types, particularly in the alphabetical system. Works which are alphabetically 
arranged, albeit on first letter/radical basis only, go back as far as Abū ʿAmr 
al-Šaybānī’s (d. 206/821) K. al-Ǧīm, which was authored contemporaneously 
with Ḫalīl’s (d. 175/791) al-ʿAyn, or even earlier. Other early works with a simi-
lar arrangement include Ibn Ḥabīb’s (d. 245/860) Ḫalq al-insān fī l-luġa, and 
Dīnawarī’s (d. 282/895) al-Nabāt, and only certain chapters of Abū Zayd’s 
(d. 215/830) al-Hamz. Yet long after full alphabetical order was used in both 
muǧannas and mubawwab lexica – e.g. Ibn Durayd’s (d. 231/933) al-Ǧamhara – 
one finds authors who revert to the one-third type of arrangement, that is, 
the one that takes only the first radical into consideration. Among the lat-
est examples are Ibn Nūr al-Dīn’s (d. 825/1422) Maṣābīḥ al-maġānī, Ġazzī’s 
(d. 984/1577) Ḏikr aʿḍāʾ al-insān, and Ḫafāǧī’s (d. 1069/1659) Šifāʾ al-ġalīl. To add 
to the chronological overlap, the two-thirds type, i.e. arrangement based on two 
letters or radicals only, extends over several centuries, as exemplified by Kurāʿ 
al-Naml’s (d. 310/922) al-Muǧarrad and Abū Ḥayyān al-Andalusī’s (d. 745/1344) 
Tuḥfat al-arīb. Variety exists even within this type, for whereas in such works 
the first two letters or radicals normally form the basis of arrangement, Abū 
Ḥayyān’s book is based on the first and last. It is noteworthy that Ibn Kamāl 
Bāšā’s (d. 940/1534) argument that, by not going beyond the second radical of 
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 419 -->

409
epilogue
the word, he managed to cut down on the number of sections and chapters 
of his al-Tanbīh ʿalā ġalaṭ al-ǧāhil wa-l-nabīh is not convincing at all since one 
rarely finds in this short monograph words that share their first two radicals, 
and even if these words were to be arranged by taking their third radical into 
consideration, the only effect would be on their order of appearance and not 
on the subdivisions of the book – which are not indicated anyway. Finally on 
periodization, we have argued against the attempt of some to prove that data 
collection, single-topic monographs and formally arranged lexica represent 
three consecutive stages. In fact, the early history of Arabic lexicography and 
its later development do not lend themselves to any meaningful division into 
distinct stages that would enhance our understanding of the field as a whole, 
although such division may be useful in tracing the development of specific 
genres of the mubawwab type.
In order to best understand the Arabic lexicographical tradition, one has to 
place it within the wider context of Islamic scholarship. Its close affinity to the 
study of the Qurʾān, prophetic tradition, poetry, proverbs, etc. has been amply 
demonstrated, as, for example, in the whole genre of ġarīb al-Qurʾān; in the 
debate about whether the Qurʾān contains Arabized words and in the discus-
sion of ištirāk (homonymy) based on Qurʾānic context; and in the use of isnād 
or chain of transmitters in both Ḥadīṯ and linguistic riwāya. At the level of the 
general cultural life of the Arabs, the lexicographical tradition, particularly in 
its earliest stages, preserved much of the Bedouin cultural life which would 
have otherwise been lost to us. Its cultural dimension can also be clearly dem-
onstrated by reference to heated debates on issues such as those stimulated by 
certain views expressed by the Šuʿūbiyyūn. The latter’s claim that aḍdād (words 
with two contradictory meanings) lead to obscurity and confusion, and that 
the Arabs often use proper nouns that have no origin (aṣl) in their language 
and whose meanings may be unknown to them gave rise to interesting debates 
in the genres of aḍdād and ištiqāq (derivation) respectively. Despite the pres-
ervation of the Bedouin culture, the lexicographers – due to their conservative 
approach and rigid view of the criteria of acceptable usage – generally did not 
keep pace with the linguistic and cultural developments that took place fol-
lowing the epochs of reliable usage, and thus largely ignored the new notions 
and technical terms that were created in literary and scientific domains, or 
only included some of them long after they gained currency.
But the discipline that is most inextricably linked to lexicography is gram-
mar. It is remarkable that the grammatical views of Ḫalīl (d. 175/791), who 
laid the foundations of lexicography in the introduction to K. al-ʿAyn, greatly 
influenced his student, Sībawayhi (d. 180/796), author of the most influential 
work in the history of Arabic grammar, al-Kitāb. Moreover, both grammar 
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 420 -->

410
epilogue
and lexicography share the same material that was amassed during the era of 
data collection in the second/eighth and third/ninth centuries. Yet in spite of 
the affinity between the two disciplines, the grammarians almost totally left 
to the lexicographers the distinctive hallmark of their domain, namely, the 
explanation of lexical items. The influence of Sībawayhi in this respect cannot 
be exaggerated enough. Having briefly pointed out in the Risāla of the Kitāb 
certain types of semantic relationships in order to demonstrate the bound-
aries between them and the syntactical and morphological issues which he 
analyzes, Sībawayhi excludes from the rest of the Kitāb the function of explain-
ing the cited lexical items, even those that are extremely rare or unfamiliar. 
Another difference between grammarians and lexicographers, particularly in 
the early period, is the much more frequent use by the latter group of material 
derived from Ḥadīṯ. This can be most visibly demonstrated by comparing the 
seven or eight ḥadīṯs in the Kitāb with the 428 ḥadīṯs and aṯars found in al-ʿAyn. 
Furthermore, the partisan divide between Basrans and Kufans is almost totally 
absent in lexicography, and authors often cited material from both Basran and 
Kufan sources, such as what Qālī did in al-Bāriʿ. In certain areas there exists a 
complementarity between the two disciplines, as in the study of Qurʾānic mate-
rial, for whereas the grammarians mainly analyze the syntactic peculiarities 
of Qurʾānic verses (cf. al-Kitāb and the linguistically oriented exegetical works 
authored by Farrāʾ, Abū ʿUbayda and al-Aḫfaš al-Awsaṭ in the early third/ninth 
century), lexicographical works of the genre ġarīb al-Qurʾān focus primarily on 
the meaning of Qurʾānic lexemes. In certain other areas, however, there seems 
to be a large degree of congruity between the two disciplines. Among the clear-
est examples is that most of the notions and morphological issues discussed 
in works of the genre abniya (patterns) – such as hamzated forms, muḏakkar 
and muʾannaṯ (masculine and feminine), maqṣūr and mamdūd (abbreviated 
and prolonged patterns), and the verbal forms faʿala and afʿala – are derived 
from grammatical sources, particularly al-Kitāb. Similarity between the two 
disciplines is also apparent in that both Ḫalīl and Sībawayhi were keen to 
explore the characteristics of Arabic so as to detect the naḥārīr’s artificially 
created words – dismissed by the former – and the naḥwiyyūn’s neologisms 
– criticized by the latter. From another perspective, the existence, both in the 
introductions of al-ʿAyn and in al-Kitāb, of technical terms that still preserve 
their general, non-technical meanings, as well as Ḫalīl’s method of ḏawāq (i.e. 
sampling; lit. tasting of phonemes) and his experimentation in this respect as 
reported by Sībawayhi, are among the strongest arguments in favor of the view 
that Arabic lexicography is the product of indigenous factors that shaped the 
awareness of the importance of linguistic enquiry, and not simply the result of 
the borrowing of an available system that merely had to be Arabized.
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

<!-- page 421 -->

411
epilogue
The Arabic grammatical tradition certainly produced a theory that had its 
own set of terms, axioms, tools and methods of analysis. But apart from the 
interest shown by some grammarians in demonstrating the interrelatedness 
between lafẓ (form) and maʿnā (meaning) in the constructions under analy-
sis, the grammatical tradition did not develop a semantic theory in any mean-
ingful way. Questions of semantics were more profoundly discussed by the 
balāġiyyūn (rhetoricians) and, at a later stage, in the branch of study known 
as ʿilm al-waḍʿ. In the lexicographical tradition, one certainly cannot talk of a 
theory in the same sense as is done for grammar, given the absence of a set of 
elements that match the above-mentioned ones with respect to grammar. Yet 
there were numerous theoretical issues that had to be resolved in lexicographi-
cal writing, particularly in the muǧannas type. The wide variety of the models 
of arrangement and of scope should not deter one from the realization that, 
as early as the time of Ḫalīl, elements of a lexicographical theory were already 
being contemplated and have left their mark on the tradition as a whole. The 
three principles employed by Ḫalīl in exhausting all possible Arabic roots, 
whether they were actually used or not (i.e. the three closed sets of the num-
ber of the letters of the alphabet, of consonants in words, and of permuta-
tions or taqālīb in roots – particularly in biliterals and triliterals), represent the 
discovery stage at the theoretical level. To be sure, many modifications were 
later introduced to specific aspects of Ḫalīl’s model (e.g. shift to alphabetical 
arrangement and abandonment of the taqālīb in favor of listing each root inde-
pendently of the ones that share its radicals), but no alternative scheme for 
exhausting the roots of the language had to be invented from scratch.
Apart from Ḫalīl’s three principles which resulted in exhausting and arrang-
ing roots, several theoretical issues lay at the heart of the muǧannas lexica 
irrespective of the type of their arrangement. Some of these were discussed 
by lexicographers, typically in the introductions of their lexica. Questions of 
authenticity and correctness of cited lexical items are among those most com-
monly discussed by lexicographers (cf. Azharī’s criticism of earlier authors 
on this count and Ǧawharī’s method of including only those items which he 
deems to be correct). But authors primarily relied on their judgment in such 
matters rather than on clear and established, albeit controversial, criteria such 
as those of grammatical analysis (e.g. qiyās, ʿilla, ʿāmil, aṣl, etc.). Ǧawharī, for 
instance, nowhere explains the criteria for not including in al-Ṣaḥāḥ a large 
number of words, said by Fīrūzābādī to amount to half the language. Similarly, 
expressions of the type lā aḥuqquhu (I cannot confirm its correctness) and 
laysa bi-ṯabt (not certain), recurrently used by Ibn Durayd, among others, do 
not rest on clearly defined principles that determine acceptability or other-
wise. The lack of established criteria in this respect gave rise to conflicting 
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 422 -->

412
epilogue
views of early lexicographers vis-à-vis the authenticity, correctness and mean-
ings of lexical items. Accordingly, it is not uncommon in al-Lisān and other 
lexica which combine several earlier works reporting such disagreements to 
find one lexical item that is accepted by some but not by others or is assigned a 
number of senses, each of which attributed to a specific scholar.
Another issue of a theoretical nature that was mentioned in the introduc-
tions of several lexica is the scope of material to be included. The three closed 
sets of Ḫalīl had resolved the issue of exhausting Arabic roots, but there still 
remained the issue of exhausting either the derivatives of the roots or the 
various meanings of these derivatives. Some authors chose to limit the scope 
of their material by focusing on a specific linguistic aspect, as did Ibn Fāris in 
his approach to uṣūl and Zamaḫšarī in highlighting tropical usage. The amount 
of data cited by both authors was commensurate with what they were primar-
ily interested in demonstrating. But with the expansion of the data available 
in numerous sources, a number of authors of the seventh/thirteenth century 
onward were inclined to compile lexica that would surpass earlier ones in 
comprehensiveness as well as correctness resulting from comparing sources 
and determining the most authentic and reliable meanings. Several of these 
lexica expanded the material of a particular lexicon, such as Ṣaġānī’s ­al-Takmila 
wa-l-ḏayl wa-l-ṣila li-Kitāb Tāǧ al-luġa wa-ṣaḥāḥ al-ʿArabiyya and Zabīdī’s 
al-Takmila wa-l-ḏayl wa-l-ṣila li-mā fāta ṣāḥib al-Qāmūs min al-luġa, both titles 
of which specify their main objective. An alternative method was to combine 
the content of several lexica, as did Ibn Manẓūr with the five sources from which 
he derived the material of al-Lisān, and to a lesser extent Fīrūzābādī, who in 
al-Qāmūs availed himself of numerous sources but primarily brought together 
the material of Ibn Sīda’s al-Muḥkam and Ṣaġānī’s al-ʿUbāb. Moreover, and 
perhaps due to the conviction that lexica are tools of information and should 
not be limited to explaining lexical items, several works, such as al-Lisān and 
al-Tāǧ, have assumed encyclopedic dimensions by including information on 
issues related to grammar, morphology, phonetics, prosody, proverbs, Arabized 
words, etc. as well as place names and proper nouns, and often details on sev-
eral areas of interest, such as plants, animals, metals and natural phenomena.
But in spite of the attempt of some authors at comprehensiveness, they 
inevitably could not exhaust all the lexical items of the massive corpus of 
Arabic. To cite an example related to a relatively early period, about fifty of 
the words used by Ǧāḥiẓ (d. 255/869) – one of the most renowned and highly 
esteemed authors in the literary tradition as a whole – are nowhere to be 
found in the lexica (cf. Sāmarrāʾī [1983: 203]). Absence of lexical items applies 
even to some of the most comprehensive lexica, such as al-Lisān and al-Tāǧ 
(as we know from European supplements; cf. also Ǧabal [1986b] and Ḥassūn 
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 423 -->

413
epilogue
[2008]). Similarly absent from all lexica are specific meanings of certain words 
which they cite. It is quite interesting, for example, that Tamīmī’s (d. 538/1143) 
al-Musalsal fī ġarīb luġat al-ʿArab contains twenty-five words whose specific 
meanings, as explained by the author, nowhere feature in any muǧannas 
lexicon. This significant number of items found in a single book shows that, 
despite their concern for ġarīb, authors of muǧannas lexica left unrecorded a 
large number of words of that type.
That not all of the derivatives of certain roots were listed in some of the 
most exhaustive muǧannas lexica is primarily the result of the absence therein 
of a template that determines the patterns to be included and the order 
followed. Had such a template been adopted, both the verbal and nominal 
patterns would have been more systematically arranged in each root. One rea-
son for the absence of such a template in most lexica is the authors’ inclination 
to start with the most frequent or widely used pattern, irrespective of whether 
it is a verb or a noun, and whether it is the unaugmented ground form of the 
verb or any other augmented verb. Another reason may be that some authors, 
such as Ibn Sīda in al-Muḥkam and Ibn Manẓūr in al-Lisān, try to list together 
within each lemma semantically related items (be they verbs or nouns), sepa-
rately from other items that form a different semantic group – a method that 
unavoidably leads to disrupting the order of listing nouns and verbs as well as 
unaugmented and augmented verbs. As a result of the absence of a clear order 
of arrangement, the user often frustratingly has to read through much of the 
lemma to be able to find the lexical item that he seeks. There are, however, a 
few notable exceptions to this general lack of internal arrangement. In his plan 
for al-Muḥkam, Ibn Sīda stipulates that the unaugmented form of the verb 
should be cited before augmented verbs, but he neither uniformly applies this 
principle nor lists nominal derivatives in any specific order. Ṣaġānī, in al-ʿUbāb, 
is much more systematic in separating the unaugmented verb and the nouns 
that are related to it from the augmented ones and the nouns related to them. 
For his part, Fīrūzābādī organizes his lemmata based on a uniform template 
applied throughout al-Qāmūs, but, in pursuit of brevity, often mentions with 
the unaugmented verb those augmented verbs that are synonymous to it, thus 
effectively upsetting the normal order and consequently making the task of 
the user more complicated.
Although clear and succinct definitions are by no means absent from most 
muǧannas lexica, it is not uncommon to find definitions that lack clarity or 
precision. This occurs, for example, when a lexical item is defined as being the 
contrary of its antonym. One thus finds in the lemma ǦDB in al-Ṣaḥāḥ the 
expression al-ǧadb: naqīḍ al-ḫiṣb (Barrenness is the antonym of abundance of 
herbage) and in the lemma ḪṢB the reverse expression al-ḫiṣb: naqīḍ al-ǧadb. 
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano
