<!-- page 424 -->

414
epilogue
Another source of obscurity is the use of the defined word itself (or one of its 
derivatives) in explaining its meaning, as in Ibn Durayd’s expression al-qatab: 
qatab al-baʿīr (Packsaddle: camel’s packsaddle; al-Ǧamhara I, 255). Other than 
circularity, ambiguity frequently occurs when one lexical item is explained by 
another that may not be familiar to the user (probably even in the author’s 
time). An example from the lemma KDŠ in al-Ṣaḥāḥ is al-kunduš: al-ʿaqʿaq (for 
a species of birds), and another from the lemma ṢʿFR is iṣʿanfarat al-ḥumur: 
ibḏaʿarrat (The wild asses were startled). In other cases, no attempt is made 
at defining the lexical item in question if it is deemed to be well-known and 
is hence merely described as maʿrūf. Among the earliest authors to apply 
this method frequently is Ibn Durayd in al-Ǧamhara, and Fīrūzābādī uses it 
so regularly that he abbreviates maʿrūf by m in his al-Qāmūs. The supporting 
evidence of usage (mainly poetry šawāhid) that is cited by the lexicographers 
normally clarifies the meanings of words that are not sufficiently explained. Yet 
one also finds šawāhid which confirm the use of the lexical items at hand, but 
do not elucidate their meanings. Thus in the above cited word al-kundūš, the 
verse cited by Ǧawharī, munītu bi-zammardatin ka-l-ʿaṣā ⁕ alaṣṣa wa-aḫbaṯa 
min kunduši (I was afflicted with a man-like woman who resembles a rod and 
is more thievish and malicious than a kunduš), is totally useless for the clari-
fication of the meaning of kunduš. Also related to šawāhid is that the lexicog-
raphers themselves often try to deduce meaning from context, as one finds in 
ġarīb material in poetry, Qurʾān and Ḥadīṯ. Accordingly, there may be disagree-
ment among a number of lexicographers as to the exact meaning of a certain 
word because each interprets it differently based on context. Needless to say, 
the strategies used in providing semantic information in Arabic lexica often 
reflect the absence of agreement on the criteria by which elements of a lexical 
definition and the modes of its formulation are identified in a manner that 
guards against ambiguity or guess-work.
The period of data collection chiefly in the second half of the second/eighth 
and the first half of the third/ninth centuries had a great impact on the lexico-
graphical tradition as a whole. The transmission of usage by the Aʿrāb focused 
more on rare, even obsolete, words than on words that were more commonly 
used in spoken or literary language. This is matched in grammar by the con-
siderable attention devoted to irregular patterns and to constructions that do 
not conform to the norm. Lexicographers even recorded obscure words that 
were cited no more than once, as in qiṣāṣāʾ and buḫduq (cf. above, 16). Concern 
for ġarīb and nādir is common to both mubawwab and muǧannas lexica (cf. 
in the former the various ġarīb genres, and in the latter Šaybānī’s K. al-Ǧīm, 
which is almost restricted to ġarīb). In a remarkable statement in the intro-
duction of his monograph Maqāla fī asmāʾ aʿḍa⁠ʾ al-insān, Ibn Fāris expresses 
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 425 -->

415
epilogue
his ­astonishment that some of those who are very well versed in the study of 
strange or unfamiliar usage may not have enough linguistic competence to 
name their own body parts, and if they need to indicate a feeling of pain point 
out those parts by hand rather than utter their names. This shows how little 
the focus on strange and unfamiliar material contributed to a real mastery 
of the language among scholars, let alone ordinary people. Furthermore, the 
predominance of such material in the recorded data and the disproportion-
ate attention it received from philologists was indirectly criticized by a num-
ber of authors who explicitly promised to include in their lexica words said to 
be commonly used in the speech of the Arabs, rather than focus on obscure 
and uncommon words. But whereas some faithfully kept this promise, as did 
Ibn Fāris in al-Muǧmal, others hardly did. In particular, Ibn Durayd, who titled 
his lexicon al-Ǧamhara to refer, as he says, to the ǧumhūr (i.e. bulk or mul-
titude) of the speech of the Arabs, to the exclusion of what is waḥšī (barba-
rous) or mustankar (objectionable), included a large number of words of the 
two latter types to the extent that Azharī doubted the authenticity of some of 
the lexical items cited by him, particularly quadriliterals and quinqueliterals. 
On the whole, concern for strange, rare, or even obsolete words is one of the 
main features of Arabic lexica. But whereas most of these words had been 
documented in the period of data collection of Bedouin usage and poetry 
riwāya and quoted in the earlier lexica – at a time when they were still used 
in certain areas or were still part of recent memory – their inclusion in sub-
sequent works long after the dissipation of the culture to which they had 
belonged demonstrates the schism between those works and actual linguistic 
usage of their times.
The above reference to the persistence of strange, uncommon and obso-
lete words in later lexica unavoidably raises the question of the high degree 
of dependence of most lexicographers on their predecessors for data. Later 
authors understandably had to rely on earlier works for their material, par-
ticularly as the so-called epochs of reliable usage (ʿuṣūr al-iḥtiǧāǧ) were con-
sidered to have come to a close by the end of the fourth/tenth century when 
the Bedouin fuṣaḥāʾ were no longer in existence. Yet reliance on earlier authors 
was not matched, as mentioned earlier, by the inclusion of new material that 
had come into use (and is apprehensively referred to as muwallad), in particu-
lar the new meanings which words assumed as the need arose to express new 
concepts and the neologisms and technical terms that were coined in various 
fields of knowledge or aspects of material culture. Among early muǧannas 
lexica, K. al-ʿAyn includes certain lexical items which Ḫalīl attributes, as 
evidence of actual usage, to speakers of his native Basra, rather than to the 
Bedouins, and other items which he indicates as having acquired a specific 
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 426 -->

416
epilogue
meaning used by a certain group of speakers (cf. above, 297). It is the recording 
of such contemporary usage that one misses in most lexica, especially after 
the fourth/tenth century, with some notable exceptions, such as the medi-
cal terms recorded by Fīrūzābādī in al-Qāmūs and Egyptian vernacular usage 
cited by Zabīdī in al-Tāǧ. The same phenomenon of reliance on earlier authors 
is also evident in several mubawwab genres. To begin with, it is not uncommon 
to find a relatively late author derive much of his material from a much ear-
lier one and fully adopt his method of discussion or argumentation. Ǧawālīqī 
(d. 540/1145), for example, is heavily dependent in Mā ǧāʾa ʿalā faʿaltu wa-afʿaltu 
bi-maʿnā wāḥid on Aṣmaʿī (d. 216/831) and marks with the letter ṣ all items 
derived from him. A more flagrant example is that more than seven centu-
ries after Ibn Ḥabīb’s (d. 245/860) Ḫalq al-insān fī l-luġa, Ġazzī (d. 984/1577) 
authored Ḏikr aʿḍāʾ al-insān by using that book’s material as a core element 
which could be supplemented from other earlier works. On a wider scale, there 
are several genres in mubawwab works in which later authors added precious 
little to much earlier scholarship. In works on muʿarrab (Arabized words), 
one thus finds that the observations of scholars of the formative period in the 
second/eighth and third/ninth centuries and their classification of the types of 
changes Arabized words undergo in the process of their adaptation to Arabic 
patterns were hardly surpassed by later authors in spite of the fact that the latter 
enlarged the repertoire of Arabized words cited by those early authors. Works 
on maqṣūr and mamdūd (abbreviated and prolonged patterns) also evolved 
very little over several centuries, and apart from the introduction of alphabeti-
cal ordering possibly by Ibn Wallād (d. 332/944), the criteria of arrangement 
(e.g. by initial vowels of words) as well as the topics of discussion largely follow 
the example of the earliest authors in the genre, such as Farrāʾ (d. 207/822), Ibn 
al-Sikkīt (d. 244/858), Nifṭawayhi (d. 323/935) and Waššāʾ (d. 325/937). Similarly, 
we demonstrated that the genre of mutarādif (synonyms) hardly developed at 
all since the times of Ibn al-Sikkīt, Hamaḏānī (d. c. 320/932), Ibn al-Marzubān 
al-Bāḥiṯ (d. c. 330/941) and Qudāma b. Ǧaʿfar (d. 337/948).
In tandem with the temporal limits applied to the epochs of reliable usage, 
there were temporal considerations specific to one of the major sources of lin-
guistic data, namely, poetry. Although lexicographers were in general some-
what more tolerant than the grammarians in citing poetry after a relatively 
very early date – generally marked by the death of Ibn Harma in 176/792 – they 
only sporadically cited šawāhid derived from later poets, although the cor-
pus was left open until the end of the fourth/tenth century in the case of the 
speech of the Aʿrāb. These strict temporal limitations had a negative impact on 
the lexicographical tradition as a whole, not only because they set a rigid stan-
dard of faṣāḥa that did not allow for natural linguistic development, but also 
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

---

<!-- page 427 -->

417
epilogue
because they led to the exclusion, as linguistic evidence, of the poetry of those 
who were considered among the greatest poets of the Arabs, some of whom 
like Mutanabbī (d. 354/965) and Maʿarrī (d. 449/1057) were very well versed in 
linguistic sciences. That these two poets, and many others, such as Abū Nuwās 
(d. 198/814), Abū l-ʿAtāhiya (d. 211/826), Abū Tammām (d. 231/846), Ibn al-Rūmī 
(d. 283/896), and Buḥturī (d. 284/898), are scarcely cited by the lexicographers 
(Zamaḫšarī in Asās al-balāġa being a notable exception), contrasts with their 
heavy reliance in their poetry šawāhid on Ǧāhilī and early Islamic poets. This 
discrepancy faithfully reflects the conviction that the standards of faṣāḥa and 
acceptability of usage should be confined to a specific period – one that in fact 
precedes the earliest lexicon – even if it meant the exclusion from the corpus 
of a large body of data (mainly in innovative expressions and new meanings of 
lexical items) and depriving the user from tracing the semantic development 
which lexical items naturally undergo.
The above discussion has pointed out a number of shortcomings that apply 
in varying degrees to most Arabic lexica, particularly of the muǧannas type. 
To these we can add a few more, such as the lack of distinction of the levels of 
usage; the vast expansion of the material due to deriving the data from a host 
of dialects (e.g. laqiya in al-Lisān has thirteen verbal nouns, and makaṯa seven; 
and raġwa has seven variants); the listing of certain words, including muʿarrab 
ones, under presumed roots that may not occur to the user (e.g. ist “anus” and 
miʾzāb “spout” are listed in al-Ṣaḥāḥ under STH and WZB respectively); the 
listing of lexical items in more than one location (as is frequent in Ibn Durayd’s 
al-Ǧamhara and Saraqusṭī’s al-Afʿāl); the lack of separation between the 
literal or veridical (ḥaqīqī) meaning and tropical (maǧāzī) usage; and the abun-
dance of taṣḥīf or error in diacritical points to the extent that authors who had 
criticized their predecessors in this respect were themselves blamed by subse-
quent authors for committing errors of the same nature. Serious as they may 
be, these shortcomings should not deter one from greatly admiring the pains-
taking efforts of the lexicographers in gathering the data, arranging and ana-
lyzing it, and trying to ensure its correctness and authenticity. It is because of 
these efforts for more than ten centuries (i.e. the period covered in this book) 
that Arabic lexicography came to have a crucial role in Islamic scholarship as 
a whole and constituted a legacy that is certainly worthy of broad recognition 
in the history of linguistics.
Ramzi Baalbaki - 978-90-04-27401-3
Downloaded from Brill.com02/08/2023 11:21:33AM
via Universite degli Studi di Milano

al-ʿAṭiyya. Beirut: ʿĀlam al-Kutub, 1989.

2009.
Ibn Kamāl Bāšā, Risāla = Šams al-Dīn Aḥmad b. Sulaymān Ibn Kamāl Bāšā, Risāla fī
